So much so I went back to Saigon Sq as I felt more at ease and happy to pay that bit extra for not being hassled every second. Worth going but be warned!! Have an idea of what you want to buy as it sells everything a tourist needs. Lots of shops selling the same.

Don't venture past the food vendors unless you enjoy being physically grabbed by vendors as you are walking past. Nothing really interesting being sold apart from your usual tourist stuff like T-Shirts etc. Hot and Cramped and the sales technique is really not working. Unless you are really interested in something don't bother stopping to peruse items
Avoid if you can and expect no respect from locals if you do get sucked in. cheap and tawdry. Maybe I didn’t see the best bits?
If you love confined spaces and being pestered non stop this one for you. Was an experience, however it wore thin quick. Found Saigon market close by - much more friendly and spent our money their.
お土産ゾーンに入ると、あちこちからしつこく声を掛けられ、振り切ってもついてくる。そんなに安くもないし、落ち着いてゆっくり見ることも出来ない。暑い、生ものを扱っているお店の傍なので匂いも気になる。 結果、10分もいることが出来ずに出てきました。

Your standard large market with vendors hawking the same wares on every stall. The big difference is people in this complex is very grabby (they grab you to make you go into their stall). Just watch your pockets. A quick walkthrough is enough. Unlikely anything of quality here to pick up. Tees are poorly made (look at the neckline of tees) and bags have low grade zippers. Look but don’t buy.

Marché local pour y trouver aussi bien de l'alimentation que des cadeaux. Seul point positif, les stands de nourriture qui sont pas mal mais les vendeurs sont trop agressifs et à peine arrivé, on vous saute dessus, c'est très désagréable !!! Fermeture à 19h pour laisser place au marché de nuit, très touristique. A voir tout de même sur HCM.
Terrible aged stuff,yikesIt may have been ok 20 years ago as a flea market but now its just a sad and depresseing place of bygone years,sorry
Sehr aktive Verkaufsveranstaltung, man wird aktiv bedrängt Waren zu berühren. Insgesamt kann man, muss man aber nicht...

Ways too busy for a place that people keep pushing and moving all around the place, it's quite annoying for me to go there. Hate people touch me all the time and keep shoving as well as pushing me side to side, back and forth.

If you don’t like hot, sticky, cramped, noisy and smelly conditions then many could say you shouldn’t be visiting Vietnam anyway but today the markets turned on all the above with added extras as a free bonus. Accosted by beggars using children as leverage wearing ice blue coloured contact lenses pretending to be blind - yet running to grandma several minutes later showing off what they had managed to score from elsewhere - the scene was set for a rough ride. I really wish the stall holders would just leave you in peace to look at the wares. Yes they all sell the same stuff and it’s competitive (I was going to say dog eat dog world but that’s another story) but observing other tourists, it’s not hard to see very few people like being hassled in this aggressive manner and just continue to walk away, often leaving without any purchases at all. The small shops in the street may be slightly more expensive- not by a lot - but they leave you alone to look at things and make your own mind up. It’s an experience in itself without any shopping needed. Don’t go when you are tired, grumpy or if you like your personal space.

We went to the market twice - morning and evening. It was packed with people and crazy busy with lots of pushing and shoving. We didn't buy anything from the place because it was confusing with so many varieties! Prices vary a lot so make sure you shop around before you buy
Touristy and busy. Hassled by EVERY stall holder to buy things. Not much of interest except for a few fruit and veg stalls.
一進去就被主動的商家摸來摸去的，很不舒服，裡面的商品同出一徹，也沒有比較便宜，但推薦可以去吃個河粉和冰，應該是不會再去了。
I feel cheated when I knew the price of the shirt I bought in Ben Thành market was extremely cheap. I haggled already at 100,000vd per a shirt. Then outside it, the shirt can go as low as 45,000vd.

Since this market was the closest to my hotel, went there a couple of times to browse and trying to buy something. 1. Looking for a satchel bag (500'000 VND in a shopping all). At one stand, the lady says this one very good, 1'250'000 VND, special promotion for me 900'000 VND. I didn't like the bag nor any of them and politely declined. She got quite aggressive ("What you want?!") and three of them boxed me in, making it difficult to slip out. When i finally managed and walked away, one shouted "200'000!". So, if 1'050'000 less is still ok, what is their margin? 2. Looking for trekking sandals. At one stand the woman says, yes, they have my size, "350'000." Can i see the shoe first? "350'000!" Please show me the show first. They did not have it. Tried a few other stands, they tried to push a significantly smaller size on me. Others were honest and simply said they would not have such shoes.  Bottom line: if one goes there they should be aware of the need to haggle and still being overcharged.

I’m all for embracing a country’s culture but I didn’t find this market very nice at all. From seeing rats and cockroaches running around to witnessing large frogs being pulled from a bag, thrown onto the floor (to stun them) and then to have their legs, and finally head cut off with scissors was too much for me - Maybe I’m just too sensitive as an animal lover.  Also, I found it hard to find anything truly vegetarian friendly. Everything is meat based or, as with many foreign countries, believe fish is ok to include in vegetarian dishes.

I get it, it's a market and it's going to be crowded but the place is filled with locals and tourists and it's hard to get through the narrow rows at times. Also, the sellers can be quite pushy and can even get violent if you don't buy their items.
一定要從對折殺起，不然就是被人宰。東西相對柬埔寨精緻，但是實在不是什麼好貨。除非是真的喜歡要帶走，不然來逛逛就可以了。

We have been to many markets around the world and the Ben Thành markets have the pushiest (read: rudest) hawkers we’ve ever come across. Not to mention the prices...cheap looking knockoffs galore at exorbitant ripoff prices galore. No thanks.

Es ist total stickig, gibt nichts anderes zu kaufen als überall anders auch. Man hat aufgrund der zig Verkäufer keine Möglichkeit sich mal richtig umzuschauen. Weder an den Essens, Klamotten oder souvenierständen. Wir wollten dort essen und sind letztens Endes quasi nur geflohen.
In dieser Halle wird alles auf sehr beengtem Raum angeboten. Man kann nicht zwei Meter gehen ohne angesprochen oder angefasst zu werden. Ich war nicht begeistert, wie oft beschrieben
Pleins de petites boutiques ,mais l’agressivité des vendeuses et les prix multipliés par 5 nous ont décidés de partir et de faire nos achats dans les petites rues adjacentes
客引きがすごく、お姉さんに腕を引っ張られるし、だだをこねられます。 値切るのが面倒なのでゆっくり買い物するには不向きです。ベトナムTシャツはドンコイ通りで私の見た限り最安60kでしたのでそちらで買った方がいいです。ブランドパチモノを買いたい人くらいですかね。

I only saw tourist trash so ran away quickly after being harassed by loads of stall holders. I loved the pictures on the outside showing the 1930 representation of fish and meat - but it seems that today it is a far call from its original roots.  In terms of shopping you can walk around and you will see more interesting things in different shops and stalls and will avoid the hard sell

Although we ended up buying a few things from Ben Thanh market it was an awful experience. We left after around 10 minutes. The women thrust their goods in front of your face and grab your body pulling you into their store. I really did not enjoy being pulled or touched by people who I did not know and did not want to buy their goods. If bartering you will not leave without buying something as the women will grab you as you try to walk away.  We had a iced coffee and a soft drink which were very cheap and it was nice to watch things going on in the market whilst we were there.

We went to the market just to look and maybe eat lunch. As soon as we entered the market, some of the salespeople started to pull me in my arms and beg me to buy something from them because they did not sell anything during the day.  We tried to walk around for a while but the same thing repeated over and over again, incredibly disturbing.  In addition, the prices seemed twice as high as in the regular stores so you have to bargain a lot.
This market provides wide range of varieties and if you can bargain, you can reduce the price 60% lower than the price quoted. Must bargain as the price offer is very steeped.

Big market full of everything, but very very aggressive vendors working their hardest to grab your cash. They charge tourists 5 or 6 times what the items are worth so that you’re still overpaying once you bargain downward a little. I regret the purchases I was pressured into by pushy shopkeepers.

Great place to be! But just dont buy anything here...they are expensive.Walk around the market you will find better products with cheaper price.Be aware they like to cheat on the materials...all happens when they do the packing.Keep ur eye open all the time till u have u item in the hand only than pay.When u busy counting ur money...there's where they change the materials.

A typical asin market . you can't just walk and enjoy your visit. Almost every stand they will offer you something to buy. I understand that they try to sale but if they have the items open i can see it. All of the items are fake.from handbags watches to shampoo.

I was pretty disappointed with this market. To be clear, I usually go to markets for souvenirs and it's one of my favorite things to do. There really wasn't anything good here. Most of it is t-shirt, shoes, cheap knock-off bags, stalls selling crappy Western shampoos. Not really any nice handicrafts or anything. It took about five minutes to take it all in, and then each stall was just a repeat of what you saw five minutes before.  Second, it's really high pressure. People are pushing you to buy things constantly. I know that's just how it is at markets, but this was worse than normal.  I shop constantly and love markets. I was out of there.

Very large market, some what repetitive with lots of similar type stalls - fabric, clothes, watches, bags and fresh food. Be prepared for a strong odour from fresh fruit and meat with little or no refrigeration. Lots of beggars circuit the perimeter of the market. We were approached by a young girl selling marijuana. Be prepared to barter for a reduced price.

Der Markt wird so angepriesen jedoch waren wir völlig überfordert. Die Verkäufer waren dermaßen aufdringlich und unverschämt. Lieber geht man auf den chinesischen Einheimischen Markt. Nicht zu empfehlen
行くだけでいい気が個人的にはしており、結局お土産は別のちゃんとした店で買っても値段は変わらない。  むしろ値切ってねぎって値切り倒して買ったのに、綺麗なお土産屋さんと同じ値段だったりするとどっと疲れるのは私だけなのか？  観光がてらふらーっと行って、 なんか飲んで 帰ってくるので良くないか？と思うのは私だけなのか？

Not the exciting market that we expected. Stall holders harass and grab you constantly especially if you’re not Vietnamese. They’re trying to make money so will happily rip you off if they can. If You’re wanting to be cheap copies of brand name items, go to Saigon Square. (It has air conditioning and the stall holders are much nicer)

If u want to see what it looks like , then check it out. But don’t eat or buy anything here unless u are an expert at knowing what the base prices for in HCMC. The majority of the vendors will rip you off. We got ripped off when we ate there. Ordered one dish but they handed us two and pretended like we ordered two. And wanted to charge us. We refused. We asked why the price is higher than the menu listed. She said she pick the biggest snails for us that’s why price is higher. But all the snails in her stalls were the same size. Bunch of liars! Go to eat at other places. Especially the malls. You get AC and no tricks or ripoffs like these markets. And oh, avoid street vendors too. Like I said, unless you know the base prices already.

Да, здесь есть сувениры. На них я не обращала внимания, так что не могу прокомментировать. Всё остальное, то есть одежда, обувь, сумки... Я не знаю, кому это может понадобиться, потому что любой китайский рынок будет лучше по качеству и разнообразию. Ну а цены, конечно, безумные.

wir sind nur einmal kurz durchgelaufen und haben festgestellt, dass es die gleichen Waren gibt wie überall anders auf den Straßen und auch auf den Tschechenmärkten an der deutschen Grenze. Sind dann nur nochmal zurück um uns ein paar hundert Gramm Trockenfrüchte zu kaufen, die dann aber sehr stark gezuckert waren. Aus unserer Sicht nur eine Touristenfalle.

Feel free to visit and have a look and also get a view of what could be baseline prices. Be very aware of aggressive tactics to get you to spend your money - including Sellers raising their voices and really pressuring you. Also, be careful when parting with a large bill. There is a simple con of handing over say, a 500k note - only for the Seller to claim you only handed a 100k one. One way around this is to state how much you are giving them as you give it to them (before it ends up with their pile of notes). So you can say, "here's 200, give me change for 200". That way, you reduce the chances of being scammed.  We bought a few things here and regretted them when we crossed the road to the Saigon Square Mall only to find that the environment there was much better and that prices were actually better. So, save your money and skip buying anything here - especially clothes - you will regret it when you get to the mall.

Whitnessed a fellow tourist buying some shoes, told him what i paid for mine and the shop owner slapped my arm and growled me. Fair enough in her defence, i took away potential profit, but dont come here expecting to get a great price. They will mostly let you walk away unless your willing to pay half of the starting price (which is usually x4 the actual price). Items are fake and fall apart quickly so buyer beware :).

Get a price and more than half it at Ben Thanh, big rip offs waiting for you, get a price on similar item at least twice guys of put you money in a boat and float down the river, 60,000 to 75,000 for conical hat try the end at the back a lady back of food court large suitcase 700,000 dong will ak 1m800....but pay 700,000
大きな市場で、碁盤の目状に小さなお店がギッシリ並んでいます。 食料品はバラエティありますが、お土産関係は、あまり変わり映えしません。ゆっくり選びたい人は、ドンコイ通りの方が良いかもしれません。
It is a very touristy market, mostly a bit overpriced and every store sells basically the same things

Jammed packed markets with a lot of similar stalls. Haggling is a must so be prepared to be questioned with the smallest of glance. We came from the North of Vietnam first so were spoilt with laid back shop keepers. Plenty of great stalls just outside the markets, some fixed pricing, some negotiable.

Ok for a quick look around and walk through just to have the experience of being there. Most items are overpriced due to the high tourist draw. A lot of the same products throughout the market with pushy sales people. A much better option is Saigon Square a few blocks away. Not our cup of tea.

Shop owners are pretty aggressive. Not much to see if you are looking for a bargain. As with any Asian country make sure you understand the exchange rate on your money otherwise you will get ripped off big time.

Terrible experiance for me and my partner. The worst market for pushy and aggressive shop owners. I have never been pushed and pulled at so much before. My girlfriend was looking at a bag and she then decided that she didn't want the bag so she said no thanks and walked away. The owner of the shop grabbed my girlfriends arm and said repeatedly you say sorry to me you say sorry to me. I show you all these bags and you not thank me for it. I immediatly insisted that we leave before the situation esculated further. Avoid this market, having travelled most of Asia this is the worst experience we have had at a market. I wouldn't bother with it. The produce and goods are very average quality and expensive too.

Food selection was good and reasonably priced. Merchandise was priced 4-5x higher than in Hanoi. Vendors were handsy and grabbed my arm as I walked by their stalls, but were unwilling to negotiate much on prices. Go for the food and experience, but there are better deals to be had elsewhere.

The trick to survived shopping here is to bargain 50% off from any open price. Leave the store if you didnt get the bargain price. Many similiar vendor will want your business as it is very competitive here. As for food just eat at your own risk. Its filthy & unhygenic but this is local Vietnam culture.
東西的價格都非常貴，在地人都不來這買東西，如果很喜歡殺價再來，因為這邊的賣價一定會讓你砍，只是可能殺了一半的價錢還是比其他地方賣的貴

I had Pho and Banh Mi in one of the stalls and ended up in hospital due to food poisoning. Food are not cheap. 80000 for a bowl of Pho, whereas you can get a nice bigger portion at Co Bac for only 40000. If you think about buying souvenirs, please bargain till your last breath. It's a good place to buy coffee powder though.

Much better markets in Asia, prices are not good ones and we were able to find things much cheaper else where, they can be very in your face pushy and not willing to budge on price like elsewhere even though their prices are high. Apparently a lot of pick pockets and scammers around this area.
Once this may have been a market where locals came to get their stuff. Nowadays it looks to be almost completely aimed at tourists. The charm of one had its all but lost.
ベンタイン市場は庶民の市場だろ、観光客はボラれるので言い値で買うのはやめましょう。半額まで交渉。 ここは営業時間後は夜市があるけど品揃えはイマイチだし高い。交渉は結構難航、複数買うことで半額を目安にしないとボラれます？

We were dumbfounded by the way that some of the shop owners or workers accosted us, trying to get us into their shop. We visited twice, thinking that it may have been obvious that we were new to Vietnam, however the second time was even worse. If you stop to look at something, you are set on and basically pushed into the shop. If you touch it, you own it 😩 We appreciate that not all shop owners where like this, however most were. Very disappointing

This has to be one of the worst market experiences I’ve had in my travels so far. You can’t walk more than a couple feet without having someone approach you to buy. While bartering is part of the culture and the fun, prices are hiked as much as 4 times what they are worth. Add to that An unpleasant experience when we were bartering for a pair of shoes (prices higher than the real thing in NZD) and we decided not to buy and and walked away only for the woman to grab my daughters hand and hold her there to try and make me buy. It wasn’t intimidating to us as my daughter(15) is 5’11 a lot taller than the average Vietnamese, but it was an unpleasant and unnecessary gesture which shows the aggression of the sellers in these markets.

Überteuert und hauptsächlich für Touris , muss man nicht haben , nur gefälschte Textilien , Obst und Gemüse welches es auf jedem Markt gibt , warum dieser Markt ein „muss“ in HCM sein soll erschließt sich mir nicht
Good for a look see, tourist trap to say the least. Overpriced items and at times the sellers are aggressive. Nothing special.

Für mich war das kein schöner Ort zum Einkaufen. Man wird natürlich von allen Seiten angequatscht, nicht übermäßig unangenehm, aber dennoch ständig. Davon abgesehen kriegt man aber wahrscheinlich alles, was das Touriherz begehrt.  Für mich war es aber kein schöner Markt.

Plusieurs touristes m'avaient recommandé d'aller voir le marché Ben Thanh.  GRAVE erreur!  Ce n'est que pour les touristes et ce n'est aucunement authentique. Aucun local ne va y faire des courses. Les vendeurs sont agressifs et l'ambiance stressante. Je vous conseille de ne rien acheter, ce ne sont que des babioles en plastique ou des souvenirs peu originaux.
通路を歩くと、何が欲しい、お姉さんこっち来て、ととにかくしつこく付いてくる人達がいて居心地は悪かったです。 買い物もしましたが、かなり高い金額を言ってきて、電卓で必死に値切りましたがかなり大変でした。 正直、もう今度は行きたくないです。

Prices are very over inflated and they are extremely pushy with tourists. The stalls around the parimeter of the market are government run so you can't barter with them as the prices are fixed. You are better off to go to Saigon Square , better prices less pushy. Go up stairs and get even better prices. You can still barter.

It was actually super hot and smoky and dirty inside. As a real Vietnamese, I would say that the market was such a shame. And no real Vietnamese stuff there, there are all Chinese foods with artificial chemicals and was imported illegally to Vietnam. Shame!!!! It is just a symbol for its architecture. But sometimes it worth a visit once. (small tip: don't take a photo in front of it too long because Vietnamese drivers are gonna smash you). But anyway, I'm not an anti-fan of this place. It's still beautiful but you shouldn't eat too much colorful stuff there!! Have a nice trip!!
If you are after a souvenir this is the place to go otherwise I’d avoid it. It’s hot, cramped isles full of a lot of the same stuff and every second person is harassing you to look or buy something.
夏場だったので風が通らず館内全てで蒸し暑さを感じ息苦しさがありました。手芸品の土産物やベトナムコーヒーにサンダルなど同じ様な店が並んでいます。かなりボッタクリ値段で言われるので値段交渉は必須です。

It is a 'must' visit as recommended in all travel guide books but i beg to differ. The moment you step into the market, you will be pestered to buy things you have not even set eyes on. And when you would like to ask for the price, they purposely mark the price high and make you negotiate. However, negotiation doesnt always work and then it turned nasty. My friend was trapped in her tiny store, as the owner blocked the entrance/exit by her body, insisting she pays for a USD15 dress which only looks like it worths USD5.
Vendors are not very pushy here, some will not bother you at all, but the market lacks atmosphere. It’s just full of junk.

The centralized local and all in one shopping makes for the perfect tourist trap. I found it easier haggling over prices at the smaller shops and received a better deal. The only disadvantage this way is you must visit more places.
그냥 동남아나 어디서든 흔히보는 야시장 입니다. 짝퉁 물건이며, 일반 물건도 파는데 딱시 신기한것도 없고, 구매의욕 제로!! 만약 아이들이 있다면 비추! 사람 사이로도 오토바이가 종종 다녀서 위험해 보입니다.

What a joke! I guess I was lucky enough to stay in Tan Bihn sector where everything is cheap regardless if local or tourist so walking in a district 1 market had me calling out the touts on majority of their prices. Even when I lowered to retail price they said no too low. The game is to haggle but why should I. Even their lowest price is over inflated because it's district 1 Central market. I'm not saying don't go, I'm saying sharpen up. Even 50mtrs from the market will see lower prices and you can haggle with ease on the street.

Of our 16 day stay in Vietnam, visiting many cities, this place has left us the worst impression. Politeness is returned with rudeness.  Having already been to many cities and markets in Vietnam, this one is not unlike the others. There is a selection of food (coffee, tea, dried fruits, herbs and spices, dried seafood, snacks and candy, shoes, bags, accessories- mostly counterfeit). The starting price is about 5-10 times what is normal/reasonable. When I tried to offer reasonable prices, the store sellers turn from getting my attention and pleading me to look in their store, to being rude, throwing up their hands, slamming down the goods and telling me “go away!!”. Just as a reference, I asked for an 18 pack of tea bags. I was quoted 180k VND. As comparison, in the (expensive) airport, the same tea was labeled 50k. And in the supermarket (where only tourists shop, again, due to inflated prices), it costs around 35k. After attempting to politely ask and negotiate prices at a few stores, I gave up. But only after purchasing many items from one store and realizing afterwards that I had paid far, far too much! For coffee which I can’t even be certain is authentic. Should’ve purchased from the Trung Nguyen store instead - it was far dearer in this market anyway! While I do enjoy browsing markets- the crowds, heat and smell doesn’t bother me too much- this is one I would avoid. I accept that tourists will always pay more than locals, but these prices are unreasonably inflated. Even if you are able to remain calm and polite, the stall holders can be rude and abrupt at the end of an unsuccessful negotiation. Tip- I ran out of VND and the store holder was able to take AUD and give me change in AUD. She said it’s from her sisters store and that they have 10 in this market. It’s either that, or the many money exchange places around the market. Tip #2- I exchanged some money at a smaller gold shop across the road. There was a convinience store 3 doors down and I was able to buy tea, coffee and snacks at a much better price. Seeing locals making purchases in there is a sign that the prices aren’t ridiculous.

Who doesn’t love going to the markets where you find great gifts at great prizes!!! Don’t be fooled, just because it’s a market doesn’t mean it’s cheaper unless your prepared to bargain to less than half of the price. Before entering the markets, I had already purchased my souvenirs from actual shops that you cannot bargain with. Eg: a magnet from the Post Office cost me 50,000 dong, the same magnet at the markets, they had a price for 80,000 dong. They then give you the spill that they will give you a good price.

Dieser Markt ist lediglich für Touristen ausgelegt. Man kann jedoch kurz mal drüberschlendern. Die Preise sind den Touristen angepasst und es gibt überhaupt nichts authentisches zu erleben. Achtung vor Abzocke und Diebstahl. Es gibt sicherlich schönere Märkte.

Mi spiace ma alla fine è un miscuglio tra un mercato locale è uno per turisti mischiato insieme. Non che non sia importante capire qual’e lo stato sociale ed economico del paese ma questo insieme no fa giustizia della cultura artigianale del Vietnam. Sono rimasto deluso perché questa confusione non aiuta il turista che pure viene a visitarli. Si può fare di meglio sua per i locali ( magari con più igiene e per i turisti.

It has a lot of souvenirs, snacks and clothes for sale but be prepared for some hard bargaining. All the shops sell roughly the same thing. I got an uncomfortable vibe walking inside which in April is very very hot and stuffy. Think twice about entering.

A busy place, apparently entirely for tourists these days. Lots of, for Vietnam, annoyingly pushy sellers. Quite a wide choice, but expensive. Smaller markets we ran across on our walking tour were much more pleasant, and far cheaper too.

I go to Ben Thanh for buying some Ao Dai....Then the seller push me to her shop...then I choose the design for ao dai.... she told me the price is VND 1.350.000 then I said that was to expensive then I said discount she said oke I said VND 500.000 she said no...till the final price is VND 550.000 then I bought it still think that is ok for the price I walk to the stall in a Block with FIXED PRICE then I saw the same Ao dai with the same color and same design only VND 340.000.....when you shopping in the Market be careful .you need to bargain the price...or you can go to the stall with fixed price
Naja also ich finde man muss nicht unbedingt sehen. Es gibt die typischen Souvenir stände und ein stände für Klamotten, Schuhe etc.
Positives  Lots of characters Good location An experience  Negatives  Same old store repeated 50 times ove Hot hot humid Smelly Sellers can get touchy feely
Typicall atrraction for tourist. Price for everuthing are doible or triple and u can find many copy, yes good quality but in other place u pay better price

Bien sur que c'est touristique ! Mais si vous recherchez de l'authenticité et de la culture vietnamienne, ce n'est pas l'endroit idéal. Vous allez etre harcelé par les vendeurs pour vous refourguer leur article à prix d'or... Perso, ce n'est pas ce que je recherche.
Mass produced stuff, typical tourist trap... nothing authentic... Maybe the food would be worth a try.
市場內的紀念品差不多一樣，沒有什麼特色，重複性很高，晚上的夜市也是一樣，每三攤就出現相同的東西，讓人失望。

Big indoor market with all sorts of items. Marked fixed price stalls are situated around the exterior of the building. It's an experience but too expensive even with haggling. Inside the building is hot and humid. Watch what change you are given, I purchased only one item and realised afterwards that I hadn't been given enough change. Better shopping options elsewhere.
Very large indoor market selling fresh food, cooked food, snide designer clothes and other tat. Typical of other Asian indoor markets, very hot and not particularly pleasant!

Go for a look, the stalls are a tourist trap and the vendors are really annoying. The food stalls look great, we were full when we went past and I would go just to eat. The stalls here are 3x the price of everywhere else and they all tell you a bleeding heart story. Just go for the food, if you want to buy stuff ask around your hotel for shopping areas and save yourself some money that you can then spend on food.

We were in HCMC 15 years ago and had good memories of visiting Ben Thanh Market. Not so on visiting a few weeks ago. It is now noisy and crammed full of stands selling the same cheap low quality souvenirs. On our walk there from our hotel we did pass some decent shops and at Ginkgo bought some nice good quality gifts. If you want cheap trinkets to give away Ben Thanh may suit but for something worth keeping try the stores outside.

We went early morning to avoid the crowds for a quiet browse. Being early had a big problem, being bothered by the stall holders. We walked down the first aisle and every stall holder was either shooting as us or grabbing or arms to get us in. This I did not like at all. We turned left, got a drink and walked straight out.

Ansich mag ich solche Märkte. Feilschen, Handeln etc. Was meiner aber nicht geht wenn man von jedem angefasst wird und das ist nicht immer nur sanft. Mag sein dass das Gang und Gebe ist aber in Hanoi oder Da Nang passiert sowas nicht. Man wird auch von jedem angeredet egal wo man steht oder sitzt, das ist aber ein generelles Problem in HCM
観光客が多く訪れる場所のため、こちらが観光客だとわかると高いの値段をふっかけてくるから要注意。最初は言い値の十分の一くらいからディスカウントを始めてみてはいかがでしょうか。
Typical place to buy basic souvenirs. You can find these stores anywhere and don’t need to go here specifically to find it.
Very touristy spot. Its crowded and hot inside. Items sold are priced slightly higher. Would recommend going for a look and buy on one of the outside store instead.

Worth looking at. The stall owners get quite aggressive, pretty much if you are looking at something you are expected to buy it, dont bother asking them how much it is if you don’t want it. Someone got so annoyed at me they actually hit me. I wouldn’t go back again, a few hours was more then enough. It’s extremely hot. Lots of fake brands if you are interested in those things. Recommend for a look but don’t have high expectations.

Pros - Good central location  - Very large and a good place if you are in a rush as each section is quite clear on what it is selling. E.g. clothes, shoes, dried food, beauty products, fresh produce etc.  Cons - Very expensive...high tourist prices. Almost 2 or 3 times what other markets nearby charge and the quality here is worse!  - Vendors are used to the travellers that are happy paying the ridiculous prices. If you want to haggle, they will offer a very small discount but get very angry if you try to go lower (to the actual normal price).  *** Go to Saigon Square, less than 10mins walk from here for MUCH better prices and offers. More locals shop there and will offer you more reasonable prices with a much friendlier attitude. Most vendors are also open to haggling and will try their best to make a sale which you're both happy with.

I find the workers at this market to be rather rude and unhelpful compared to other markets. There is a lot here but most things can be sourced from a supermarket at a better price. There are a lot of shops near by if you walk up the street by the Phuc Long near the front entrance and where you will find great souvineers and better service. Use ben tahn as a jumping ground for better seevice and roducts at better or comparable prices
Et sted med mye å kjøpe men også veldig mye folk og veldig pågående selgere. Vi orket ikke tilslutt p gikk ut.

Wir waren eben dort, wurden von jedem zweiten Verkäufer angefasst und von so gut wie jedem angesprochen. War super anstrengend und unglaublich teuer. Straße nebenan sind die preise für die gleichen Sachen nicht mal halb so teuer.. klassische Touristen Falle^^

I went to the market to get some good bargains but most prices were the same as I’d pay back home in Australia and New Zealand for knock off brands. The shop staff are quite confronting and annoying if your looking to have a browse this is not the place.  Be prepared to barter and never take there first offer because they will say an extremely high amount to trap tourists and see if they can get it.. you have to be as crafty as them with prices.  Overall I think it’s overrated and preferred shopping in Hanoi and brought everything I needed there

Très déçus par ce marché visité à la fin de notre séjour, ce qui nous a permis de faire la comparaison avec d'autres. Vendeurs agressifs et qui vous harcèlent dés que vous passez près de leur échoppe, prix exorbitants pour des babioles "made in China". Sale, à deux reprises des rats énormes sont passés à côté de nous. Bref, attrape touriste à fuir.
If you want to go where all the tourists are sent that's the place. Prices are about double or more than in other parts of the city. Unpleasant, pushy vendors. Motto: Hand over the cash and get out.

I read so many bad reviews. Like sellers are aggressive and they rip you off. Half true. They rip you off. Things are very expensive. I got ripped off. I thought I negotiated but still was way too high. Sellers are super nice though. Not aggressive at all.
When you have never seen a market in Asia it’s maybe ok. But if you have seen some before it’s maximum average. Has nothing from a local market. Better visit Binh Tay market in district 6.

If you've just arrived in Vietnam and the Ben Thanh market is your first market experience, I can happily say it gets better as you got further north. This was our last market stop before leaving Vietnam, and I hated it. It's crowded, really smelly (raw fish and meat greets you as you first walk in) and the stall attendees are pushy and rude.You are pounced upon by multiple stall owners as you walk the aisles, without even stopping to look at anything. "What you looking for Madame, you want to buy a shirt? We have many colours. You want etc etc etc" as they thrust items in your face. It actually puts you off stopping to even look at what the stall is selling. If only they understood that we are more likely to make a purchase if we are actually given time to look at what they have for sale without being hounded.It is relentless, and your polite " No thank you" eventually turns to complete silence or worse. Prices are ridiculous, and they start at over double what you need to pay. But while bartering was fun and playful in places like Hoi An, these vendors are just rude and get really aggressive if you choose not to accept their price, or just change your mind completely. My only reason for returning to this market a second time, was that it was THE only market I had seen anywhere that sold NBA jerseys and EPL shirts. I had to find out team/player names my son wanted and return to face the onslaught. On a plus side, the Ben Thanh Street Food market close by was great. Big choice of food in one place and prices were fairly good. In general, I did not enjoy HCMC (Saigon) at all. Prices are over inflated, tips are expected more frequently, and many places add on VAT 10% which we did not find further north.

We had lunch first in the food market next door. It was ok, not brilliant and very hot. The market itself is like many others except I've never before experienced such aggressive sales people. We were looking at buying a suitcase and politely asked the price in one store. We had heard you needed to haggle - noone seemed up for that by the way - but decided to check a few more out before buying. When we said we'd have a look round and come back the woman went nuts and started angrily shouting at us.... She wasn't the only aggressive person we encountered... When we discovered our hotel was by the Lucky Plaza we bought all our 'tat' in there as it was a much more relaxed experience and they had air con!

Badly organised and overpriced. Be aware of the vendors just outside the market that will create prices based on the colour of your skin! Vendors don't like to haggle (in Thailand they do). This country is not ready for tourism... I cannot recommend Saigon (Ho Chi Minh) or the South of Vietnam altogether.

Le vrai bazard. Énormément de monde. Vous ne faite pas un pas sans être interpellé. Très soûlant. Vêtement et autre qualité très très moyenne. Sans compter les marques de contre façon. Y aller une fois OK. Après il y a bcp mieux à faire sur HCMC

You walk in and it is nonstop harassment? Not enough room to get around. I wanted to try some food but all the restaurants at the front were more expensive than across the street. No Value. You can experience Ho Chi Minh elsewhere.

強引な客引きがあるのは聞いておりましたが、本当にひつこく買い物する気分が失せました。女性なのもあるのか、「いくらー？」と言いながら手を掴かまれ、、立ち去ろうとしても強く引っ張り離してくれない！ とても怖い思いをしました。ベンタイン市場で売られてるものは、他のマーケットでも手に入るので、わざわざここで買う必要はないと思います。市場の雰囲気を楽しむなら言って見ても良いと思いますが、日本人の方への客引きは特に強引なので気をつけてください。

Försäljare som rycker i en så fort man går förbi. Varmt och trångt. Luktar illa. Råttor sprang omkring i matavdelningen. Stor prutmån om du nu vill köpa något. Hörde ett par som prutade ner en artikel med 75%, så pruta på.

The building is beautiful but that is about it. You probably have 4 types of stores here: clothes (cheap touristy stuff, nothing original), accessories (meh), household things (cosmetics, hygiene products, ...) and food. The wet market is probably the best part but you don't need to come here to buy good produce and certainly not if you want a good price. Still, if you like touristy stuff and haggling, dive right into it.

Een markt die gebruikt wordt door locals maar ook door grote groepen toeristen. Vooral waar de t-shirts te koop zijn, zijn de prijzen aan de hoge kant. Het eet gedeelte van de markt is wel aardig en je kan er goedkoop Vietnamees eten. Leuk om te zien maar geen must see vind ik.

Tłok, upał.... jeśli chcesz zobaczyć cały ten targ to potrzebujesz minimum kilku godzin. Jeśli zdecydujesz się na zakup i po trudnych negocjacjach zjechałeś do 30%ceny wyjściowej to możesz czuć się zwycięzca ... ale tylko przez krótka chwilę bo okazuje się ze w innych miejscach ceny wyjściowe będą niższe od tych wynegocjowanych. Nocny targ ma zewnątrz Ben Than jest dużo tańszy, choć miejscowi przewodnicy twierdzą coś zupełnie innego, ciekawe dlaczego?

This is a tourist trap of note. It’s hot and full to capacity with tourists and hagglers. You get harassed from the time you enter to the time you leave. The food is poor quality and expensive compared to what else is available. I was highly disappointed.
Total unspektakulär und wie überall in Asien zu finden.. kennen wir genau SO aus Bangkok, Yangon und anderen asiatischen Großstädten. Macht nicht so wirklich Spaß.
Dit vlug gepasseerd want op en top toeristisch is niet de werkelijke locale markt, maar een val om toeristen te doen kopen aan veel te hoge prijzen

In Vietnam gibt es weitaus schönere und günstigere Einkaufsgelegenheiten. Der Charme hält sich in Grenzen. Wer auf den letzten Drücker Souvenirs sucht oder auf Plagiate steht, dem wird hier sicherlich geholfen.  Wir sind trotzdem mal durch spaziert. Das reichte auch.

We went very early on the morning, with a tour. I am not sure why they took us. The place is very well organized. They had all the vendors that sold the same product together. It was 2 floors. Maybe we were to early but the vendors would not sell anything to us. I believe it was a market place for stores to find vendor to buy from.

This market is just a tourist trap. The stuff is so over price it not even worth haggling. If you do go, go to the outside area of the market as it has a fixed price area and the price is reasonable. They told my boyfriend 450k for a t shirt and went we went to haggle they stuck to 350k if you go to Saigon Square they will only quote you 200k for the same item and you can still haggle some off that. A Vietnamese guide we spoke to said Saigon Square is where the locals go, so it’s got better deals.
우리나라 동대문시장이나 남대문시장을 보는 느낌입니다. 짝퉁시장이구요. 통로가 매우 좁아요. 굳이 방문 해볼 정도는 아니에요

It’s like every other market, hot stuffy with people yelling at you to buy stuff. Would be good if you were specifically looking to buy cheap and meaningless gifts. Otherwise I’d say stay away, save your money for some of the nicer places in Vietnam.

I was with my family just to see around whether there are good things, there were many things we can buy but the price extraimaly high, recommemded to burgain if you are really like to buy. The price can be drop more than 50%. Many good street food though. It was fun but there was small incident which really made us bad impression of the seller, they are some times too rough. Just be alert and be good negotiator!

Very Hard to walk around, it was so crowded and small aisles. The sales persons were really stubborn and not friendly at all.  It was better at the evening though than in the Day time.  I would recommend to go to the market Saigon Square instead.

A very crowded and noisy place (this is the only one positive aspect). Maybe in the past there was a local market but now this place is kept only for tourists. It is very difficult to move around as all sellers are intrusive and try to grab you and sell you all the things you do not need. In vast majority of shops prices are fixed and can not be negotiated (information on this hangs above your head). Food - overpriced and low quality, far away from a real good Vietnamese street food. We tried food on stand with clams, snails and oysters - we do not recommend (very high prices, small portions).

Actually, everywhere in Vietnam will be the same. When come to the items which price are not labelled, and you start to ask "how much?", the dealer will raise the price to unreasonable high price. If you really think of purchasing here, please bargain and cut it at least 80%. Even the food here are for tourist, overcharged and not so tasty. Suggest to get a local friend to show u around and help you to bargain.

This is just a mixed bag of hundreds of stalls. Usual tatty tourist orientated souvenirs and trust me, as you move through parts of this market, you will see every type of that for the tourist trade.(guess they use the same wholesalers). There are clothes( again usual rip off of some well known brands.) The food section does at least give you something to eat to try local street style food. And this is all in the inner part of this market.( bartering is advisable). There is also an outer area around the outer walls.. This part does have signs saying 'fixed price.." So perhaps they pay more for their pitches... Overall, it is what it is.. A tourist trap , with local canteen street food available.. Not a MUST, but if you have time too kill before you leave the city and need too do something, then it will be ok...

Place is very dirty n in reality a huge death trap if ever a FIRE started in there as there is no FIRE sprinkler protection at all in a place full of highly flammable materials!! Far too much aggressive shirt pulling sales attendees there also but safer than getting run over by a beep-beep motorbike that they stupidly n foolishly still let run at speed through the night markets they have!! So any tourists who go there be very wary of everything n everyone in there as it's a pickpockets paradise both night n day if you don't get run over or clipped by a speeding bike as we seen 3 tourists injured in less than 2hr's there!! Much better markets in District 10 n half the inflated price as it's all rigged to take Forengers for as much as they can...BEWARE!!

Dieser Markt ist voll mit Touris und die Preise werden immer in Dollars angesagt und zu dem sehr Teuer alles. Wenn man gute Qualität kaufen will , geht man besser in das Einheimische Einkaufszentrum Saigon Squer 1gleich um die Ecke viel billig und viel mehr Auswahl. Auch in den Läden um den Markt gibt es viel Billiger Ware, als auf dem Markt.

What was once a great market filled with traditional, authentic and quality products used by locals has turned into a pure tourist trap.  Locals will tell you that there are two Ben Thanh Markets. One outer and one inner. These refer to the market inside the building complex that operates during the day (inner) and the stalls spread out on the streets outside this same building at night (outer).  Regardless of which, all vendors will slap you with prices that are x2 to x4 of what you'll pay elsewhere. Outrageous as it may be, these aren't tourist prices as locals would also be charged similar prices. Vendors don't worry about being unable to make sale with the countless tourist so prices are freely pushed up, and some may not even bother with hagglers. Naturally, no locals visit this market for purchases and the driving force are purely tourist.  Food and drinks in the area are also 'unreasonably' up-scaled while being sub-par in taste. Take the sugar cane drink as an example. Anywhere else you'll pay 5,000-7,000VND, but here they are 20,000VND. A price a local elsewhere told me if she were to charge her customers she'd have no customers at all. What you're paying for are food and drinks from an attraction which taste bland and stale. Definitely not worth it. Better options exists just a few streets down that are better priced, taste better and are also much cheaper.  A big market worth visiting if you're nearby and want to see what a market is like in asia. However, its not worth making a trip out of your way to see, nor is it a good idea to buy anything here. See what you like, take some photos and find them elsewhere.

有芒果干，还有海鲜等，服装，工艺品，旅游纪念品之类的都很多，但是里面道路狭窄，秩序非常的乱。货品开价也很高，我想买一把折扇，开价要60块人民币，然后我马上跑了，他就给我打折打到6块。 特别要注意的是在滨河市场的门口就有黑粗黑出租，我们就打到了黑出租，然后从滨河市场达到我们的小酒店，本来是30K的，然后他竟然要了140K，然后我给了他500K的时候，他还把我的500K给抽走了，然后说我没有给钱给他，最后后来我又给了他140K的车费，简直是超级大骗子。在滨河市场，还有各大旅游点，一定要注意黑出租。只能坐Vinasun, Minh的两个公司标志的车子。不是看见白色的士就上。

Go to this place only if you’re strong enough to walk away from the vendors. I only asked the price to compare it from hoi an, but then the 2 women ended up cornering me inside the store when i try to walk away cause my grab diver came already. Just so i can go, i ended up paying for it tho i did bargain as hard as i can.

The place really had so many collections tho so if you’re a shopper and good at bargaining then i think you'll have fun here. Quality of the products are so-so but if you’re paying less it should be fine.

The market has a wide variety of items and plenty to explore.

Bargaining is a must—prices are often more than double. Be careful with some vendors, as overcharging or extra fees can happen.

Honestly overrated. We really wanted to shop and enjoy our time in the market, but the foul odor (thick stench in most of the market) and persistent vendors drove us crazy. The surrounding streets were more pleasant to shop.

My experience wasn’t great here, maybe because I have already been to other markets around Vietnam. This particular market is way overpriced even after negotiating and dropping the price ~40% I feel like I was ripped off. Also some workers are extremely salesy, pulling you by your hand to their shop and pressuring you to buy stuff. When refused they become very moody. You can visit it for experience but I don’t recommend showing any interest in buying things. Saigon Square which is nearby is a bit better option.

A famous market, spoilt completely by arrogant and rude shopkeepers. If you're a pro at bargaining, go for it. Everything is overpriced at first until you bargain. They pass rude comments at you that makes you drop the idea of shopping souvenirs and other items. Very few shopkeepers take out the time to show you their products, while others are okay to let go and are least interested to sell. Unfortunately this was my experience.

I did buy some home decor items for a good price as some sellers were kind.

The market has food, clothes, jewelry, electronics and everything you could possibly want. The people are very aggressive and will touch you, put things in your hands, stand in front of you, etc. It's hard to walk around and look because they won't leave you alone.

They have bathrooms for 5,000 dong.

If you are interested in culture and local, this is the place but honestly, there is nothing to see/do. The local was aggressive asking us to dine in or buy stuff. To the point, i wanted to leave the market immediately. Anw, it was ok to walk thru and see but not to stay for longer

I loved the food at Be Than Market, but I was disappointed with how I was treated by one of the souvenir vendors. She became upset when I didn’t purchase an overpriced lighter, even though I was only asking for the price. She could have simply displayed the price so customers wouldn’t need to ask.

Bought fruits from the local granny.. saw the fruits displayed on shelf were huge and juicy… but the box we bought were full of leaves and stems.. and at the bottom were smaller ones… dishonest and shams.. skip fruit stalls to save your money

Ben Thanh Market Not gonna lie… the sellers here were super aggressive. Me and my friends were just walking through, and one seller even tapped my shoulder and pressured us to stop and buy — with an angry face somemore 😭
We got scared and left immediately.
Personally… not recommended.

I agreed with the other reviewer.  Some of the vendors are too pushy and the food court stalls are very grappling, which made me very uncomfortable.  Especially, the stall that sells many kind of shellfish and snails.  She’s also the only stall sells baluts (Hột Vịt Lộn ) in the food court.  She’s meant and will scam you if you don’t know how to speak Vietnamese or don’t know how to bargain.  Stay away from this stall.   I forgot to take down the name and picture because I just wanted to get away from her.  Do not buy from her.
Did my custom blazer here - but the person missunderstood 3rd quarter with short length sleeves. Otherwise it would be a perfect experience in Ben Thanh

This is an amazing place to shop with a wide variety of items and a lively atmosphere. However, if you’re a foreigner, be cautious — prices can be quoted two to three times higher than usual. Bargaining is a must, so don’t hesitate to negotiate. Also, make sure to check the product thoroughly before purchasing, as returns or exchanges might not be possible.

One thing to watch out for — some of the shop aunties can be quite rude. If you’re just browsing and don’t buy anything, they might give you angry looks or even say something rude directly to your face. So be prepared and don’t take it personally.

Most vendors accept cash only, so carry enough with you. On the bright side, you can enjoy tasting some delicious local food while shopping, which definitely makes the visit worthwhile!

⭐ Rating: 2/5
Not the market experience we were hoping for…

I was really looking forward to exploring Ben Thanh Market — it’s such an iconic spot in Saigon and I was excited to walk around, try some local treats, and maybe pick up a few souvenirs. Unfortunately, the experience turned out to be quite overwhelming in all the wrong ways.

From the moment we entered, we were approached non-stop by vendors who were very pushy — not just encouraging us to buy, but demanding tips even before we had a chance to consider purchasing anything. It felt less like a cultural market and more like a high-pressure sales zone. The energy was intense and honestly quite uncomfortable.

It was a hot day, so we decided to sit and take a breather. I had a beer 🍺 and my friend ordered a fruit relish 🍉 — hoping we could relax and regroup. But even while sitting, the pressure continued. It felt like everyone was so desperate for money that we couldn’t truly enjoy the market atmosphere.

I understand that tourism is a livelihood for many here, and I absolutely respect that. But the aggressive approach made it really difficult for us to engage or support the local vendors in a meaningful way. We ended up leaving far earlier than we planned.

It’s a visually interesting place with a lot of potential — but for those looking for a relaxed or authentic shopping experience, just be prepared for the intensity.

Remember to follow 💕 Fauzia for more authentic and I’m awesome reviews doesn’t matter where we are we will give you the real meal deal every single time follow us on a blog that you can click on the link on our bio and you can follow us on Instagram Facebook YouTube and of course TikTok !’

Very crowded place and the sellers are relentless. You have to be able to haggle, as the prices they first give you are outrageous (I thought she agreed on a price in dong but our seller wanted it in dollars). Not for the weak of heart.

It's definitely a cool experience, and since I'm used to markets like this in my home country, it wasn't too overwhelming. What bothers me are the outrageous prices and rude sellers. I bought a small item for 20,000. At another stall, the seller wanted 80,000. When I said that I had bought it elsewhere for 20, he immediately dropped the price to that. 1/4 of the actual price. In addition, you are constantly being approached and literally pressured to buy something. But the worst was the saleswoman at these BTD stalls. We bought something the night before and realised at the hotel that the size was wrong. In the morning, we wanted to exchange it for the right size, but the new saleswoman refused at first. She wanted us to buy something from her first before she would give us the right size. After a long back and forth, we finally got the almost right items. The saleswoman was very annoyed, told us how angry she was and then eventually stopped talking to us.
I would definitely recommend the New Market under the park. Much quieter, better prices and friendly salespeople.

Ben Thanh Market is known as the most famous marketplace in Vietnam, but honestly, I don’t understand why. We didn’t find anything unique here – whatever you see inside can easily be found in shops outside the market as well.

We were quite disappointed with the visit. The shopkeepers were very rude, which made the experience even worse. We went expecting a lively night market, but most shops started closing after 8 PM, and by 9 PM, more than half were already shut.

However, after all this, I recommend sitting outside the market along the roadside. There’s a small area where many people sit and relax. It felt so much better than going inside – a very pleasant experience, especially with the cool breeze at night. You can rest there, grab some ice cream or street food, and simply enjoy the city views while chatting peacefully.

As usual, this place caters to foreign tourists. No air conditioning, poor ventilation, and a noisy crowd. I used AI to search for a local "com tam," a Vietnamese-style semi-optional buffet, and one of the options I was given was this place. The stall number given didn't exist, so asking again was useless. Maybe I should try the other options the AI gave me. It's not that the stalls here are necessarily bad, but it's hard to find a truly good one. The dining environment was even worse: hot, crowded, noisy, and smelly.

If you’re willing to pay inflated prices for knockoff items (including fruits), this is the place to visit. You might think you’re getting a good bargain, but once you explore other districts outside the city centre, you may feel disappointed.

We came back to the apartment and found that some of the fruits were rotten too. GG..
Outrageously priced. Shopkeepers are not nice. You can get better deal outside the market.

Really hot and tourist traps everywhere. The prices even after haggling aren't that much more expensive than buying in a store elsewhere. With clothes, keep in mind that you can buy even cheaper clothes on sale at uniqlo and h&m at a much higher quality. I was quoted 400-700k vnd for most clothes. They wouldn't let me haggle more than 100k off. Not worth. Good to experience but not to buy.

This is a big market with so many sourviniers items as well as foods. It was very crowded. Be careful with the price. I got cheated here. Hand-made fridge magnet costs VD50 each but only VD20 at the roadside.

What a mess of a place.
All the stalls start incredibly high and many of the products are sub par compared to other markets close by.
Only thing I purchased was a large 28inch iFLY wheeled luggage. The guy started at 3 million. I laughed and offered 600k. He settled for 700k
Haggle aggressively or get ripped off aggressively.
A much better market with more space and much lower starting prices is Central Market just down the road.
Come here if you need souvineers or fake goods and aren't going to Hanoi or other parts of vietnam. It's super hot and stuffy and super narrow in there!
Got food poisoning from some vermicelli soup. Good intro to bargaining but more expensive than smaller street vendors due to high tourist traffic.

Not a pleasant experience at all. I was actually looking for souvenirs to bring back home. The pushing nature of sellers made me not want to look at anything! And the food is terrible, definitely don’t eat there.
The only positive part of the market is the historical significance of the building.
Very crowded place and people are not so friendly, they don’t let you check out the items and want you to buy the item. But you have almost everything available there…

If you like to go to a market where it is hot, humid and stuffy, this is definitely a place for you to visit..

In addition to all the goodness that comes with the unbearable air quality, you will also experience rude vendors selling fake merchandise and charging tourist prices for goods that you can buy for less than half in places like Da Nang..

A better alternative is closeby called Saigon Square which is slightly cheaper and has air conditioning in some parts…

Visited Bến Thành Market and found it quite underwhelming. It’s very crowded, and most items are priced much higher than their actual value. The experience largely revolves around aggressive selling and haggling, so unless you’re comfortable negotiating and know the correct price ranges, it’s easy to overpay. Not something I would recommend for a relaxed shopping experience.

As anything in District 1, Ben Thanh Market is convenient and a touristy mess: within walkable distance if you’re staying in the area and makes sense visiting on a very short stay. If you’re in it for shopping and have
time to cover more ground, Chinatown / Cho Lon offer better deals and experience overall.

Visit if you are one who can't afford genuine branded stuff but want to show off wearing fake stuff!
You don't know if the price is right. Too much bargaining.
Probably the Indian tourists have already spread their 'reputation' so be prepared for a very rude response ⚠️ from the vendors, they will first show you the price and then the stuff!

This was overhyped

Very big and lots of people. The food was great; sir at the local stalls and made friends immediately!

But the rest is bog standard Markets with tons of stuff you don't want being poked from all directions.

Keep valuables safe.

Not trying to be negative, it is a must, but still just a bunch of closely combined pop up stalls shouting at you.

Hi Chi Minh’s famous day and night market selling souvenirs, clothes, bags, shoes, and many other household items. There many local food and fruit stalls too. Shopper beware - Prices are very inflated, so be sure to bargain and start with half of the price quoted. Some sellers become aggressive and edgy, and what I don’t like is they touched my arm to get my attention. Not a pleasant experience. So just walk away when that happens. Otherwise this is a must visit for first timers to HCMC.

So very disappointed with this place. This place will rip you off. As long as they know you are a tourist they will charge thrice as much. There's the same ref magnet that is being sold in the airport and the price in the airport is cheaper. Lol.

We found a place and they're selling cheap ref magnet (Like 40K VND compare to other stores 150K-200K VND per piece) for the same designs. But the sales lady was very very very pushy. And even got disappointed when we asked for our change.

The same item down each row.

If you are looking for coffee or fresh seafood this is the place otherwise there's plenty of stores outside the market selling the same stuff.

This is an experience! It certainly has all the things you can possibly want, and has great vibes if you're after the experience of bargaining, haggling and chaos. You can buy everything in the market in other places though which may make for an easier experience

Had high expectations, but Ben Thanh Market was disappointing. It’s extremely crowded and chaotic, vendors are very pushy, and prices are heavily inflated unless you bargain hard. Product quality felt hit or miss. Overall, it felt more like a tourist trap than an authentic shopping experience

Tourist trap. Stall owners there keeps sticking to the tourists and ask them to patronise their stalls. But there are still some hidden gems here. Food wise, the che, seafood noodle, and the bowl of dough are very good, especially the dough.

A tip for all: if the food is good, they won’t need to ask you to buy their food. Go to the stalls where the owners are too busy or don’t bother to buy their food/stuffs.

When compared to Saigon square, things are of low quality but less expensive. Prices are crazy high when compared to other places in Vietnam. And that is even after hard bargaining. Attitude of the shop keepers towards foreigners is unpleasant. If you don't buy after seeing the clothes, they make fun of you and tell something in their language. Only positive is that this market is large and there's a huge variety of goods, basically it's a one stop shop all. Very convenient.

Its a local market extremely congested and none of the items have price tag on it. Its on you how much you can bargain, which is why you never feel relaxed in your mind, you always feel you have overpaid for things you bought. Better option is to go to post office souvenir shops, fixed price, good options you find and peace of mind as well.

Not for me , so many people tryna force u to buy stuff , they literally restrain u ,NO JOKE ,but in saying this there's alotta cool stuff in there , u just gotta be strong with your no's and don't pay the first price haggle hardcore

This place is a tourist trap. I'm a Vietnamese first time visiting and believe me, the price for the stuff inside is 2 or 3 times higher than how much they should cost. It's not authentic Vietnamese culture or anything. The goods there are mostly counterfeit and have little value. If you want to learn more about Vietnam, go to the countryside.

My experience:
The food stall shopkeepers in the market are not very friendly and they tried to talk you into buying their stuff. I said  "no thanks I just wanna see around" but they shamed/ roasted me for not buying. I didn't care though. If you just only want to go to see the old market building and the stalls there, beware of emotional damage and don't make eye contacts.

I'll never coming back that hell hole of a market.

This is a large market selling mainly clothing and food. But lots of stores sell the same products. Be very careful not to get scammed - prices vary greatly from stores selling the exact same products. Example, initially offered Vnd2.8M for a luggage, final offer Vnd800K. I walked away and bought from another store for 550K. Another negative experience is some store owners keep pressuring you to buy or offer counter  price. One store had a 2nd person blocking my path when trying to leave. Alternative is Saigon Square nearby which is slightly better.
Toerist prices, scam taxi drivers with super charged meters. In the end he asked 350.000 dong for a trip what would be 25.000 dong max. Watch out!!!!!
Can't buy anything, seller's annoying, push prices very high. Even if you bargain 50% its also expensive. I was in Danang before and there much better atmosphere is markets.

Very pushy people only after your money not many items are marked with price tags so they start with very high prices and when you say no they give you another price if you still say no and start to walk away then they start swaring at you . Very Rude people and if you stop at the bars or restaurant around the market be prepared to be approached by atlease 5 beggers while your trying to relax drinking your beer.
Good luck

With a large collection of counterfeit goods purveyors, this is the go-to place for shoppers wanting to fake it till you make it. For me, I’d rather wear an original Casio watch than a fake Rolex. Seasoned travellers should give this place a pass as you can get better quality and prices of such goods in Thailand. All prices quoted to tourists are always double what they would sell to locals, start your bargaining by cutting the prices by 60%

This is the largest market in Saigon. It has everything you could possible want. However it is hard to walk through as you are constantly accosted to buy things, when you say ‘No thank you’ the market sellers can on occasion be impolite. They charge tourist prices so be prepared to haggle. My advice is don’t buy from the first stall you see selling what you would like. Try a few to get the best price.

It is very crowded and can get quite hot inside. I wouldn’t recommend going on a hot day.

If your looking for a market with western sizes, and less pestering I would recommend the Russian Market. I found that one very good!

What more do I need to describe this market and it’s environments. It’s a tourist trap with full of useless memorabilia and knock off merchandises. It’s wall-to-wall of vendors trying to make a buck. So, buyers be aware… if you are browsing and windows showing, then you are in a for a real treat. Otherwise, don’t waste your time… and move along.

It is a paradise for those who are looking for fake branded products. But there is zero availability of bags, shoes, belts, caps without brand name on it.
Outside of the market is very dirty

Store owners and sells people are very pushy and put a lot of pressure in buying things. They physically push and grab you to get your attention. It does not feel safe as a tourist because they get really close to your personal space and won't let you go. In addition, they are very rude if you say no or reject an offer.

Some of the food stalls are not that good. Definitely need a ton of research before finding somewhere to eat in there.

Overall, the price and the environment is not worth the hassle to come here. Saigon Square is about a block away and it is ACed with nicer people. The price is the same as Ben Thanh, which makes Ben Thanh over-rated.

Very crowded market with lots of stalls and products, but the aisles are extremely narrow, making it hard to move around. Some vendors are quite pushy and aggressive, which makes the whole experience feel stressful and unpleasant rather than enjoyable.... annoying

Proceed with caution. Decent place for some fruit or items like that, but don’t buy any electronics and inspect clothing carefully before buying. We bought some headphones and a charger, not expecting supreme quality but at least expected they’d work. When we tried them out at home, neither worked. I bought a top that after I took it out of the plastic packaging was already ripping at the seams. Plus they will try and scam and overcharge ridiculous amounts for knock off items.

I got scammed within minutes of walking in the entrance. I successfully ignored the first couple of guys calling out to me, however, I got hooked in by a domineering woman, acting as some kind of scout, standing in my path wanting to know what I was looking for. I made the mistake of giving her my attention and got led across half the market. I ended up paying an exorbitant amount of dong for a couple items of clothing and to get rid of her. She was super pushy and aggressive in her tactics not allowing to think clearly and forcing items on me. I went into it knowing that haggling was essential, however I wasn’t prepared for how futile it is when you’re up against a brick wall bent on scamming tourists, coming down in price in such minuscule increments saying it’s a special deal to make me happy. I tried to make a run for it when she was leading me to make a card payment, I didn’t have enough cash for the several million dong she wanted, she chased me out on the street and grabbed my arm and pleaded, so, I paid just over 1 million dong cash, for 2 items of clothing, and to get rid of her. I was just a solo tourist still grasping the value of the currency. I do not recommend going there. The next day I went to Saigon square and had a much better time.

Well, after reading mixed reviews before leaving AUS, this most defiantly not the cheapest or welcoming market in the area, prices highly inflated, sellers rude (helped that i understood some of their lingo) ad swore if you didn’t buy anything and completely ignored us at times when negotiating, at one point one of the sellers grabbed my wife to get a sale, my wife was accomodating in the beginin, however when she said no, she was blocked in by two sellers, my wife being fairly tall and not one to be pinned in a corner, gave them a bit of curry and they backed off, again the expletives were unacceptable. The clothing here is rubbish. The other markets were Saigon Square, Takka Mall and the Russian Market, These three were not as in your face, however you need to visit Saigon Square early, the second toime we went there was in the afternoon and it was a little feral. Takka Mall is good and some fixed prices, you can negotiate (a lot cheaper than Ben Than and Russian Market), this was probably my favourite, where my wife’s had some good bargains at Saigon Square, the Russian Market was also good, no one in your face and really a lot of winter cloths and sports gear (my wife picked up a great leather bag here, there is only two shops on the second floor for bags), basically we purchased sports tees and collared shirts that are dri fit for around eight dollars AU, track pants for ten dollars, sketchers we bought, sandles ten dollars, thongs eight dollars and a pair of slip ons twenty dollars, 43 litre north face back packs two for fifteen dollars and another bigger one for ten dollars, my wife bought several leather hand bags (not sure on the price) These items were purchased at Takka Mall, Russian Market and Bags at Saigon square including the wife’s leather bags. Also bought a few dress shorts outside of Ben Than market on the street and purchased these for around twelve dollars for two pair. I guess the key is to do your readership and check the stitching and zips, yep they are knock offs however for what we paid, you can’t complain (sorry bought a north face jacket at Takka mall for twelve dollars AU), Again keep away from Ben Than and do your research before visiting. Just my opinion.

First thing I'd say is that if you are also planning a visit to Hoi An, DO NOT buy anything at Ben Thanh market in HCMC. Everything offered there (except items that specifically reference Saigon) is also found at the Old Town Market in Hoi An for MUCH less, typically half the price or even less than that demanded by the vendors at Ben Thanh Market.  My jaw dropped when we saw the identical t-shirts that we'd picked up for souvenirs at BT for 180vnd (after intense haggling down from 300) being sold for 100vnd at the Old Town Market in Hoi An.  Second point, the vendors at BT are extremely pushy and aggressive. They will literally grab you by the arm and pull you into their stall. My wife had to wrench her hand out of a vendor's clutches when we were there two days ago. And if you buy one item, expect a very aggressive sales pitch to buy another of the same or something else.  And once you buy your item, watch out! All the other vendors in the vicinity are watching who buys what, and once you make a purchase at one vendor, you will be beseeched and grabbed and followed by others as you walk past their stalls. It's literally like running a gauntlet.  We found the asking prices absurdly high; clearly the vendors are preying on tourists who don't understand the currency. If you don't have a currency calculator app on your phone, get one before you enter the market, and don't get rattled by the intense pressure to buy right away. Just be calm, do your calculations and make a decision. We saw one vendor selling cotton t-shirts for $30 that would be sold in Vancouver for $25. Just set your price and if they don't accept it, walk away. Guaranteed that they'll chase after you with a reduced price.  Back to the Old Town Market in Hoi An, the vendors are completely different, ie. polite, low key. We really wish we had started our souvenir shopping there rather than Ben Thanh Market.

Scam Alert: Ben Thanh Market, Ho Chi Minh City – The "ATM Terminal" Trick
I want to share my experience to warn anyone shopping at Ben Thanh Market in Saigon. I fell victim to a sophisticated payment scam, and I don't want the same to happen to you.
How the Scam Works
I was buying t-shirts from a specific vendor (see photo). When it came time to pay, I chose to use my card.
1. The "Error" Ploy: After tapping my card, the vendor claimed their portable terminal had an error and the payment didn't go through. No receipt was printed.
2. The Double-Cross: My banking app (Revolut) immediately showed a deduction of €120. When I showed this to the seller and asked for my goods, she refused, insisting: "The money will be automatically refunded to you, it’s just a system glitch."
3. The Trap: Naively, I believed her and left without the t-shirts.
The Banking Twist
A week later, I filed a dispute with Revolut. It was rejected. Why?
• ATM Manipulation: Somehow, this portable payment terminal was registered in the banking system as an ATM.
• False Confirmation: Despite telling me the transaction failed, the vendor later confirmed to the bank that the "cash withdrawal" was successful. It is much harder to dispute an ATM withdrawal than a retail purchase.
The Confrontation
Two months later, I returned to Ho Chi Minh City and went back to the same stall. The seller recognized me instantly but continued to lie, claiming she never received the money.
The most frustrating part? Just moments before I spoke to her, she was checking her banking app to look at today's sales. But the second I asked her to scroll back to the date of my transaction, she suddenly "didn't know how" to use the app and refused to show me.
Key Takeaways & Warnings:
• Cash is King: Avoid using cards at market stalls in Vietnam.
• The ATM Red Flag: If you pay by card and your app labels the transaction as "ATM" or "Withdrawal" instead of the shop's name, you are being scammed.
• No Receipt, No Deal: If the terminal "fails" but your money is gone, do not leave without a printed "Decline" slip or calling the tourist police.
Stay vigilant and avoid this seller at all costs!

Overcharged at souvenir stall – please be careful

Today I was overcharged at a souvenir stall inside Ben Thanh Market.

The seller quoted me 150,000 VND per fridge magnet in Chinese. Many other stalls sell the exact same magnets for 100,000 VND and even bargain down to 50,000 VND.

Because the currency has many zeros, I misunderstood the price at first and paid 450,000 VND for three magnets. After leaving the market and realizing the mistake, I returned to ask for a refund.

The seller refused, claimed the price was correct, and said the money had already been given to the boss. Several nearby vendors gathered, spoke in Vietnamese, laughed, and said it was impossible to return the money.

There are no clear price tags, and tourists can easily be confused by large numbers.

Please double check prices carefully before paying. I hope the market management can improve price transparency and protect visitors from this kind of experience.

🚩 Ben Thanh Market Review
Headline: Iconic vibe, but be prepared for extreme price markups!
"Ben Thanh Market is a must-see for the atmosphere and architecture, but if you are planning to shop, be very careful.
The price tagging here is almost non-existent, and the initial quotes are often 3 to 4 times higher than the actual value. For example, I was quoted 1,800,000 VND for an item that they eventually offered for 500,000 VND after I started walking away.
My tips for fellow travelers:
• Negotiate hard: Never accept the first, second, or even third price.
• The 'Walk Away' trick: This is your best weapon. If they don't agree to a fair price, start walking; they will usually call you back with a much lower offer.
• Check quality: Since prices are flexible, sometimes quality is too. Check your items thoroughly before paying.
• Fixed Price shops: If you hate haggling, look for the 'Fixed Price' stalls on the outer edges of the market to get a baseline idea of what things should actually cost.
It’s a fun experience, but stay sharp or you’ll definitely overpay!

I visited Ben Thanh Market a few weeks ago, and unfortunately it was not a good experience at all.

This place feels like a complete tourist trap. Most of the products are low quality but extremely overpriced. Unless you have very strong bargaining skills, it’s simply not worth the money.

What made the experience worse was the behavior of the sellers. Many of them were rude and aggressive. If you ask for a price and decide not to buy, some sellers start scolding loudly in their own language. A few shop owners even follow you until you buy something, and in some cases, they touch your hand or try to pull you back to the stall. There was absolutely no sense of manners or personal space, which made the visit very uncomfortable and stressful.

Overall, it was not a pleasant shopping experience at all.

If you’re looking for peaceful, budget-friendly shopping, I would strongly recommend skipping Ben Thanh Market. Instead, visit the street shops in Hoi An or Hanoi, where shops line both sides of the streets, sellers are much more polite, prices are more reasonable, and many stores stay open until late at night. Those places offer a far better and more enjoyable shopping experience.

Ben Thanh Market - still overpricing tourists
My husband and I are foreigners visiting Saigon again after many years.
While exchanging money nearby, we decided to walk through Ben Thanh Market out of curiosity.
My husband asked about a pair of fake crocodile Crocs-style sandals. a young woman, confidently quoted 1,500,000 VND.
When my husband turned to walk away, she immediately called him back:
“Sir, sir, 1,000,000 VND, okay?”
After he walked a few more steps, she dropped the price again to 500,000 VND.
At that point, my husband asked me:
“Why do people here still think foreigners are that naive?”
Even if someone has money, no one likes being treated like an easy target.
Unfortunately, the sales attitude and pricing practices at Ben Thanh Market still feel outdated and do not reflect the level of development Saigon has achieved today.
