I’d like to share this as a warning to all travelers who plan to exchange money or visit the area around Ben Thanh Market. While I was waiting for my boyfriend who was exchanging currency at Ha Tam Currency Exchange, I stood nearby and was approached by many street vendors, all of whom I politely declined.

Suddenly, a man came up to me and applied some kind of liquid onto my shoes, making them wet. I told him multiple times that I didn’t want my shoes cleaned, but he insisted and said it was free of charge. Shortly after, he dragged me to a corner of the building and continued cleaning my shoes.

I had already made up my mind not to let him do it for free, so I decided I’d pay him 100,000 VND. But when he finished, he suddenly demanded 950,000 VND (around $37), which shocked and angered me.

His tactic was to dirty my shoes with an unknown substance and then pretend to clean them for free, only to charge an outrageous amount afterward. It was a frustrating and expensive lesson, and I hope this review can help others stay alert and avoid being scammed by people like this.

2. Do try the products from many shops before buying from one shop.
3. The shop keepers are a bit rude. I found a few shopkeepers probably abusing a few white guys in their local language and laughing by pointing fingers to them.

I was prepared to haggle a little and be taken advantage of a little, since I am not a local. But what this woman in stall 518 did was appalling. I didn’t even want to bargain that much; people are trying to make a living and I figure a couple dollars here and there would do more for them than me. So for this woman to take advantage of me and then have no remorse is disheartening. I believe she assumed there’s nothing I can do about it once I leave town. I tried on an ao dai that fit in size XL (equivalent to an American size Medium) in red. I liked the fit, but wanted to change the color, so I asked her for a cream colored version and she tried to give me 2XL, I said thats too loose, so she gave me what we assumed was “XL.” We left town the same day. I opened the package to try the outfit on for a family photo shoot the next day. To my surprise, it was loose so I checked the tag. The “2” was cut out in front of the “XL” and the pants still had the “2.”

When I asked my cousin to confront her last night and ask for an explanation, she said she changed the pants for me so it would be more comfortable and that the top is XL. When I kept pressing, she said the warehouse must have made a mistake and it’s not her fault since it was in the package.

No ownership, no real apology. She said to bring it back and she’ll exchange it, knowing I don’t live in Vietnam.

We had heard a lot about Bến Thành Market on various forums and decided to visit since we were dining at Pizza 4P’s nearby. Unfortunately, the experience was quite disappointing.

The market was extremely overcrowded, and many vendors were not welcoming at all. Most stalls quoted prices that were easily 8x higher than the actual value, despite the products being of very questionable quality. Some vendors even got into arguments with tourists — which was surprising and unnecessary, especially considering tourism is such a major part of the local economy.

What surprised us even more was that the shops outside on the main roads offered better quality at far more reasonable prices, and the people there were genuinely friendly.

Overall, Bến Thành Market did not live up to the hype for us. The atmosphere, pricing, and attitude of several vendors made the visit less enjoyable than expected.

Shopping here is not a great experience. Many vendors start with top-tier pricing for mediocre-quality products and become overly aggressive if you don’t commit to buying. Heavy haggling is expected, and even then, value rarely feels aligned with the price.

Service from vendors varies, but the pushy sales tactics make browsing uncomfortable rather than enjoyable. It’s not a relaxed environment if you’re just looking around or comparing options.

That said, Ben Thanh Market is still worth visiting for local food. The food stalls offer solid Vietnamese dishes at reasonable prices and give a more authentic experience. Come here to eat and explore, but look elsewhere if you’re shopping for souvenirs or goods.

Ben Thanh Market – Iconic, But a Tourist Trap Alert!

Ben Thanh Market is one of Ho Chi Minh’s most iconic landmarks — centrally located and packed with everything a tourist might want, from souvenirs, clothes, bags, dried goods, to local snacks. But make no mistake — it’s a tourist trap.

Bargain hard. Always start with half the asking price or less. If it doesn’t work, walk away without looking back — chances are you’ll find the same thing elsewhere for much cheaper. Prices can be absurd, especially without visible price tags, and that’s a red flag. So do your homework beforehand if you don’t want to feel cheated.

The worst part? Sellers can be aggressive and even rude. Sometimes, just asking for the price seems to offend them. It's unfortunate because many are willing to spend, but the attitude pushes shoppers away.

One redeeming point — some stalls offer same-day tailoring, which is useful if you’re in a pinch.

In short: visit for the experience, but keep your expectations (and wallet) in check.

My visit to Ben Thanh Market was incredibly disappointing and far from the vibrant local experience it’s often advertised to be. As a tourist, I understand bargaining is part of the culture — but the outright rudeness and unwelcoming behavior we faced here went beyond that.

Firstly, several shopkeepers treated us with suspicion even before we approached their stalls — asking us not to touch any items, as if we were there to steal. This kind of hostility is not only off-putting but also deeply disrespectful to visitors.

One particularly unpleasant experience was at a stall selling fake football jerseys. A chubby, blond-ish, Asian-looking vendor quoted us an absurd price of 700,000 VND for a low-quality knockoff. When we declined, he became extremely rude — yelling at us to leave, saying “don’t come back,” and mocking my 12-year-old brother who was simply excited about the market. He continued to laugh and sneer at us every time we walked by, which was immature and unprofessional.

Another vendor blatantly ignored us multiple times and eventually waved us off aggressively when we tried to ask a question — only to turn around and warmly welcome local Vietnamese customers just moments later. This double standard in treatment was hard to ignore.

The market overall felt like a tourist trap — with most stalls inflating prices ridiculously and offering poor customer service. Out of the dozens of shops we walked past, maybe only 2 or 3 vendors were genuinely kind and respectful.

Ben Thanh Market could be a beautiful cultural hub, but our experience was ruined by hostility, overpricing, and a lack of basic courtesy. Would not recommend it to travelers looking for an enjoyable or fair shopping experience.

This is the kind of place where tourists get ripped off — and not just by a little. We’re talking 5 to 10 times the real price. Honestly, who comes to a place like this to buy fake stuff and expects to pay original prices?
Well, there are probably fools who do, which is why this scam still works. But in the long run, know this: this place will never be remembered as a great shopping spot to recommend to friends. Vietnamese sellers here will have to settle for scamming a few naive tourists instead of attracting a steady flow of visitors genuinely interested in shopping.
So here’s the message for you, tourist: don’t be a fool. Buy from official stores — same price, maybe even cheaper

Disappointing Experience at Chợ Bến Thành Market

We recently visited Vietnam as part of a guided tour and were genuinely excited when our knowledgeable tour guide, who has been working in the tourism industry since 1999, recommended Chợ Bến Thành Market as a great place for shopping and bargaining. Unfortunately, our experience was anything but enjoyable and left us, along with several others in our group, feeling disappointed and frustrated.

Our first stop was at an outdoor stall, where we were looking for a simple thank-you card. My wife picked one up to examine it, and the vendor told us it was 45,000 VND. We offered 30,000—a completely reasonable counteroffer given the bargaining culture we were encouraged to enjoy. Shockingly, the vendor snatched the card from my wife's hand, rudely refused the offer, and then started calling us names and swearing at us in Vietnamese. We are respectful and easygoing travelers who enjoy lighthearted bargaining, so being treated with such hostility was both unexpected and upsetting. This vendor was part of a cluster of stalls where the sellers wore orange shirts—an area we would advise avoiding based on our experience.

We continued into the main part of the market hoping for a better encounter, but things only worsened. At a watch stall, I was browsing some imitation Rolexes. The seller quoted an outrageous price of $1,200 USD for a fake watch and then insisted that I had to buy it because I’m American and, according to him, “rich.” I corrected him, stating that we’re Canadian, not wealthy, and not interested in being blatantly overcharged. It was offensive and presumptive.

Later, at a shoe vendor’s stall, my wife found a pair of fake Nike sneakers priced at an astonishing 1.5 million VND. She was able to negotiate a better deal and made a purchase, but the starting price was clearly exploitative. I also looked at a pair of rubber Birkenstock-style sandals priced at 900,000 VND—no negotiation allowed. Considering that real Birkenstocks cost about the same, it was absurd to ask that much for knock-offs.

In the end, our entire visit to Chợ Bến Thành Market felt like a tourist trap. The vendors seemed more interested in taking advantage of foreigners than in creating a fun, fair shopping experience. After speaking with others in our group, we found our negative experience was far from unique. Thankfully, we had much better interactions shopping on the streets outside the market, where the vendors were friendly, welcoming, and happy to negotiate respectfully.

We do not recommend Chợ Bến Thành Market to other travelers. Save your time—and your money—and shop elsewhere.

Leider war dieser Markt die schlimmste Erfahrung, die wir in Vietnam gemacht haben. Bis zu diesem Zeitpunkt hatten wir mit Vietnamesen wirklich durchgängig sehr positive Erfahrungen, vor allem in Punkto Höflichkeit und Respekt, aber in diesem Markt ist alles anders. Die Verkäufer haben sehr aggressiv versucht zu verkaufen und waren teilweise beleidigend, am schlimmsten war es als eine Verkäuferin mich hinter die Theke gedrängt hatte und den Weg versperrte bis ich sage was mein letzter Preis ist. Und bei keinem Stand konnte man länger 5 Sekunden stehen ohne intensiv bedrängt zu werden. Wirklich sehr unangenehm und aus meiner Sicht sind die Produkte, die man da sieht so schlecht, dass man sich das wirklich sparen kann & sollte. Wie auch in anderen Kommentaren gibt es wirklich deutlich bessere Orte in der Stadt, zum Beispiel auch Saigon Square, was ähnlich ist, aber schon deutlich angenehmer.

Mình và bạn người Úc đi chợ muốn mua thử mấy chiếc áo phông ở 1 sạp quần áo. Chị bán hàng hỏi mình muốn mua giá bao nhiêu, sau khi mình nói mình muốn đi vòng xem thêm thì chị vẫn cố bắt mình mua với lí do mua ủng hộ chị. Mình hỏi nếu mua nhiều thì giá tốt hơn thì chị không đồng ý và liên tục thúc ép mình mua, vẫn không quên xúc phạm chủ tay thẳng vào mặt mình. Mình không hài lòng và bỏ đi thì chị chạy theo đuổi theo chửi càng lớn tiếng. Bạn trai mình không hiểu sợ chị tấn công mình nên đã chặn chị lại - nhấn mạnh là ko dùng tay chân gì cả. Tuy nhiên chị không dừng lại tiếp tục lấn tới xúc phạm bằng tiếng anh nữa. Hai bên to tiếng vì bạn trai muốn bảo vệ mình thì chị xông vào cào bạn trai mình. Tất cả sự việc được camera an ninh ghi lại và có thương lái cũng như khách nước ngoài chứng kiến. Toàn bộ sự việc chỉ xảy ra trong chưa tới 10p nhưng ảnh hưởng tới sự an toàn, trải nghiệm và tâm lí của mình.

Đây là tóm tắt sự việc chính cho trải nghiệm khá tệ của mình vào ngày 4/12/2025. Có lẽ đây là lần cuối cùng mình quay lại chợ. Mình không muốn ảnh hưởng tới miếng cơm của ai, cũng lo tới an ninh an toàn của bản thân. Do lịch trình gấp gáp và cảm xúc nhất thời không thể bình tĩnh, mình lựa chọn không quay lại chỉ điểm với các chú an ninh để xử lí vụ việc ngay lúc đó. Tuy nhiên mình vẫn đang thấy khá hoảng và không biết nên xử lí việc này sao.

*Rất mong tìm được sự giúp đỡ của mọi người về vấn đề này. Hình ảnh là vết thương của bạn mình sau khi đã rửa sạch máu và bôi thuốc

Schrecklich. Sicherlich spielt hier der Metropolen- und Touristenfaktor eine Rolle, aber ein wirkliches Schnäppchen macht man hier, wenn man Vietnam schon bisschen bereist hat, nicht wirklich.

Die Preise setzen schon extrem hoch an, obwohl ja jedem klar ist, dass es sich bei dem überwiegenden Teil der Kleidung mutmaßlich um Fake - Artikel handelt. Zum Test habe ich mit einem Händler über ein T-Shirt verhandelt, was ich wo anders schon gekauft hatte 😂 - wir kammen bei einem Preis als "Best I can do" raus, der mehr als doppelt so hoch war wie der Preis den ich in einem anderen Laden etwas außerhalb verhandelt hatte. Bei gleicher Qualität.

Wer nicht grade Freude daran hat sich zu den Stoßzeiten durch die Menschenmassen zu quetschen sollte eher sehr früh oder kurz vor Schluss den Markt besuchen. So oder so wird man an jeder Ecke vollgelabert. Ein einfaches Nein reicht manchen Händlern offenbar nicht, sodass sie dann anfangen leicht an der Kleidung zu ziehen oder einen an den Armen anzufassen. Wenn man dann sagt, dass sie das lassen sollen, werden sie beleidigend.

Da das rege Treiben an und für sich schon interessant ist hier mein Tipp. Setzt euch eure Kopfhörer auf, vermeidet Augenkontakt zu den Händlern, füttert die Händler nicht, geht zielstrebig.

Oder gönnt euch eine Kleinigkeit zu essen im hinteren Teil des Marktes.

Wer wirkliche Schnäppchen machen will dem empfehle ich in die Randbezirke Richtung Chinatown zu fahren. Das ist nicht weit und mit dem Grap Bike in 5-10min für paar Cent zu erreichen. Da gibt es den gleichen redundanten Marken-Firlefanz (Patagonia, usw.) nur halt zu angemessenen Preisen und die Händler empfinde ich dort auch als diskret und sympathisch.

Es ist getrimmt auf abzocke, wer hier irgendetwas einkaufen tut muss verrückt sein, denn die Preise sind absurd hoch, die werden extra hoch gesetzt um scheinheilig zu handeln, ihr werdet an jedem Schritt die ihr durch die Hallen macht attackiert und gefragt ob ihr was kaufen möchtet, klar wollt ihr das, als Erinnerung, als Geschenk für die lieben daheim, für Freunde und Kollegen, dann zeigt ihr Interesse und schon fängt das Spiel an, für Artikel die beim Einkauf so günstig sind werden utopische Summen genannt, Beispiel ein Figur die beim Einkauf die selber 0,30€ Cent kostet, kostet euch 30€, dann wenn ihr nein sagt werdet ihr gefragt was euer Preis ist, die ist dann natürlich zu günstig, dann kommen sie dir entgegen doch auch dieser Preis ist absurd hoch.

I personally do not recommend this market at all. Almost every vendor inside is dishonest and tries to scam tourists!

We encountered a drink stall where every single beverage was spoiled—they were all sour and undrinkable, and there were even cockroaches in the watermelon juice. At first, we tried to explain the issue in English, but the staff pretended not to understand. It wasn’t until we angrily used an AI translator to yell at them in Vietnamese that they finally refunded us. And surprise—they suddenly understood English perfectly when it was time to apologize and give the money back. Unbelievable!

Also, do NOT buy their fruit. We asked four different vendors, and the prices were more than five times higher than those at other markets—and many of the fruits weren’t even fresh.

Honestly, this is the most scam-filled market in all of Ho Chi Minh City.
Do yourself a favor and stay away.

*Let op wat je betaald!*

Op 2 plekken in Vietnam garen gekocht waarbij ik 20.000 per stuk had betaald. Dit had ik zonder onderhandelen betaald, dus ik wist zeker dat het eigenlijk nog goedkoper kon. Bij de markt kon ik geen garen vinden. Na meerdere keren vragen, waarbij de dames mij in ieder geval wel wilden helpen, vond ik een vrouw die garen verkocht.

Deze moesten gek genoeg ergens vandaan gehaald worden. Ze zei dat ze er heel veel had en toen een man met een zakje van ongeveer 15 stukken wol aan kwam zetten, waarbij sommigen zelfs geen papiertje meer erom hadden of zelfs viezigheid erin hadden van het karton, koos ik er 4 uit en vroeg ik hoe veel deze kostten.

Hij pakte de rekenmachine erbij en typte 850.000 in! Ik was verbaasd en dacht dat hij een nul te veel had getypt, maar hij zei dat hij mij een prijs kon bieden van 820.000 dong. Na aangegeven te hebben dat ik het overal in Vietnam voor 20.000 per stuk had gekocht, bleef deze man zeggen dat hij niet lager kon bieden! Zelfs toen ik weg liep bood hij geen lagere prijs meer aan.

**Do Not Shop At This Store**

To get straight to the point: I purchased what was advertised as Napoleon Ground Coffee. The store priced it at 850,000 Vietnamese dong but later agreed to sell it for 630,000. However, I later found the same product in a supermarket for just 490,000.

Prior to my purchase, I had checked the official Shopee marketplace in Vietnam and saw that it was priced around 500,000. When I confronted the store about this, they deceitfully claimed that it was a different kind of bean. They went into great detail describing the beans and even offered a freshly brewed cup for us to taste. Pressed for time, I decided to buy it as a gift for a departing friend. My disappointment grew when I subsequently found the same product in a supermarket priced at 490,000.

What angered me the most was the feigned sincerity of the store owner. She pretended to be concerned, saying she didn't want tourists like us to be cheated. To make matters worse, upon opening the product, my friend informed me that it was damp and had lost its freshness.

When I get the chance, I will document this store to ensure future tourists are not similarly deceived.

I've attached a photo of the product's price in the supermarket for your reference.

인근 숙소에서 4일 머물면서 시장을 잘 경험했습니다.
1.  메뉴에 있는 음식을 주문하면 즉시 소스와 물수건을 주는데 별도요금인데다가 엉뚱한 금액을 요구합니다. 또 음료수를 보여주는데 다 바가지 요금을 씌웁니다.
2. 봉투에 담긴 자두를 얼마에 달라고 했는데 주면서 쓸쩍 반을 다른데 부어버리고 줍니다. 2배 바가지인 셈이죠.
3. 특히 저녁에는 과일이 더운 기온 아래서 오래동안 내어놓아 은근히 상해지고 있으며 이미 모든 상인들이 이런 바가지 수법에 익숙하여서 아무 양심도 없이 팝니다.
4. 다시 말씀드리지만 모든 상인들이 한통속으로 물건 속이기, 반찬이나 물수건 휴지 덤테우기 품질이나 양 속이기에 아주 숙달되어 있습니다. 구경만 하시고 물건이나 음식은 절대로 시장에서 거래하지 마세요. 여행기분 잡칩니다.

Chợ kiến trúc đẹp, không chỉ ở phần mặt tiền có phần khoảng không gian rộng mà còn hệ thống mái, dầm, vì kèo bên trong.
Còn mua hàng trong chợ này thì suy nghĩ kỹ nhé. Đối tượng của chợ là khách du lịch nước ngoài và Việt kiều thôi. Người trong nước mua trong đây là mua dao lam đấy :)))
Cung cách bán hàng: một số thương nhân tử tế, còn lại thì cũng có thành phần thì trời ơi, bán buôn như bà nội người ta vậy. Nói thách trên mây, nên nhắm được giá thì mua còn không thì khỏi mua. Mấy cái món bán trong đó bên ngoài có hết mà còn đầy ra, giá cả rõ ràng và rẻ hơn rất nhiều.
À mà chụp ảnh trong đó nhớ chú ý nha, kẻo bị ăn chửi. Thử cái gì thì cũng phải hỏi trước kẻo bị chửi nếu không mua, bạn mình còn bị đốt phong long. Một số tips là vậy, chúc mọi người có chuyến tham quan đi chơi vui vẻ:))

Se state facendo un tour del Vietnam e passate da Ho Chi Minh con l'intenzione di comprare qualcosa in questo mercato, il mio consiglio è di comprare in altre città. Questo mercato è caro, caotico e i venditori sono scortesi, poco propensi alla trattazione. I prezzi sono molto alti: ad esempio, una scarpa da ginnastica di marca l'ho pagata 850.000 (naturalmente contrattando) ad Nahoi, in questo mercato sono partiti da 2200.000. Pazzesco! Secondo me da evitare. Se provenite da altra città, come Hanoi, Ho Ian, il mio consiglio è comprate lì. Molto più economico e con gli stessi articoli ;-)

The trip to the Mekong Delta was one of the highlights of our Vietnam trip. Even the drive out of the city showed how the landscape is slowly changing – from hectic city life to green rice fields and tranquil waterways.  The boat trip through the small canals lined with dense palm trees was particularly impressive. You get an authentic insight into the lives of the people of the Delta. We visited small family farms, tasted fresh tropical fruits and local honey tea and could see how traditional sweets are made from coconut.  Special praise goes to our guide Nick. He organized the tour excellently, answered all the questions in detail and explained a lot about the region. Growing up here himself, he was able to give many personal insights, which made the tour even more interesting.  Overall a very nice and well organized excursion – absolutely recommended!
