I am writing this review to express my deep dissatisfaction with my experience at VinWonders today, particularly at the Aquarium buggy station around 4:00 PM. Today.  First of all, the entire buggy service experience was completely disorganized. I purchased a ticket that explicitly includes buggy rides, yet no wristband was provided at entry. As a result, I was forced to take out my ticket and prove my entitlement every single time I wanted to ride the buggy. This is inefficient, frustrating, and reflects very poor operational planning.  The most unacceptable part of this visit was the extremely rude behavior of a staff member at the Aquarium buggy station. I approached this staff member politely to ask for directions to other stations. He pointed out the way, and I clearly stated that I wanted to go by buggy. He immediately told me it was “not allowed.” When I explained that my ticket included the buggy service, he shouted at me, saying it was “not free,” before even bothering to look at my ticket.  This behavior was humiliating, aggressive, and completely unprofessional. Customers should considering this how VinWonders trains its staff. There was no apology, no courtesy, and no attempt to communicate respectfully.  VinWonders markets itself as a premium family attraction, yet this experience felt chaotic and hostile. Poor organization combined with rude staff completely ruined what should have been an enjoyable visit.  I strongly urge management to: • Properly train staff on basic customer service and respectful communication • Fix the buggy service process (wristbands should be provided at entry) • Investigate the behavior of the staff at the Aquarium buggy station  This was an unpleasant and disappointing experience, and I expect VinWonders to feedback seriously.

AVOID THIS PARK AT ALL COSTS. If you’re a Westerner — or frankly anyone outside their absurdly narrow weight range — expect humiliation, exclusion, and a complete waste of money.  I’ve visited over 150 theme parks and waterparks around the world, and nothing prepared me for the absolute nonsense at this park. I weigh 103kg — tall, athletic, under 10% body fat — and they refused me from nearly every water slide. They weigh you at every single attraction, and if you’re not between 60–100kg, you’re denied.  To make it worse, when I stepped on one of the scales, the lifeguard laughed at me before saying “no.” Being publicly embarrassed by staff is completely unacceptable.  Now here’s where the stupidity really shines: The average human weight globally is about 62kg, in Asia about 58kg, and in Europe roughly 80kg. That means their “safety range” immediately excludes an enormous chunk of the world’s population — adults, teens, men, women, families. If your daughter is under 60kg or you’re over 100kg, say goodbye to most of the slides. So why is the park always so quiet? Because management has literally engineered a system that blocks over half the world from participating. Brilliant business strategy.  And their weight logic? Completely illogical. They refused to let me ride a double slide solo at 103kg — even though the combined limit for two people is 160kg. Yet somehow, on a four-person raft, they allowed just two riders with no issue. How is a single 103kg rider “unsafe,” but two people totalling 160kg perfectly fine?  I asked if I could use a double tube alone — and was told “no.” Zero explanation. No consistency. Just dumb rules enforced without thought.  And let’s be clear: this isn’t “safety.” As someone who has worked in theme park engineering, I know ride structures are built to handle far higher loads. I’ve ridden identical slides from the same manufacturers in Europe, America, China, Australia, South America — and even at the other waterpark on the same island — with no weight issues whatsoever. This park is the only one inventing these bizarre restrictions.  To make the scam worse: none of this information is disclosed before buying tickets. No warnings. No disclaimers. You only find out after paying — and they refuse refunds. After I told my friends, ten people in our group chose not to go because half of us wouldn’t be able to ride anything together.  And the irony? They obsess over “safety” in the comments, yet I watched staff skipping the most basic safety practices. Workers climbing under tracks without harnesses, no test runs before opening, and even a coaster operating with a misaligned or loose wheel bolt — something any competent engineer would catch immediately. The rides themselves are secondhand, decades-old purchases that clearly haven’t been maintained well.  Operations are a joke too. Dispatch times of 12–15 minutes (with only one train!) turn what should be a 5–10 minute queue into a painful 30–40 minutes. This is what happens when management has no idea how to run a park.  If you’re from the West, or simply outside their ridiculous weight limits, skip this park entirely. Sun World in the south is run like a normal, competent operation — not whatever this mess is.  This park’s management is incompetent, discriminatory, and actively driving customers away. No wonder the place is practically empty. Avoid at all costs.

Настроение было испорчено — мы возлагали большие надежды на этот парк аттракционов. На кассе нам сообщили, что самая большая американская горка закрыта на реконструкцию, хотя нигде об этом не было указано. Мы хотели посетить парк именно из-за этой горки. Перед поездкой я специально заходил на сайт парка — там не было ни слова о закрытии. Мы спросили о возможной скидке, ведь узнали о закрытии только на месте, но нам отказали. Когда мы попросили позвать менеджера, сотрудник с ухмылкой ответил, что никакого менеджера нет. Билеты на двоих стоят около 70 $, и дело даже не в деньгах — мы не какие-то «скидочники» или «торгаши», просто хотели получить справедливую компенсацию. К тому же персонал плохо говорит по-английски — постоянно приходится переспрашивать или пользоваться переводчиком.  Our mood was ruined — we had high hopes for this amusement park. At the ticket counter, we were told that the main roller coaster was closed for renovation, even though there was no information about it anywhere. We came to the park mainly because of that ride. Before the trip, I even checked the park’s official website — there was no mention of the closure. We asked if they could offer a discount since we only found out about it on the spot, but they refused. When we asked to speak to a manager, the staff member smirked and said there was no manager. Tickets for two people cost about $70, and it’s not even about the money — we’re not bargain hunters or complainers, we just wanted fair compensation. On top of that, the staff’s English is terrible — we constantly have to ask them to repeat or use a translator.

**Awful, Disgusting, Overrated. Save your time and money!**  This place doesn't live up to the hype as Asia's budget Disneyland. It's the worst theme park we’ve visited.  Most rides are poorly maintained and often closed, especially when it drizzles. The iconic Sky Wheel was not operational during our visit.  The “Typhoon World” water park is filthy with flooded showers, and some areas smell like sewage.  The only redeeming spot was “The Sea Shell” aquarium, where we enjoyed the mermaid show and the shark tank bridge on the second floor, which hardly anyone notices.  If you're okay with paying full price just to stroll around the theme park, then go ahead; otherwise, consider spending your trip elsewhere.  The “Once Show” (closing show) was average at best—nothing special.  If we ever return to Phu Quoc, this park would be at the bottom of our list. And to those who’ve never been here but leave reviews, please stop—you're raising everyone's expectations unfairly!

VinWonders has non-crowded amusement park rides, but our experience was completely ruined by seeing the absolutely horrific animal abuse that occurs inside its aquarium and safari properties. Fish, penguins, and other animals are kept in cramped, dirty, and crowded conditions with zero care for enclosure design or enrichment. I would not recommend that anyone go here, unless you’re looking to have your trip ruined by seeing sad, lifeless animals packed into tiny cages.

Испорченный отпуск!!  Купили билеты на 2 дня за 1450 тыс донгов (5000 руб). Сначала сьездили на сафари, все понравилось. Поездку в парк отложили на более позднее время, тк через 4 дня переселялись в отель рядом с парком. При посещении нас не пустили, окапзалось сафари и парк нужно посещать обязательно 2 дня подряд, без перерыва. При покупке билетов нам это не обьяснили. На встречу не пошли, только предложили купить билеты по новой за 7000 руб на двоих!!! Отя мы купили билеты на 2 дня, а использовали только один. Отвратил ная работа администрации, испортили весь день и впечатления от отдыха!!!

Очень ждали этой поездки, специально купили билеты на 2 дня с неограниченным входом, чтобы можно было полноценно насладиться парком все два дня. Билеты обошлись в 6млн донгов, в рублях больше 20 тысяч на семью из 4 человек. Младшему ребенку 7 лет и рост 125см. Его не пускали на большинство аттракционов и горок в аквапарке🤦🏽‍♀️ надо ли говорить, что ребенок был очень расстроен, он строил такие планы на этот парк развлечений.. за день до этого были в парке в Sunset town за канатной дорогой и там того же самого ребенка пускали везде и все было в порядке, он был в восторге.  Стоимость этих двух парков очень отличается, но в vin wonders цена явно завышена. Он этих денег не стоит совсем. Несколько горок вообще закрыты, на других очень сильно пахнет горелым пластиком, явно горит проводка. Комната страха сломалась уже в 11 утра.  Электро-кары в парке платные, 100 тысяч донгов, хотя входной билет стоит недешево, можно было бы и включить их в стоимость..  Если вы выбираете между этими двумя парками, наш совет, езжайте лучше в sunset town. Там будет больше положительных впечатлений за меньшие деньги.
The buffet has really bad, food was cold, seats too tight as the Gateway restaurant is too small, and floor dirty, anywayI would rate it is 1 star buffet. I do not recommend the buffet in Vinwonder.

This has to be without any doubt the worst theme park I have ever been to in my life. I rather suspect the worst for anyone in the world. Beautiful architecture, perhaps the best I have ever seen for a theme park. But the good experience ends there. It is a deserted ghost town just as most of this VinGroup dream is( they own all of these sites, hotels, taxis etc.) While they are willing to charge for everyone who enters they do not tell you those below 1.4M cannot ride any rides. Nor do they tell you the 60+ year olds ticket cannot ride anything. But the worst part is that all rides are only open on a scheduled basis and then only for a 30 minute window. Then they close for 90 minutes. So you spend most of your time walking in 38C heat looking for a ride that is open. So we stayed for a bit over an hour and left this disaster There might have been 50 people in the park at 10 am. Hopefully soon they will be out of business.

A terrible experience, we went in with our 2 daughters of 6 and 9 years. Booked tickets including VIP service as we expected a busy day .  It's surrealistic, the park is empty and must be heavily loss-making in general. No need to ever buy the VIP package. The staff mentioned this to us.  When we wanted to enter the ladybin charge refused to let us in, out daughter of 9 years young had to buy an adult ticket, as she was exactly 141cm tall.  To buy an additional adult ticket was not enough, more was needed, she also needed a VIP adult upgrade. If we would not pay another 50 usd on the spot the whole family would not be able to enter.  Called the service desk, they mentioned that I needed to send an email and they would respond within an hour. Well...that did not happen. Called again and a not so friendly voice mentioned to send another email and then the management team of Vinwonders would take 2 weeks (.....) to review our issue. Then if it was approved by top management it would take another 3 months to refund the extra money. We needed to send copies of passports and a special form.  2 hours later and not yet been able to enter, but already paid 300usd in total, we decided to pay the additional 50usd and go in.  2 attractions were closed and 1 stopped in the middle due to electric problems, which happens regularly according to the staff.  Poor management, really sad to see as the park could be a great tourist attraction. However, with the attitude to scare away tourists it will never lift off.

Im Internet werden Ticket Angeboten für nur den Besuch im Aquarium und Combo ,wenn du vor Ort bist gibt es kein Ticket nur für das Aquarium . Es gibt Leute ohne Kinder die sich gern nur das Aquarium anschauen und müssen jetzt den vollen Preis bezahlen für vinwonder ,schlechte Abzocke meinen Gästen werde ich abraten dort hin zu gehen .

My husband had a business trip on the island and so I decided to take my three kids for the day, they were very excited however that was quickly destroyed at the hassle it was and the rudeness we experienced, firstly I assumed a theme park would accept eftpos and have atm machines inside, we went in and tried to purchase a locker and they only took cash, there was not a single atm inside, fortunately one of the staff let me back outside to use the atm, that was the first half an hour into our visit which was completely troublesome, then my son was in tears as he had attempted to go on a waterslide and was scolded by the staff that he had a button on his swim shorts, how was he to know when all of the signage and rules are in Vietnamese. Luckily he had another pair of sports shorts to wear otherwise he would have missed out on all of the waterslides. The next issue we faced was there was nothing we could go on as a family due to the height restrictions which are much stricter than in Australia, the lifeguards are all extremely rude at explaining anything and my youngest child really missed out on most of the activities as she was not tall enough so there is not a good variety for younger children. I could not even ride with the little one on the smaller waterslides that she was scared to go on alone…..the park is very large and hot and to get to each section takes a lot of time, many of the amusements are just for looks and did not have any fun activities to do. The worst part of the experience however was my son got caught on the high ropes zip line as he accidentally clipped one part to the wrong section, my other son had to call for my help as the ride was not attended to well with staff on phones, I then raised the attention to the staff that my son was stuck and it took some time for a staff member to get up to him. Once they did it was quite a difficult retrieval as he was very stuck, it took about 40 minutes I was very panicked because he was in direct sun with his legs hurting in the harness and feeling very distressed, eventually after putting my friend on the phone who could communicate in Vietnamese they managed to get him down and take his hat and water bottle to him, however once free he was left to complete the course by himself feeling distressed and disoriented, and we had to coax him through the rest of the course to safety. All in all the staff did their best during the difficulty and were helpful however they were not well equipped to deal with an emergency situation that could have been really dangerous. Unfortunately the park is all smoke and mirrors and we all left hot, flustered, traumatised and disappointed. Save your money for other activities as it was $200 aud for 4 people.
Das Management muss den Gästen kommunizieren, wenn 80 Prozent der Attraktionen geschlossen hat. Man darf dann nicht den vollen Eintritt verlangen!

We went as part of a Combo ticket which included the Safari Park. We arrived in the early afternoon after morning at Safari. The place was literally empty. My daughter simply stayed on rides she wanted to do again. Note, there are not many rides at this place and the height requirement is strictly followed. However, where this place really disappointed was the way they handled a breakdown. During our visit, approx 3pm there was a power failure. Everything shut down. Very few staff spoke english so no one knew what was going on. Only Lazy River remained available. 3 hours later, at 6:15pm, after the waterpark closing time, the power was restored. However, it was limited and the Fountain Show was also cancelled. At this point staff should have been at the exits with vouchers (and apologies) inviting guests to come back another day for free to make up for the lost day and experience that they paid for. Was anything offered? NO. After some searching, I found a manager who was not in hiding, and only after alot of discussion he agreed to a return ticket, only after having my photo taken 3 times and explaining to them what time I arrived they agreed we could come back, only tomorrow, only for waterpark, no buffet, only this and only that. I'm not trying to cheat them and the park is literally empty. At the exit I still had to locate someone who would give me a voucher. Again, manager hiding in an office. The management of this place could not have handled this worse. I understand power cuts happen, but to not give people a return ticket for the experience they paid for is simply cheating customers. Sorry for the length of this but I am also writing for the many Russian guests who didn't have the english skills to argue with the management, they were out of luck. When we finally left, my daughter said she didn't said she didn't like the whole place that much and really didn't want to come back anyway. She said it was so empty it felt creepy. Instead we discovered the new Aquapark at the cable car, which we visited twice. Vinpearl management had a chance to fix this and instead they took the cheap way and now everyone can read this.

We are frequent travellers from Australia, who travel twice a year. We have been to many destinations such as USA, Canada, Singapore, Bali, Japan, Brazil, Sweden, etc. but we’ve never experienced such rudeness from staffs at such as Vinpearl Land.  We were at Vinpearl Land Phu Quoc on Friday 27 December 2019. Our group consist of 8 people. We were lining up at the Rotary Bees. We waited for close to 10 minutes before we finally got to the front of the queue. When it was our turn, we were stopped by the attendants, who said that we had to let a group of kids who just arrived behind us to go on the ride first.  When we queried the attendants why they get priority over us, as we did not see any signs indicating this nor did they tell us this when we were waiting in line. He just smiled and waved us off as though we were some insignificant flies.  We asked to speak to his manager, who’s name is Huynh Thanh Phong. We asked Phong whether this was a policy of Vinpearl Land and if so, why weren’t there any signs to indicate this, or was this rule made up by the ride attendant at his own discretion just because he had a distaste for foreigners who spoke English and not Vietnamese?  Phong turned around and in Vietnamese, told the ride attendant “these people were first inn line, they should have been allowed to go on the ride first”, so clearly this was the rule that the Rotary attendant made up at his own discretion. Both the ride attendant and his manager, Phong didn’t know that we understood what they were saying!  Please avoid Vinpearl resorts, especially at Phu Quoc at all costs.  If you’re still going, do NOT expect too much, this place is mainly suited for the locals, international travellers.  Go to Disneyland or Disney World, 100 times better, we know, we’ve been to both.

it has absolutly nothing to do with phu quoc or the vietnamese culture !! huge buildings in a fantay world with overrated european prices. if you want to be i an resort out of everyting that has do to with the country you are travelling, you will be at the right place. it makes me sick. too many chinese people of beiing horny to gamble in big amounts.

Inte värt en enda krona för att åka hit. Det finns cirka 2 karuseller för ”ungdomar” att åka. Vattenlandet måste man vara 4 personer för att kunna åka något värt att åka, vi som åkte endast två personer hit hade 2 rutschkanor att välja mellan. Var av en fick jag inte åka för att jag hade dragkedja på badbyxorna. Så att betala 200 kronor för att åka 2 karuseller och 1 rutschkana var under all kritik.. Som följande skrivit är akvariet också förfärligt. Lägg pengarna på något annat istället för att åka hit..

Была вчера в аквапарке Винперл, на горке получила перелом крестца! Чётко соблюдала технику безопасности из-за которой вероятно и получила травму. Инструктор говорил не откидываться назад во время катания, а наклониться чуть вперед, в итоге во время катания в таком положении и от ударов потоками воды, в какой-то момент просела в надувную ватрушку слишком глубоко и ударилась крестцом о горку. Экстренно отвезли в госпиталь, сделали рентген - перелом. Горка опасна для жизни! Удар произошел во время одного из поворотов на ней. Теперь я могу только лежать, даже в туалет не могу сходить, меня необходимо транспортировать в Россию и только в горизонтальном положении, а Винперл пытается отстраниться от решения вопроса. В госпитале со мной был представитель Винперла, он оплатил рентген, консультацию и выписанные таблетки и после этого стал просить подписать бумагу, что мое лечение оплачено и я не имею претензий, а я имею просто огромную претензию! Лечения фактически никакого не было, только рентген и обезболивающие препараты, после чего в больнице меня отпрвили в отель, а как я туда поеду - это моя проблема, "наша страховка это не покрывает" твердил представитель аквапарка. В итоге с горем пополам выбила у них трансфер, но дали обычную легковую машину, каким образом меня в нее класть, как ехать и как из нее вынимать - никого не волновало. Получается исключительно по вине аквапарка я получила серьезную травму, чреватую многими последствиями,теперь могу только лежать и лежать буду долго, а они отстраняются от минимальной компенсации, которая мне необходима - просто организовать трансфер лежа. Если от Винперла не поступит компенсации их вины - буду подавать на них в суд. Врагу не пожелаешь попасть в такую ситуацию, не ходите в этот аквапарк, там опасные для жизни горки и никто вам не поможет если вы на них себя изуродуете!
Un DisneyLand version 0 Pour les chinois ou russes peut-être et encore... Nullissime ... Tellement nul que drôle 😂

We visited the Vinpearl water park last week with kids 130cm. The feeling on entry was that it lacked soul and was just a big nasty corporate venture. The people running the water park were rude and wouldn't let my husband on the ride because his board shorts had a zip!... the big water slides were fun for the kids but they couldn't go on more than 2 or 3 because of their height... and it wasn't cheap. We would say save your money and treat the kids to an ice-cream instead. Souless waste of money and time.

A complete waste of time. The splash pad/slide area was rusted and the panels lifted as you walked on them. Half the buckets didn't dump water because they were rusted in place. Adults were not allowed to use the slides, so I couldn't play with my children. The wave pool barely qualified as such, and the lazy river was about 2 foot deep so even my 5 year just walked in it. My 8 year old couldn't ride anything but a couple of slides and one family ride, but it required 4 people and we only had 3. Supposedly the park will find an employee to ride with you, but they wouldn't for us. You can rent towels and lockers. However, the "locker" just means giving your belongings to a staff member who keeps it out of sight. If you want anything during the day, such as money from your wallet, you have to ask for it. You don't have access yourself. I found that incredibly annoying. Almost none of the kiddie rides were working /staffed, so we only rode the carousel. It had no lights or music. The aquarium was fine, but incredibly hot. In fact, there was no adequate air conditioning in any building. We ate at a stand on the water park side and my burger was raw. We took the free shuttle bus there from our resort, which took about an hour and was very crowded. It was a miserable day. I couldn't recommend this place to anyone.

Akvariet är horribel! Pingvinerna är helt gula och har tappat massor av sina fjädrar. Sedan har dem flera av den akut hotade beluga stören i ett trångt akvarie. Trånga akvarier gäller för alla djur i akvariet. Jag mådde psykiskt och fysiskt dåligt efter att ha besökt akvariet!  Tivolit var trist, inte en enda attraktion var värd att åka.  Vattenlandet är okej, tyvärr måste man vara fyra personer på flera attraktioner och dessutom minst 140 cm lång, så vattenparken är varken för barn eller vuxna par.

Das hätten wir uns sparen können. Abgesehen vom Eintritt 500000Dong der für diesen"Park"übethöht ist können wir sagen:spart euch diese Enttäuschung. Es gibt ein paar große Rutschen die nur zu 4 zum rutschen gehen.Dabei muss man über einen langen Weg den schweren Rutschreifen für 4 Personen selber holen und dann in einen Lastenaufzug heben.Das Personal sieht dabei gelangweilt zu absolut unorganisiert. Wenn wir nicht zu 4 gewesen wären hätten wir gar nicht rutschen können. Die anderen paar Single Rutschen waren eher ermüdend. Leider nix!
Niet ons type pret/dierenpark. De dieren hebben erg weinig leefruimte en er drijven zelfs dooie vissen in de aquarium.

From the moment I entered this place, I instantly felt as if I were at a cheap copy of Disney World. The music heard on the loud speakers were also from Disney cartoons however not the original ones.  It was hot so we decided to start at the water park - at 11am in the morning the shower rooms smelt like pee - and what struck me most was the fact that there were no toilets and signs everywhere for people not to pee in the showers.....that was the first joke of the day.  The second one was that my husband and I spent the first half an hour trying to find an entertaining slide that we (2 adults) could go on but unfortunately that was impossible since the 3 entertaining slides there are in the whole water park accomodate 4 people only not more not less. The greatest problem was that there were no other couples that we could go up with - wonder why there were only a few dozen people with their kids in the kids area?  The third joke was that none of the employees understand English - now if this is actually the case or if they were just acting it out so as not to deal with clients, I don’t know. We tried to explain to around 10 employees that if there is no appropriate slide that we can go on since we are 2, we could might as well get our money back and leave. They just stared blankly at us, not a clue of what we were saying. The only thing they could say was ‘yes madam’ but they weren’t understanding a thing. After an hour of going around trying to find something to do, we decided to buy ourselves some food. My husband went to ask for the bag from the locker to get his wallet. The lady at the counter started sulking when he got out his wallet and gave her the bag back - yes another ridiculous thing is that the lockers are behind the reception area so everytime you need something from your bag, you need to ask an employee to bring it for you. We went to get something to eat but the only thing we could find that didn’t look like plastic was an ice-cream cone and a can of 7up and Coke. The chips and sausages offered were very similar to those plastic food stuff we used to play with when we were young.  Anyway, we decided to go to the amusement park to see what there was to offer there. Back at the locker area, my husband asked for his bag and a very unmannered employee at the desk told him in very broken english and it took us a whole 5 mins to understand, that he cannot come and go every few minutes asking for his bag (he had only asked 3 times if you can call it that - once to get the wallet, then she had to take the bag back to the locker and again to get our things to leave). Purely rude and very unmannered - if not to say ridiculously stupid! We went back to the pee stinking showers to get changed and left.  At the amusement park the only thing that amused us was the fact that there were rides, but no one to operate them. We asked around the place to see if maybe there were some opening hours and we got the answer that it is normal since there aren’t enough employees to manage the rides. Again we tried asking for a refund since again, we couldn’t use the facilities of the park as there was no one to man them. But again, the employees did not know english.  Finally after just a few hours, we decided to leave the place as it was useless sticking around in that hot weather and we couldn’t ride anything.  One word of advice for the park - trying to imitate Disneyworld is not getting you anywhere since my slide and swing in my back yard are much more enjoyable that your crappy place. We wasted $44 + another $2 for the towel and locker rental as well as our time and in return got a crappy day surrounded by uneducated, rude employees.  One word of advice for my fellow travellers - don’t bother going to this place - the sea and waves in Phu Quoc in July and August are by far much more entertaining and thrilling.

Vinpearl land was like the places we visit in the US: pricey, noisey and not at all authentic. It is very expensive for what it is. It is a shame that Phu Quoc's authenticity has been destroyed by huge enterprises like this.

The best water rides you had to be 4 people to take. The arcade was in bad shape, almost every games was broken or missing balls etc. Carousel was for children. I was shocked at the aquarium to see the animals had so little space and some of the habitat was fake. We felt bad for the animals to see them live so bad that we went right away.

Thank god for the aquarium, it was great and the only saving grace for this park. Watching the fish feeding show was great fun, from the stingrays gliding through the water and across the tank glass to the ever hungry turtle nipping at the feeder. The waterpark however was not so good. The waterslides barely had enough water running down them, adults and kids alike got stuck constantly. The ‘5D’, more like 1D theatre had seats that didn’t move or vibrate, no water spray or smells. The rides were ok but even when there are no queues you have to exit and line up again, and without explanation a ride would close for seven minutes. The waterpark closes at 6pm, well the slides stop but there are heaps of people still playing in the water which gives the appearance that you can remain. But if you check your bag in a locker or hire towels, make sure you have collected your bag and returned the towels by 6pm otherwise you have to try and find a staff member and then security for the return of your bag and towel deposit which is expensive at VND150k per towel. For food, the Lotteria serves a few eastern meals and burger combos at about VND 70k each. Getting to the park is easy, just hop on the free shuttle bus, you can get a timetable with the purchase of your ticket from any tour company that sells them. We bought our tickets from Johns Tours which is where the bus stops also to pick you up. If you hang around at the park for the light show make sure you are in the line quick for the return bus to Duong Dong otherwise you might be waiting a while. Travel time from Johns Tours to the park takes about 50 mins. If you do want to visit, I recommend combining Vinpearl Land and Safari ticket and doing both in one day as there's not enough attractions to keep you interested for the whole day. I have attached photos of the bus schedule (current as of April 2018) if you want to check where the bus stops for pickups.

kan inte förstå varför detta har så högt betyg. pigvinerna ser ut som dom är redo att ta livet av sig , fiskarna har extremt dålig syretillförsel och i hela akvariet finns inte en enda riktig växt. efter att ha sett akvari2t gick vi raka vägen ut och jag tycker inte någon ska stödja detta företag för vad dom gör mot djuren är hemskt.
Vi gick in i akvariet, de va vidrigt att se hur fiskarna lever. Dom har knappt utrymme att simma runt. Flera fiska shippade efter andan, allt va i plast, Inga riktiga växter. Vi vände och åkte hem.

How can you ask a person for $25 entrance fee and get nothing in return? Most of the rides were boring and the atmosphere was dull. If you have kids and plenty of cash, it's might be your kind of thing.

Me veo en la obligación de dar tan mala nota exclusivamente por el acuario, tienen a los pobres peces hacinados. Peceras muy pequeñas con mucha cantidad peces, tanto los pequeños como los grandes tipo tiburón, nadando casi en círculos,de verdad que salí de allí con mucha lastima... Lo cierto es que fuimos a pasar el día a las piscinas ( geniales ) y el resto de atracciones fueron de rebote, el cine y los videojuegos estuvieron muy divertidos.. el precio de 20€/pers con transporte gratuito esta muy bien. We visited Vinpearl Land and waterpark with our children and saw a person drown in the waterpark. There was a young life guard on duty, but he obviously didn’t know any CPR. The War Remnants Museum (formerly the Museum of American War Crimes) in Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam, is one of the country's most frequented and sobering museums. It presents a graphic and uncompromising perspective on the horrors of the Vietnam War (known locally as the American War), with a strong focus on the impact on Vietnamese civilians.

Key Themes and Exhibits:
· Military Hardware: Courtyard displays of U.S. artillery, tanks, jets, and helicopters, as well as unexploded ordnance.
· "War Crimes" Exhibits: Confronting photographic evidence and descriptions of the effects of Agent Orange, napalm, and phosphorus bombs. This section includes preserved fetuses affected by dioxin.
· Historical Documentation: Rooms dedicated to the international anti-war movement, war photography (including the work of photojournalists who died during the conflict), and the postwar struggle for justice by victims.
· Prison System: (did not get to see as group tour Rushing for time)
· Requiem Exhibit: A moving tribute to Vietnamese and international war photographers from both sides who lost their lives.

Visiting the War Remnants Museum in Ho Chi Minh City was one of the most powerful and emotional experiences of my trip to Vietnam. I went in expecting to learn about history, but I left with something much deeper: a profound reflection on humanity, suffering, and resilience.
The exhibits—especially the photographs—were deeply moving. Some images were so raw and disturbing that I almost cried while walking through the galleries. I found myself needing to pause and take time to process what I was seeing. The human cost of the Vietnam War, especially for civilians, was heartbreaking.

What struck me most was how personal the museum felt. It doesn’t just present facts or military artifacts; it shows the faces, names, and lives of people affected—both Vietnamese and foreign. The section on Agent Orange and its lingering effects across generations was particularly difficult to take in.

I left with a heavy heart but also with a greater understanding of Vietnam’s past and an even stronger appreciation for the strength and kindness of its people today. Despite everything the country endured, I was met everywhere with warmth and openness. This visit changed the way I think about war, history, and reconciliation.

If you're in Ho Chi Minh City, I believe this museum is a must-visit. It’s not easy, but it’s important.

Amazing little museum. 3 floors of information pertaining to the Vietnam war and it’s lasting effects. In particular, I found the “War Photographers” section to be captivating…a lot of stories in there about journalists all over the globe that were intent on capturing the war and the brutality therein, often times paying the ultimate price for their attempts to broadcast the truth. The entire museum could easily take 2-4 hours if you’re intent on reading all the descriptions (provided in Vietnamese and English).

One small note - I didn’t see any warnings, but some of the content in here can definitely be unsettling. I’m glad they haven’t shied away from this, as it’s important to show the horrible effects of the war directly. But there are quite a lot of pictures of dead and mutilated bodies, especially within the “War Crimes” room. Also, there’s a large section dedicated to the health ramifications of Agent Orange, showing the deformities (in both children and adults, Vietnamese and foreign) and mental conditions caused by that horrible chemical. I saw a lot of families with young kids in here, and a lot of the children had slightly terrified looks on their faces. I assume if it was affecting to me, it may have been doubly so for them. Again, I recommend coming and not turning your face away from the horrible facts and effects of the war, but if you’re sensitive or have young children who may not be mentally prepared for such imagery, you may want to avoid certain exhibits that you feel could include such photographs (mainly War Crimes and Agent Orange are the two areas that are disturbing, I haven’t included any pictures from them below).

If you’re in Saigon even a little bit interested in the history and lasting effects of the war, this is a must-visit museum. I visited on a Saturday afternoon and it was crowded, but still easy to see everything.

The War Remnants Museum in Ho Chi Minh City offers a powerful and thought-provoking experience that dives deep into Vietnam's wartime history, particularly the Vietnam War. From the moment you enter, you're met with striking exhibits outside displays include American helicopters, tanks, and bombshells that set the tone before stepping inside.

The museum’s galleries are well-curated, showcasing raw and emotional photography, first-hand accounts, weapon displays, and reconstructed prison conditions. One of the most impactful sections focuses on the long-term effects of chemical warfare, especially Agent Orange, with sobering imagery and survivor stories.

The prison exhibit with life-sized mannequins and a preserved guillotine is chilling and historically significant.

There’s also dedicated space exploring global support during the war, along with detailed panels about Da Lat and Phu Quoc prisons. The layout encourages reflection and personal interpretation, rather than forcing a political stance.

You’ll want to spend at least 1~2 hours here to take in the emotional weight and historical context fully. It's not a light visit, but it's an essential one for anyone wanting to understand Vietnam’s past from a Vietnamese perspective.

Respectful dress and quiet observation are appreciated in there.

The Museum is easily accessible from central areas such as District 1 by motorbike, taxi, or bus. Opening hours are from 7:30 AM to 5:30 PM (ticket counter closes at 5:00 PM). The standard entrance fee is 40,000 VND; children aged 6–15, students, seniors, people with meritorious service, and those from remote areas receive a 50% discount; children under 6 enter free. The museum consists of an outdoor display area and a three-story exhibition building with about 4,522 m² of indoor space, plus over 3,000 m² outdoors. At the front yard and outdoor grounds, visitors can see military equipment such as the M48 Patton tank, UH-1 “Huey” helicopter, F-5 fighter jet, A-1 Skyraider aircraft, and various bombs, along with artifacts like the reconstructed “tiger cages” used to detain political prisoners, a guillotine, and victim statues, offering clear representations of wartime conditions.

Inside, the ground floor introduces the theme “The World Supports Vietnam in the Anti-American War” with more than 100 photos and 145 documents. The first floor focuses on “War Crimes” and “Consequences of Agent Orange” through images, artifacts, and documents. The second floor includes exhibitions such as “Historical Truth,” “Vietnam — War and Peace,” “Memoirs,” and war photography by Japanese and international correspondents. Displays feature captions in Vietnamese, English, and Japanese. The museum does not have a dedicated parking lot; visitors can park motorbikes at nearby spots a few meters from the entrance, with fees around 4,000–5,000 VND. The indoor space is kept clean and organized by exhibition theme.

War Remnants Museum – A Powerful and Eye-Opening Experience

As a family of four, our visit to the War Remnants Museum in Ho Chi Minh City was both powerful and educational. The museum presents the Vietnam War from a Vietnamese perspective, offering insights and stories that challenged our previous understanding and encouraged meaningful reflection.

The exhibits are thoughtfully curated, with impactful photography, preserved war artifacts, and deeply personal accounts that humanize the tragedy of war. Walking through the displays, we learned a great deal—not just about the history and events, but about the ideology and perspective of a country that lived through immense suffering and resilience.

Some sections are difficult to take in, especially for younger visitors, but they sparked important conversations for our family. We left with a deeper understanding of Vietnam’s history and a strong appreciation for the value of peace and empathy.

This is not just a museum visit—it’s an experience that stays with you. Highly recommended for families seeking a deeper, more global perspective on history.

Visiting the War Remnants Museum is a powerful and emotional experience that really stays with you. It's one of those places that gives you a deeper understanding of history in a way that books or documentaries sometimes can't. The museum focuses heavily on the Vietnam War, especially from the perspective of the Vietnamese people, and it doesn’t shy away from showing the impact of the conflict.

The exhibits are very well put together, with a mix of photographs, personal stories, official documents, and military equipment. Some sections, like the one about Agent Orange and the aftereffects of chemical warfare, are heartbreaking and hard to look at, but they are incredibly important. It’s not always an easy visit emotionally, but that’s part of what makes it so meaningful.
Outside, there’s a display of tanks, helicopters, and fighter planes, which adds to the scale and reality of what happened. Inside, you move through rooms that tell different parts of the story, from the roots of the war to the protests around the world and the aftermath that’s still felt today.

The museum isn’t about taking sides. It’s about remembering the human cost of war and learning from the past. It's also a reminder of how resilient people can be. I really appreciated how respectful and thoughtful the space felt, and how it encourages reflection rather than judgment.

If you're in Ho Chi Minh City, this museum is a must-visit. It’s not just about history, it’s about empathy and understanding. Give yourself time to take it all in. It’s not something you’ll want to rush.

The best place to visit in Saigon. You'll see how Vietnam and the US fought the war. Every minute detail of the war is being shown in the museum. I think it's such a great place to visit if you're interested in the history of Vietnam or you love reading about the world politics or international relations.

An amazing museum experience and I thoroughly recommend going here when in HCM. There is a large amount of exhibits and some are very confronting/moving; definitely a good way to see the Vietnam war from not a western perspective.

The place is quite unique and especially if you are into understanding the origins of modern Vietnam this is the best place. It accurately depicts the reasons and more importantly the depth of impact of the war. It takes you on an incredible journey of their history and how they fought for their freedom. Please take around 2 to 3 hours to enjoy the entire journey.

Best museum in HCMC if you want to understand more about Viet Nam War and how miserable people was during the war time. As our family has senior member used to serve military on the opposite site, this museum brought back long lasting memories, how and why the equipment been used for what purpose, remind us that war is not glory but pains for both sides, that we should not seek for war but must always prepare for it.

Of course propaganda is an usual thing you will see in museum like this. But I admit that if you already watched documentaries about Viet Nam war, or part of it, you will be satisfied with your subjectivity. Ticket is cheap (1.5USD), I want to pay more so they could expand the sites and more room to display.

Definitly a place i would recommand to unterstand more about the history of the country, even if you are not a history nut. No need to book a ticket before, its 40k VND per person, we waited 5min in the queue at 12am

Well worth a visit. This was a big event in the history of the country. And if you think it only finished 50 years ago! I’ve grown up only knowing one side of the story, but as usual there are two sides. There’s an interesting article about an American senator that served in Vietnam. Ive added some photos for interest. There’s a large indoors exhibition. The tiger cages were not what I was expecting.

A must see place to visit. Very touching!
Many images and objects related to the Vietnam wars and its lasting impacts (dioxin poisoning..). Some images may be difficult for young children. I recommend it for ages 10 and up.
Moving and inspiring giving the current Vietnam. My respect and admiration for the people Vietnam has increased even more, even though it was pretty high already.

Museum on the History of the Vietnam War. Numerous exhibits in the outdoor area as well as within the building. On the left side you can see how the prisoners were treated. In the middle part of the exhibition, numerous photos can be seen showing the horrors of war and the suffering of the various people. Certainly not everything is suitable for children. All in all, a very objective account of the Vietnam conflict shows what war means for the affected people, no matter which side.

Didn't take many pictures inside but it was truly a worthwhile visit with lots of things to learn from this very tragic event in history. Also shows how cruel human beings can be! 😞I guess the saddest part is to know that recent events happening in the world have shown that mankind still hasn't learnt from our past mistakes.

Eye opening experience. Vibe was heavy as the museum highlights the harsh outcomes of war and the toll it takes on humanity. A lot of graphic images, war artifacts on exhibit. A must go when in Saigon to learn about its troubled and bloody history. I had a very educational and humbling experience. Definitely worth my time.

There is a short flight of stairs to enter the main museum, once inside, there is an elevator which is convenient for those with wheelchairs and kid strollers.

War Remnants museum is reminding us of the war days of Vietnam against the US.There is a great peace of exhibition i.e tank,missiles and plenty of pictures are put in us in those days and we feel pride How Vietnamese are resilient .A must visit this historical monument.

Very cool museum.
Never felt so much pain inside a museum. It was awful what happened.
Glad I got to learn more. It makes me a little bit more considerate to Vietnamese people since they can be a little hard and grumpy.( mostly to tourists).
Tourist should go so they can understand that you need to show a lot of respect in their country.
They don’t have a responsibility to be the best hosts. But we have the responsibility to show gratitude for them letting us be in their country even though most of westerners took so much from them.

Not suitable for kids in my opinion even though they had a kids room where they can play and dress up in Vietnamese customs.

Tourists must come here to closely understand and emphasize with Vietnamese people & their history and the pain they have gone through, colonial crimes & war crimes been passed generation to generation and treated like experimental rats by Americans. We happened to visit the War Remnants museum on the 50th anniversary of reunification. So it was a huge day in Vietnam marking 50 years since the end of the war. The museum was only 80,000 Dong each (~£2.30) to get in. We felt this was amazing value.
Before entering me and my girlfriend had no knowledge about the Vietnam war. However exiting 3 hours later we felt as thought we knew a lot. Inside the museum you can read the full timeline of the war. It’s quite distressing seeing what the Americans did and many people were quite emotional. There are photos which are very disturbing and show the extent of the pain the Vietnamese went through.
Outside the building there were military vehicles and weapons which were fascinating to look at.
Overall really glad we went and it has given us a real insight to the horrors that occurred.
This really is a must if you are visiting Ho Chi Minh City.
The war remains shows the painful history and the patriotism of the Vietnamese.
What happened during this war is heartbreaking. Learning more about it gives you such an insight to how hard Vietnamese people fought for freedom. A trip to the Chu Chi Tunnels is a must too.

Powerful and somber, the War Remnants Museum in Ho Chi Minh City offers a stark look at the Vietnam War through photos and artifacts. A must-visit for history buffs, though emotionally intense. Highly recommend.
Very interesting museum, it was shocking to see all of those pictures, the information is pretty well detailed, a must when visiting Ho Chi Minh.

Recommended. Nice exposition of aircrafts, tanks, helicopters, weapons. Very illustrative museum, with plenty of information.
Good to learn about Vietnam war, but remember that war museums always shows one part of the history, the coin has always 2 sides 🤓

Typically when I visit a museum in America I'm both enlightened and uplifted - not the later case here in Vietnam😑. Horror. Dread. Empathy. Remorse? The images are too graphic to post. But if you've ever fought in war, heard stories from family members who have, or have been associated in any way with the destruction that wars cause, then well... you get the picture. As an American it's unsettling but at the same time unifying to share the experience with people from all different countries (and perspectives), including Vietnamese. Place full of history about the vietnam war, a really deep and raw insight to the country's history as well as the story of the people that lived through the war. The exhibits on the upper levels were what made the visit memorable with all the stories about the survivors of agent orange, the prisoners kept in the harsh living conditions and many more.
Must see in Saigon! Great exhibition with lots of old planes, tanks, weapons.

Exceptional collections of remnants/ memorabilia.  Gives one the true story of the war and not just the Americanized version that is so well known. Makes one appreciate the struggles of the Vietnamese and the hard won freedom.

Very informative and somber experience. Learned a lot about the US war in Vietnam. Some exhibitions and pics were sad to see and heart wrenching, but it gave context about the war and also demonstrated the resilience of the Vietnamese people. Good idea to visit if you want to learn more about the war that happened in Vietnam and the history of the country.

Wow. A fascinating and emotional experience highlighting the atrocities of the Vietnam War. Very informative and organised. There are some distressing moments. But an absolute must visit when in Ho Chi Minh.

A must-see! If there is one thing you do in Ho Chi Minh, it has to get the war remnants museum. An excellent history lesson, it's a very well equipped museum with lots of photos, relics and informative data about the war. I've read people complaining about the heat, there were air conditioning in all rooms when I've visited the place in February 2025 so I don't think this is a problem (anymore)
It was moderately crowded, I recommend more than 1 hour if you want to see it all and read some of the notes and if you go to the Orange Room it's better if you do it on an empty stomach because what's shown there is pretty hard to watch. A powerful and well-curated glimpse into Vietnam's history. The preserved war remnants tell compelling stories—both haunting and inspiring. A must-visit for anyone wanting to reflect on the past and appreciate the sacrifices that shaped the present.
Remember to go to every floor. Amazing museum, and the history tells you a lot about the suffering that the Vietnamese faced during the Vietnam War. Stop War!!

This museum is more than just a record of history — it carries the deep pain and sorrow of humanity. Once inside, I felt the preciousness of human life and the profound wounds caused by war. The museum serves as a powerful reminder that we must strive for peace in the world.

Very interesting. Very factual, not as much of the 'puppet soldiers of the Imperialist America' as there is in the Hanoi museum. Different levels explore different aspects. And some of it isn't very nice. VERY graphic photos, so i would suggest not suitable for young children.

This was well worth the money ($40k) to visit. You can use your credit card to pay separately from the ticket dispenser next to the cashier. You learn about their war history especially agent orange - very sad. Might be a little too graphic for the younger audiences.

Ticket price 40000 dong and for the little flyer 20000 dong (nice to have not necessary)

Super import museum I spend 5 hours here, start on the top floor and go down. Then it gets mentally easier ! There are books for writing down your impression-lovely idea. Personally I needed some breaks in-between to calm down.

If you want to learn about this tragic war, this is the spot! Very nice museum in the center of downtown. Outdoor and indoor exhibits with a decent shop for gifts. It shows the harsh reality of what happened in Vietnam. Difficult to see at times, but incredibly important and eye-opening. Definitely get the audio guide and take your time going through each part—it adds important context and makes the experience far more meaningful.

A Profound and Eye-Opening Experience

The War Remnants Museum is a must-visit for anyone looking to understand Vietnam’s history and the devastating impact of war. The exhibits are powerful, emotional, and deeply thought-provoking. Through photographs, artifacts, and personal stories, the museum paints a vivid picture of the Vietnam War and its consequences.

One of the most heartbreaking sections is the Agent Orange exhibit, which highlights the lasting effects of chemical warfare. The war photography gallery is also incredibly moving, showcasing the realities of war through the eyes of journalists.

This is not an easy place to visit—it’s heavy, emotional, and at times overwhelming. However, it is an important reminder of the horrors of war and the resilience of the Vietnamese people. If you are in Ho Chi Minh City, don’t miss this museum. It will leave you with a new perspective on history and humanity.

Tips:
• Allocate at least 1.5–2 hours to explore the museum fully.
• Some exhibits contain graphic images, so it may not be suitable for young children.
• Consider going early in the day to avoid crowds.

Primarily about the American atrocities from the Vietnamese side. There is always 2 sides to a story as you will see if you go to the Cu Chi tunnels. There are a few military exhibitions but primarily, it is photographs and stories.  Not expensive to go in so well worth a visit. Saw someone visibly moved to tears, so be prepared.

We just stood, watched, and cry about it in a future museum somewhere to serve as a reminder. Yet the reminder was simply just a reminder, not a lesson to learn from.

Review: Visiting the War Remnants Museum in Ho Chi Minh City was a deeply emotional and educational experience. The museum offers a vivid glimpse into the harsh realities of war, particularly the Vietnam War, and its lasting impact on the country and its people.

What a sobering but necessary experience. The War Remnants Museum holds various artifacts that highlight some of the atrocities committed against the Vietnamese people over the course of history, particularly from Americans.

This museum was difficult to get through, and at some points I actually felt nauseaous reading some of the evil things people did. Still, despite the difficulties, I think it’s an important stop on any visit to the city. I cannot wrap my head around the fortitude and also the amount of grace it takes to forgive people who committed literal war crimes against your people. If you go, I recommend starting on the top floor once you’re inside and making your way down instead of the other way around.

Very good.

Very heart breaking even if you know about this war and what was done to the Vietnamese people.

This is a place where history stands still and whispers its haunting darkest secrets to every visitor. Isn’t just a collection of artifacts—it is a deeply moving tribute to the countless lives lost forever by the Vietnam War.

From the moment you walk through its somber corridors, you’re transported into the heart-wrenching realities faced by soldiers, war reports and civilians. Each photograph, each piece of memorabilia, brings you face-to-face with the unimaginable horror borne by the people who lived through this turbulent dark period.

One of the most tear-jerking and leg trembling sections is the one on chemical warfare used by the US Army. The agent Orange devastating effects are laid bare, portraying the suffering that continues to affect generations of Vietnamese people.

Amidst all the sorrow and anger, there’s a resolute call for peace echoing throughout the museum. The Vietnam War Remnants Museum isn’t just about remembering the past—it is a powerful reminder of the importance of peace, resilience, and the human spirit's unyielding strength. As you exit, a complex blend of sadness, anger, and hope lingers, ensuring the stories you’ve encountered stay with you long after your visit ends.

Amazing big museum about the VN war with US army. How they Vietcong destroy the stupid US army, which never win a war in history 😂 poor USA 👍🏻
