전시는 3층부터 내려가면서 보면 되고 로비는 덥지만 각 전시실 안은 에어컨이 있었다.

Bảo tàng Chứng tích chiến tranh nơi cho bạn thấy những cảnh thương tâm mà chiến tranh đã gây ra cho con người khi ở nơi đây .
Giá vé dành cho học sinh sinh viên là 20000₫
Super muzeum Války ve Vietnamu. Vstup 40 VND. Technika, historie ve fotogalerii, videa. Možnost zakoupit suvenýry. Doporučuji
Buena opción si quieres conocer la historia de la Guerra del país. Muy recomendable.
Fascinating displays and an exhibition hall that tell one side of the story of the Vietnam war and before. It does get very busy and beware of the "old soldier" trying to get your attention.
The museum is evidence of what the U.S. military brought to Vietnam during the war. Visiting here gave me the feeling like that i were in the 1960s.
The museum boasts an impressive collection of exhibits. However, a word of caution: if you are particularly sensitive, you may wish to avoid it, as some displays depict graphic and violent scenes.

What an experience! We didn't know what to expect but was pleasantly surprised and moved with the elements of the museum. We were not of the generation that experienced this conflict but were moved by the enormity of it and for such a small asian country. We found many europeans or westerners as this war affected many countries, in fact several younger generations which was nice to see. The museum itself is dated but the content is relevant, fair and accurate. The flow of the museum was awkward but audio translation guides put it all together. Along with the story of the war there were several heavy war equipment on display outside as you walked in which is awesome. There was evidence of cruelty in the fog of war on display which is sad. Overall the experience was educational and emotional and at a fair price. The museum should only take a couple hours max so you can plan other things.

This is an incredibly powerful museum, there are some military hardware planes and helicopters, bombs and rifles etc, but it is mainly photographs and narrative… But none the worst for that. Truly powerful, dramatic, brutal, and tough… Overall excellent, but highly graphic and definitely not for children.

Lots of history under one roof. This is a sobering experience and there are lots of photos showing the hardships and traumas experienced by so many. The stories including children are particularly harrowing.

Visit November 2025. Sunday. Price 40,000D (1'60€ approx) Timetable: 7'30-17'30pm  It's an institution in Ho Chi Minh District 3, which displays photographs, documents, military vehicles... from the Vietnam War. It shows the impact of the war from the Vietnamese perspective. In the outer courtyard of the entrance you can see tanks, planes and a helicopter. And in the exhibition halls of the interior, there are quite 'intense' photographs, (from the consequences of Agent Orange and real war crimes) the dramatic reality of this war. The harshness of the images should be noted, as it is really impressive the climate that breathes inside by all visitors. On the day of my visit there were quite a few people, but I don't know I heard anything... Everyone silent and with a pretty broken face. (I include myself, and several times my tears broke out without being able to help it...)

It’s hard to describe this as an “enjoyable” experience, but it is absolutely a must-see when visiting Ho Chi Minh City. The War Remnants Museum presents its collection in a thoughtful and well-structured way, guiding visitors through an important and confronting part of history. Be prepared — many of the stories and photographs are harrowing. There were several moments that brought me to tears. It’s powerful, moving, and at times deeply confronting. What stands out most is that the museum is not simply about the past, but about the future. Its strong message of peace, love, and humanity shines through. Despite the heaviness of the subject matter, it ultimately leaves you reflecting on resilience and hope. A significant and important place to visit while in Ho Chi Minh City.
Bảo tàng đẹp quá trời. Em đi tham quan với bạn vào dịp Tết nên thấy bảo tàng trang trí đẹp lắm. Hôm đó có bạn nữ xinh xinh pha cho mình ly cà phê muối uống siêu ngon luôn.

Bảo tàng được bài trí khoa học, hiện vật phong phú (đặc biệt là khu vực máy bay, xe tăng bên ngoài). Những câu chuyện bên trong rất xúc động và đầy sức nặng. Một điểm đến không thể bỏ qua nếu muốn tìm hiểu về lịch sử Việt Nam tại TP.HCM."

A visit to the War Remnants Museum was deeply moving and eye-opening. The exhibits vividly portray the harsh realities of war and its lasting impact on people. It reminded me of the value of peace and the importance of understanding history to build a better future

Bảo tàng Chứng tích Chiến tranh (Quận 3, TP.HCM) không chỉ là một địa điểm tham quan, mà là một "khoảng lặng" đầy ám ảnh và suy ngẫm ngay giữa lòng thành phố náo nhiệt. Năm 2024, bảo tàng này đã lọt vào top 10 điểm đến hấp dẫn nhất châu Á trên TripAdvisor, khẳng định sức hút mãnh liệt với du khách quốc tế. Dưới đây là đánh giá chi tiết để bạn có sự chuẩn bị tốt nhất: 1. Trải nghiệm Nội dung: "Sự thật trần trụi" Đây là bảo tàng dành cho những ai muốn nhìn thẳng vào những góc khuất khốc liệt của lịch sử. * Điểm mạnh: Cách kể chuyện cực kỳ mạnh mẽ qua hình ảnh. Các chuyên đề như Hậu quả chất độc da cam hay Những sự thật lịch sử có thể khiến bất kỳ ai cũng phải lặng người. * Lưu ý: Nội dung rất nặng về cảm xúc và có nhiều hình ảnh trực diện (graphic). Nếu bạn đi cùng trẻ em hoặc là người nhạy cảm, hãy chuẩn bị tâm lý trước. * Góc nhìn: Bảo tàng thể hiện góc nhìn của người Việt về chiến tranh. Đối với khách quốc tế, đây thường là nơi họ tìm thấy những tư liệu mà sách giáo khoa phương Tây ít đề cập. 2. Không gian và Trưng bày * Ngoài trời: Trưng bày các khí tài quân sự hạng nặng như máy bay phản lực, xe tăng, trực thăng và pháo. Đây là khu vực yêu thích của những người đam mê kỹ thuật quân sự và cũng là nơi "nhẹ nhàng" nhất để bắt đầu chuyến đi. * Trong nhà: Gồm 3 tầng với hệ thống điều hòa tốt. Các gian phòng được phân chia theo chủ đề rõ ràng, từ tội ác chiến tranh đến phong trào phản chiến trên thế giới. * Điểm mới (2026): Bảo tàng đã hiện đại hóa với việc quét mã QR để nghe thuyết minh và ứng dụng công nghệ 3D/VR360, giúp việc tiếp cận thông tin trở nên chủ động và sinh động hơn thay vì chỉ đọc bảng chữ. 3. Thông tin thực tế (Cập nhật 2026) * Địa chỉ: 28 Võ Văn Tần, Phường Võ Thị Sáu, Quận 3. * Giá vé: Khoảng 40.000 VNĐ/người. Có chính sách giảm giá 50% cho học sinh, sinh viên và người cao tuổi. * Giờ mở cửa: 7:30 – 17:30 (Mở cửa xuyên suốt tất cả các ngày trong tuần, kể cả lễ Tết). * Dịch vụ mới: Hiện nay bảo tàng đã hỗ trợ đặt vé trực tuyến, giúp bạn tránh được cảnh xếp hàng dài vào những mùa cao điểm du lịch. * Thời gian: Hãy dành ít nhất 2 - 3 tiếng để có thể đọc và cảm nhận sâu. Tránh đi quá sát giờ đóng cửa. * Trang phục: Nên mặc lịch sự vì đây là nơi tôn nghiêm, tưởng niệm các nạn nhân chiến tranh. * Thứ tự tham quan: Nên đi từ tầng cao nhất xuống để theo dõi dòng chảy lịch sử một cách logic, sau đó kết thúc bằng khu vực ngoài trời và "Chuồng cọp" để thay đổi không khí. Đánh giá chung: 4.5/5 sao. Một điểm đến "phải đến" (must-visit) tại Sài Gòn để hiểu vì sao người Việt Nam lại trân trọng hòa bình đến thế.

Hôm nay mình có dịp tham quan Bảo tàng Chứng tích Chiến tranh và thật sự rất ấn tượng. Không gian bảo tàng rộng rãi, được sắp xếp theo từng chủ đề rõ ràng nên dễ theo dõi. Các hình ảnh, hiện vật và câu chuyện lịch sử được trình bày rất chân thực, mang lại nhiều cảm xúc và giúp mình hiểu hơn về quá khứ.  Điều mình thích nhất là khu trưng bày ngoài trời với các phương tiện quân sự, nhìn rất trực quan và sinh động. Tuy nhiên, một số khu bên trong khá đông khách nên đôi lúc hơi khó quan sát kỹ.  Nhìn chung, đây là một địa điểm rất đáng tham quan, đặc biệt phù hợp cho những ai muốn tìm hiểu lịch sử Việt Nam. Nếu có dịp, mình chắc chắn sẽ quay lại.
A very good experience, learning about the history of the Vietnam War and seeing the Vietnamese people fight aggression
Very good, very historical, great photos.
A big museum with lots of historical material that confronts the cruelty of the war.
Nice museum will recommend my family next time
Lots of great US military hardware, interesting to see it from the other perspective, i’ve run quite a bit about the Vietnam more, but always from the US perspective

What can one say? We have visited the World War II museum in Poland, and this museum has the same effect. It's horrendously sad, seeing the various images, watching the various videos and reading the horrifying stories. If humanity doesn't learn the lessons from War, then we have little hope. However, there are a few stories of hope and while we should not forget the horrors of war, we must live for hope, and that innocent lives were not lost without purpose.
Hay quá mọi ng ơi đáng để tham quan
Incredibly moving experience. It was hard to see what this war did to people across the countries. I am thankful to have history shared and see the impacts of war. Very emotional.
The tour guide was very helpful and knowledgeable. The experience was very insightful, especially coming from a western country without much in-depth knowledge on the war.
Impressive history from the beginning to the end of the Vietnam War 1955-75. Including atrocities, effects poison gas agent orange , prison insights, tanks and aircraft.

That was incredible Cant describe my feelings  Visiting the War Remnants Museum was an incredible and deeply emotional experience. The exhibits are powerful, honest, and sometimes difficult to see, but they help you understand the real cost of war. The photos, military equipment, and personal stories made me reflect a lot. It is not just a museum — it is a place that makes you think, feel, and appreciate peace. I strongly recommend everyone to visit it at least once.

Very confronting, but extremely important museum that I recommend everyone visit while in HCMC. The exhibits are in great detail and the guides do a fantastic job of shining light on each section of the museum
Great experience, very informative guide who has empathy for the attendees. Took all questions an answered them well and patiently

A moving experience . Normally i would be disappointed that the exhibits are in the main, press photos (apart from the aircraft and tanks ) but the photos tell a sad story . The pain and destruction that so called civilised countries can inflict on a beautiful country and people in the name of democracy is sad and thought provoking. This was clear to see from the faces of visitors looking at the horrific photos . A very good museum not to be missed and an hour is frankly is all you need.
Museum very well organized!!! Exciting experience from all points of view! To see especially in this historical period!
Amazing 100% would recommend. Bring your tissues. I can't say enough great things about my experience it was so moving.
Very good insights into the Vietnam War! Helpful if you haven't dealt with it before. Highly recommended.
It is very interesting and sad to see all that happend during the war. It brought me to tears to see the results of the war. Highly recommend the museum!
The experience is great I learned a lot on their Culture and history . I recommend this museum and I will tell this story of there culture for my family
Great experience, nice to know about the history, the audio guide is very helpful. Recommended to try this. All good...

I learned the horrors of the Vietnam War. It is not a pleasant place to visit, but I am glad I went there. It made me think a lot about the horror of the war and the impact it would have on the local people after it ended. Photographs of Sawada, a Japanese journalist, were also on display. If you've been to Ho Chi Minh City, don't miss it.
Incredible good museum, worth going to. It's informative, important and a reminder to forget what war does to a people.  Besides that, it is super cheap and the staff at the museum are super nice.

This Museum is very interesting and emotionally intense. Everything is so well documented and explained. I took the audioguide in french and it really worthed it. If you are an emotional person, get prepared before your visit. I didn't how much horror the people of Vietnam has endured during these long wars and about the genocide. This Museum is a must do for the memory of Vietnam History and to never forget about war atrocities...
Vietnam War in all its horror!! The tears just came... Strong pictures and you leave the museum full of impressions of the atrocities of war!
Today we visited this museum and we have to say that certain images and certain stories were very strong.The aid of audio gu

The stories and images captured in this museum give an unparalleled, raw glimpse into the war against and genocide of the Vietnamese people. It is a must do if you choose to visit this country and especially if you also plan to visit any of the war related tourist attractions, so that you can understand the painful history of this beautiful place.

I saw American troops invade Vietnam that year. The crime was horrifying, the same atrocities as Japan's aggression against China, cruel and appalling. Someone has been elected to parliament on the basis of war merit. This is the greatest travesty of humanity. I hope the Chinese and Vietnamese generations are friendly.

If you skip this museum while you are in Vietnam as a tourist then you do not deserve to be in Vietnam. What has been done to the lovely people of Vietnam is too horrific. This museum gives you the facts of what happened. And that's something that really needs to be told when you get home. Come to this museum!
An amazing and confronting exhibition of the atrocities the Vietnamese people suffered. Very informative and very confronting. Everyone should see this to ensure it never happens again

The museum is not just a place to display weapons, but a place that tells human stories and the aspiration for peace. I spent the entire morning here and still wanted to see more. Thank you to the museum management for preserving and presenting history in such a humane, sophisticated, and compelling way. A wonderful educational destination for all generations.

This site is a non-negotiable when visiting HCMC (Saigon). This was my second visit to HCMC and my second visit to the War Remnants Museum. At $40,000 VND, it is very affordable and shouldn’t be missed. The displays are a testament to the resilience of the Vietnamese people and give an insight in to the country, like no other site I’ve visited. Give yourself a minimum 3 hours and you will be educated, moved, shocked, troubled, saddened and enlightened.

Although you see the brutal reality of the war, it’s very informative and a must visit to properly understand the history of Vietnam. It’s very difficult to look at some of the images but equally very important. It really shows how amazingly Vietnam has recovered.

30年ぶりに訪れてみました。外国人は40,000ドン約240円 今では日本語音声ガイド80,000ドン約480円もありました。 せっかくなので音声ガイドも付けて見学しました。 クレジットカードも使えます。  正直な感想は今時の他の博物館に比べると昭和的な展示で30年前とあまり変わっていないと思いました。 楽しい博物館ではありませんが現在の平和で発展したベトナムには忘れてはならない悲しい過去がある事を思い出させられました。  展示室も扇風機はありますが外国人にはエアコンの効きが弱く展示室外はオープンエアーでかなり暑く見学に疲れます。  なお館内の自販機でお水も買えますがコンビニの3倍位の価格です。

Auf dem Rückweg ins Hotel besuchten wir dieses Museum, wo wir unseren Roller für ein kleines Entgelt parken konnten. Drinnen wurde der Vietnamkrieg und seine Gräueltaten eindrucksvoll dargestellt. Die Ausstellung war sehr informativ und bot einen tiefen Einblick in die Geschichte. Zudem gab es Kriegsgeräte zu sehen, was den Besuch noch beeindruckender machte. Im angeschlossenen Café konnte man einen Kaffee trinken, wobei es eher klein und etwas heruntergekommen wirkte – ein Besuch ist also nicht zwingend erforderlich.

It is a very worthwhile stop in Ho Chi Minh City. The museum presents the war strongly from the Vietnamese viewpoint, which can be jarring for American visitors. The focus is mainly on the impact of U.S. actions, with limited detail on the tactics or harsh realities of the Viet Cong, so the historical picture isn’t fully rounded — but it’s still an important perspective to see.  The exhibits are clear, informative, and thought-provoking, and the museum is a key part of understanding how the conflict is remembered in Vietnam. Plan for about an hour.

We visited the War Remnants Museum along with the Cu Chi Tunnels when we visited Ho Chi Min City. This museum offers a perspective about why they refer to this was as the American War of Aggression. The museum offered historical narratives about topics such as the use of agent orange, the slaughter of Vietnamese people, etc. In my opinion, what is missing is any perspective that includes information and relics related to the treatment of Americans by the Viet Cong.

This museum provides a good insight into French and USA intervention in Vietnam (mainly USA). As a UK citizen these wars were certainly not covered in the curriculum in the 70s so my understanding and knowledge was very limited. All displays have sections in English and I must acknowledge that at times I was moved to tears. The museum was developed to educate younger generations. Unfortunately with all the conflicts currently in the world, one wonders what we have actually learned. I would certainly recommend a visit as what you see makes you reflect.

Museum was a good experience but smaller than I was expecting. Eye opening experience if you’re not very well educated on the struggles of the Vietnam war. Hollywood really does paint a different picture about the United States involvement.

Very Busy Place with so much to see, read, learn and take amazing photos. It felt surreal looking and feeling real life history. This remarkable place is wheelchair accessible but it is hard to get the wheelchair through the crowds. Especially when you want to see anything up close. My Mum 74 was the only wheelchair user when we visited. We didn’t stay too long due to limited wheelchair access. However it was so worth seeing and recommend it to anyone to visit. The staff were helpful and friendly with mum. We left happy and grateful for the photos and videos we took.

We got off the Hop on hop off Bus right at the Gate where there is a Booth selling Tickets . The Tickets were 40000 Dong (AUD$ 2-40) It was very Busy with lots of people . We decided to have a look at the Various Tanks and other Armoured Vehicles and a number of Aircraft & American Fighter Jets . Very interesting . Then the Rain started and we made a dash for the Main Building . It is possible to spend a whole Day in here , This Museum makes a very Sombre Statement . It gives a very moving insight into the American War as well as the French occupation of Vietnam . All visitors to Saigon should come here to see for them selves . A down side to the Museum is the lack of Aircon & also the Stairs to the Upper floors are a Killer . We are both in our 70s , so we didn't go higher than the 2nd Floor . We were probably here for around 2 hours .

Extremely interesting museum, a must do when in Ho Chi Minh. Airplanes, tanks, items, the reconstruction of inmate conditions and means of torture, plus a photography exhibition with gorgeous pictures. Terrible historical events, great documentation. Exhibition on 3 floors, could see no lift. On the ground floor: lockers, a cafè and two small souvenir shops. No air conditioning (otherwise my visit would have taken even longer!). Plan about 2 hours, for photography enthusiasts 2,5-3 hours. Cost: for an adult 40.000 VND, with audioguide 120.000 VND.

We visited the museum by ourselves.  We hired the audio tour ( English ) which we think is a must if you want to truly enjoy and understand the tour.  The museum do not have air conditioning, they only have fan , so be prepared it will be very hot .  The museum provided lots of graphic photos , it is very sad to see them but I think it is very important to look at the perspective of the Vietnamese ‘ people.  Overall I would recommend this museum

The War Remnants Museum offers a mix of documentaries, memorials, and artwork. We found it interesting but a bit heavy. The exhibits are impactful, yet the atmosphere felt a bit overwhelming at times. It’s worth a visit for a glimpse into history, but don’t expect a light experience.

This museum exists partly to remind the world how awful the Vietnam/American war was, and the awful suffering of the Vietnamese. However, its way to graphic for a family museum, pictures of people dying and their stories alongside the various tortures inflicted on them, needs an 18 rating.  This is powerful stuff – and does make you wonder what on earth America was doing in this war.

Solid museum - 3 floors with themed exhibits, in addition to a number of American military vehicles and Tiger Cage exhibit outside. Audio guide is extensive and for an extra 80,000 VND - worth it. Good bathrooms. Elevator available for mobility issues. Unfortunately at the moment their aircon is out and, despite many fans, it's sweltering. Take water.

Informative and interesting museum. An eye opening experience and also very sad & humbling.  Pros -The aircraft & vehicles were fantastically maintained and looked great close up -The entry price is cheap -educational and lots of different language options  Cons -there was no obvious route to walk/get around the museum and we found ourselves wandering to find where to go -there were people inside the museum begging/asking for money -not a lot of interactive/submersive parts (a lot of reading & photos)

The War museum showcased the history of the Vietnam war in the 60’s with lots of graphic photos of the Vietnamese people killed. It’s good to read up first about the war before visiting the museum so we can be better informed. We visited in early July and ground floor exhibit area has no air conditioning. The second and third floor though has very weak air conditioning supported by fan but it is still very stuffy. Hard to remain too long inside the exhibit rooms.

Spent about an hour or so here giving a very good insight and pretty graphic tour for those who have heart strings and a weak stomach at some of the images that are shown here from the U.S AND VIETNAM conflict in the vietnam war. Great to see some of the artillery and machines on offer here especially the chinook up close and some of the fighter pilot planes. And fascinating and fair amount of info with the torture systems they had and with some again grapical images being showcased and that goes to say with the inside of the tour a dedicated showroom on the affects of agent orange with images and information which clearly shows the damage it has done and did in that era. Showrooms full of originsl images guns bullets ect scattered around the place and very well detailed. Again it was about 10am when i went through and it was easily couple hundred plus people in here to bump shoulders with when looking at certain exhibits. Pictures dont do it justice ive added to page and it would be abit over the top of i was to show photos of the agent orange affects and deaths you will see

Its interesting to have a walk through and see the war from the Vietnamese side. The only problem is of course the propaganda and their white washing of their own sides behaviour during the war. Some interesting pieces of military equipment outside the building. Their is also no acknowledgment that Vietnam has since fought wars with China and Cambodia.

Посетить думаю стоит. Билет стоит 40 тыс. донг. Музей не большой. На всё уйдет 2 часа максимум. Есть экземпляры техники, оружия. Но наверное 70% экспонатов это фотографии. Всё на вьетнамском и английском.
ベトナムのフランス統治からベトナム戦争までの歴史を学ぶことができます。ベトナム戦争関連の展示は枯れ葉剤やナパーム弾の被害状況が克明に示されており、残酷な写真などが展示されているので、苦手な方は注意ください。平和のありがたさを感じるとともに、人間の愚かさを感じざるを得ません。

Wat kun je anders verwachten in een oorlogs-herdenkingsmuseum dan oorlogstuig en aanverwant materiaal? De vraag stellen is ze beantwoorden. Op het voorplein staan tanks en pantservoertuigen, helikopters en straaljagers uit de Vietnam oorlog. Daatbij een verkennertank M41 ‘Walker buldog’ waarin ik 1969 mijn militaire opleiding tankkomandant kreeg. Ik kan de omstaanders vertellen dat we met vieren in dat kleine ding zaten. In een hoek vindt je een verzameling van alle soorten en maten bommen die met duizenden over Vietnam werden uitgestrooid. Binnen, een uitgebreide verzameling schiettuig. Revolvers, pistolen, geweren, mitrailleurs van klein tot groot kaliber. Uiteraard krijg je ook minder prettige dingen te zien. Foto’s van begane wreedheden, misvormde mensen na het gebruik van Agent Orange – het chemische ontbladeringsproduct- enz.

Ein "must see" in Ho Chin Minh. Der Eintritt kostet 40.000 Dong. Der Vietnamkrieg und die Folgen werden anschaulich über mehrere Etagen durch Fotografien dargestellt. Es werden auch Bilder zu den Auswirkungen von Agent Orange und Napalm ausgestellt - nichts für schwere Nerven. Im Hof stehen noch Militärfahrzeuge.

A history that we all should know, the museum outlines the French and American attempts to dominate and take over the country. Many atrocities committed against the Vietnamese are documented. Perhaps others may not have been documented. Very interesting visit for historians and Perhaps those who were too young to learn of "Vietnam "

Self guided look at the museum. Cheap to get in. A lot of things to see . You need to be prepared to read a lot of the background stories on the photo displays. For an additional cost you can have an audio guide that steps you through a self guided tour. Lots of guns, tanks, aircraft etc. you need maybe 2- 4 hours to get around. Nice cafe there for a drink and something to eat.

Came here in the afternoon with a group, museum was quite busy but manageable. Harrowing exhibitions on the Vietnam war and related items. Exhibitions were brutal and too the point, with most stories told through pictures, with most information available in English. Very much worth a visit to learn about what happened and gain a deeper understanding, however the contents are not pleasant so consider carefully if you do want to see this. We were there 2 hours and although it wasn't enough time to read all contents in detail, we felt we had enough.

Interesting museum, I think it was 40,000 VND entry fee, outside there are helicopters, tanks etc. inside there are several floors, I did find some of the photographs quite distressing, but that is war. I would recommend visiting to give insight into the horror of the war, you could spend several hours, we just spent around an hour.

Veldig fint museum. De viste flere stadier av krigen. Alt fra hvordan de lokale ble henrettet til mer massakre av hele byer med bomber av napalm og lignende, til hvordan de jobbet diplomatisk for å avslutte krigen. De viste flere videoer og bilder av lokale folk som gjorde sterkt inntrykk.  Flere tanks, fly og våpen var utstilt.  Egen utstilling for krigsreportere og deres bilder var også veldig fint - samt at man så hvorfor denne krigen ble så upopulær.  Man burde starte i øverste etasje og vandre nedover.

The Vietnamese are very proud of their military history, in around twenty years they have beaten the second largest colonial empire (France) and the strongest superpower (the US). Yes, this memorial, isn’t an objective explanation of the war, it is a celebration of the victory obtained with hard fights and widespread sufferance. Is it worth it? Yes, because for us, born in the West, it is a different way to approach something that we all know, mostly thanks to another kind of propaganda: Hollywood. Outside there is an exposition of the material that the Americans left behind: tanks (M48 and M24), self-propelled artillery and towed artillery, helicopters (Ch-7 Chinook and Bell UH-1), airplanes (A1 Skyraider and A37 Dragonfly). Inside the building, there are different expositions with weapons, objects and many pictures.

A tough museum with the photos really telling the story of a tragic time. Museum is well laid out on multiple levels, although heaps of text for reading, the photos really do tell the story. Unfortunately no airconditioning so very hot even with fans and was very busy.

Definitely worth a visit but difficult to say it’s enjoyable given the topic and the graphic descriptions of the horrors of war. Having said that, we recommend a visit. Especially good was the exhibition of photographs by war photographers from all nations, who were heroes in their own right. Interesting displays of aircraft, artillery and weapons also.

Pros : A chance to learn about war and its consequences. Highlights the atrocities of war. Not really a place for the faint hearted. It has many disturbing pictures which can be disheartening. Has a simple cafe for a quick drink. A place that is very well worth the visit.  Cons: Poorly ventilated. The rooms did not have proper fans and some didn’t even have working air conds. It was terribly hot and stuffy in some of the rooms/sections.  It spans a few floors but without any working lifts or escalators. Not exactly wheelchair friendly.

£1.10 each entry, image if this was in the UK!! Really interesting but there's a lot of reading if you want it all. Drives home how the people in their ivory towers send the common man to do their evil work for money. I have read reviews saying it was biased but I'm not sure. There are lots of photos that captured the horrors of the war but the photos were not taken by the Viet Con so it will appear biased. What it does do is tell the story of David and Goliath. They are refurbishing the vehicles all at the same time so it sort of spoils photo opportunities because there polythene and masking tape on them.

The aircraft and tanks in the yard outside are amazing. The displays inside are mainly photo based with captions. The museum could benefit from more objects, particularly with the torture section outside. Overall, the museum would do well to provide more information to explain the context that South Vietnam requested help from the United States and received funding and support to fight against the North Vietnam Vietcong communists. They seem to forget that this was a civil war in which the US supported the South Vietnamese.

This was on our itinerary on our visit to Ho Chi Minh City. Entry was quick and cheap 70,000VND also bought a tour booklet. There is plenty of remnants in the main outside area, chinook and Huey helicopters and Artillery pieces and various weapons. The museum itself is set over a couple of floors starting from the build up and the arrival of US troops, also lots of pictures from the maasacre of My Lai and the involvement of aircraft during Operation Linebqcker. The use of chemicals during Operation Ranch hand are graphic and what Agent Orange did was horrendous. It is interesting to walk around, but take your time, you will need a few hours to see all the pictures and to visit all the different sections of the museum.

This is a very confronting museum with some very strong images and displays. It's all from the perspective of the victors, as you would expect. The atrocities were horrific and the images don't hold back on the impact of the war on the Vietnamese people. Worth having a look but be prepared for haunting images and displays.

OK, this is from one perspective the winners who always write history. Having said that, 3 million dead does make you think and the photos were very chastening.  We learned a lot from the winners' perspective, but there were many attrocities committed in the name of freedom for sure. It does make you wonder why certain governments feel the need to start wars in the first place, the main lovers are the ordinary people.

The last time I visited here was in 2015.  This museum is just a reminder that "certain country" with superior machinery or dirty fighting may not be necessary to defeat the will of the people.  I kinda like the displays around the museum as it is like a show of spoils of war.

The exhibition was truly heartbreaking, offering a valuable opportunity to hear the Vietnamese perspective on the war, which starkly contrasts with the Hollywood portrayal we've grown up with. The display featured exceptional war photography, though regrettably, the information presentation caused congestion. Learning about the brutality of the US forces and the devastating effects of Agent Orange was distressing. Nevertheless, investing time here is undoubtedly worthwhile for a deeper and more nuanced understanding of the conflict.

Viaggio attraverso un’altra vergogna degli esseri umani: la guerra in Vietnam. Oltre ad esserci i mezzi militari, ci sono anche le gabbie dei prigionieri, le bombe e tantissime foto. Un viaggio impressionante ma da fare per capirne di piu della storia. Anche se, se devo essere sincera, mai capirò la necessità di questi massacri.

I would have given this attraction 5 stars but there was a lack of air con. I was there as soon as it opened and it was still crazy hot. I cant imagine what it would have been like a few hours later. disappointing. But otherwise its great. I recommend the audio guide because otherwise you will be reading for 2 hours. There is information overload. Its a great place to go as a solo traveler.

The museum was very graphic but great in content. The souvenirs sold here are the same everywhere else. No need to preorder tickets as queues aren't a problem. There are issues with toilet cleanliness and for a popular museum this one is on the deteriorated side compared to the rest in Saigon. Must do in Saigon

The place isn't huge, but it's poignant. It documents the autrocities, massacres, effacts of agent orange, and the savageness of the war of American invasion. I would have liked more history and background as I felt I didn't get a grasp on why it even happened.

The Vietnam War. The American War. Nobody won but the Americans got kicked out. The photography is factual. The vast stack of unexploded ordnance is a byproduct of USA dropping a vast tonnage of bombs….on jungle. The jets were left behind. …….. Then you learn that Agent Orange is a Dioxin that causes birth defects and lingers. Interestingly, the French colonists that preceded the Americans were really nasty.  On a practical note. It’s not air conditioned. It can be quite heavy going.

This was an important stop in our visit to Ho Chi Minh (Saigon). We had to come here.  Whilst the rhetoric in places is a little off, it is impossible to ignore the damage inflicted by the War(s) on the people of Vietnam.  Serious note of caution - There are exhibitions not suitable for children 13 years or under, for example the photography exhibit is world class but there are images that disturb adults and are *not* appropriate for children.  Our visit was about 90mins or so.  Top tip - start at the very top, room 1 and work your way down the building

Interessant å få et innblikk i Vietnamkrigen sett med vietnamesisk blikk. Tenåringen syntes det var spennende å se tanks, fly og annet amerikansk militærutstyr utstilt. Utstillingen med fotografier fra krigskorrespondentene var det som fenget mest hos oss voksne. Sterke bilder, sterke historier og ikke for sarte sjeler.

Don't bring your young children here. It was very graphic with many photos, videos and audio of those who were impacted during the war. It is very confronting, so do be prepared!  The layout of the museum was well done. There were spaces to take a seat if needed and also a cafe. There was a lot to see and be educated on. It was a very informative visit. My partner and I didn't do it through a tour. We just went by ourselves and visited for half an hour. We believed that was enough ! Good price for entry, too

You can get your admission tickets at the gate. We went early and by the time we left it was getting quiet busy and crowded. We decided to get an audio tour which wasn’t expensive. There are approx 6 rooms that you enter with various information about the war and timelines of the battle. We found the audio guide a good interactive information without needing a tour guide. Very easy to use with numbers reflecting the information displayed. Some areas quiet confronting with graphic images however all very educational.

If you're traveling to Ho Chi Minh City, make sure to visit the War Remnants Museum. This museum showcases exhibits and photographs related to the Vietnam War, including military equipment and personal artifacts. There's also a moving section dedicated to the victims of war, as well as an exhibit highlighting the work of journalists who covered the conflict. Although some of the exhibits can be difficult to view due to their graphic nature, this museum is an essential stop if you're interested in learning about the history of Vietnam and the impact of war on its people.
An important and compelling collection, but remember that it only tells one side’s story

The museum displays an important number of pictures, illustrating the horrors of the war. The exhibition and the pictures are moving. However, the museum lacked an organisation to have a comprehensible message. You will not get objective information about the war and the event that occurred during this awful period.

War Remnants Museum is a must visit place in Saigon. It takes around 2-3 hours to cover this museum. Very informative for everyone. Our daughter is 13 yr old and had recently studied about the war and could relate to the exhibits. We can also take an audio tour of this place spread across the three floors.

A really well laid out museum, so much to see. My family were really interested in war history and we spent quite a long time here. There were bats in the outside jail exhibit part which was freaky. The agent orange room I found very hard and couldnt stay there. I would not take young children there, but it is important to have this displayed to show what humans are capable of. Both my sons had a Vietnamese student approach them and talk away for a good 15 - 30 minutes. They were interested in what country we were from and had a lot of questions, both my sons enjoyed the interaction and asked many questions too. Has a cafe located near the side entrance for water, coffee and seating in the shade. It was easy to locate on google maps and plan our route from the hotel.

Well worth a couple of hours visit to truly appreciate what is on display. There are vehicles and munitions but there are very interesting galleries on three floors inside the main building. There are some harrowing images, but then again, it's showing the results of the conflict, so do be prepared.

Very good museum to visit. Can be quite disturbing at points when you learn about agent orange and the terrible effects it has had. I mean no part of war is enjoyable but very big museum covering different elements of the war

This is a very sobering museum. You know, of course, that it will be fairly gruesome and there is no shortage of information and pictures on the horror that was the Vietnam War. It serves as a great reminder of those now far-off days. The museum is well laid out with lots of information available

Very impressive museum, the pictures might not be suitable for children because dead bodies are shown during the whole exposition. But the museum tells a story that you just have to know if you visiting Vietnam. It was informative but horrific at the same time.

This museum provides an insight to the impact of the war. It houses a wide collection of photographs of the period of the Vietnam war and the aftermath. They also have some of the war remnants including a chopper, tanker on display.

my friend and I walked to the war remnants museum . I am glad I toured the museum and see the effects of war and hoped to never ever be at one . It was worth the visit . I recommend highly a visit when one visits Ho Chih Ming :-)

A very interesting place depicting the struggles of the Vietnamese people against a powerful enemy , to stay united. It also depicts the horrors of war . When a war happens it is the innocent common people who get hurt the most. You leave the Museum with a sad heart wondering why ever peoples fight at all, when they know about the horrors of a war.

This is a must see, albeit a very tough one. It was a sobering experience but I learnt a lot and came away more knowledgeable and horrified about the Vietnam war. I have not given it the full 5* as the story was patchy in places but I absolutely recommend spending 2 hours here to educate yourself about the history of the country you are visiting.

This is a museum dedicated to the entire Vietnam independence / french war / US war era. It has a series of AC rooms that provide great detail into the different parts of the conflict. Americans and French will at times find the Vietnamese perspective hard to take, but I think it is important to hear their voice.  The hardest part is the section on the effects of Agent Orange. This is an over 12 years old place.  The over all message, "war what is it good for...absolutely nothing."  The outside area has a series of airplanes, and a recreation of a prison.

Such a tragic event in history. The people of Vietnam are so strong!! and forgiving... There are some horrific photos, glad they are shown, as it occurred. Reflection on human kind and how vicious we can be to one another.  There is an audio tour guide you can purchase when you enter (headset). I did not see this until the end. I think it would be worth having while going through the museum.

Although we aren’t huge history buffs, this was still worth a visit. Cool to see all the old army planes and tanks etc. Inside does have air con but was still hot, because of this we didn’t stay as long as we thought we might have.

Took my family, including 2 kids aged 8 and 10. My 8 year old chose to leave some of the exhibits as they are graphic at times and they were upsetting to look at, but later we reflected that it did actually happen and she wanted to know more, so we tried to tell her what happened. 10 year old chose to stay. Whatever you think about it, it was humbling to see what happened to the Vietnamese people not so long ago. I would like to say we might learn from this but I’m not sure we are. My hope is my kids will.

I found it very interesting, with a lot of photos and details. Excellent. They were certain parts that they were very moving.... and emotional, specially the one with the orange Agent. Museography could be a little bit more modern and interactive. Overall a great visit, and everyone should go to understand the recent history of the country.

This place has multiple floors of materials to read, look at and immerse yourself in. The photos are pretty intense as it shows images taken by journalists during the war and some can be pretty gruesome. We had an hour to allocate there and it definitely wasn’t enough if you looked at everything. You’ll have mixed feelings but it’s worth it to see what the people of Vietnam went through.

Very hard hitting museum about the human cost of the war. A lot about American and colonial atrocities and worldwide opposition to American involvement. Nothing about VC and PAV actions against civilians or the Hanoi Hilton, but this is victor’s history.

Was worth the visit to gain a perspective on the war and its long term impact on many people. Quite confronting to read about the torture methods and the chemical warfare used. You will likely feel a little sick in the stomach after seeing many of the ‘displays’

This is an absorbing museum but not an easy experience. It is very educational and will reveal things about this war you were unaware of. A warning that the photographs of war atrocities and victims of Agent Orange are extremely graphic and deeply shocking. This should be a must-see visit to anyone coming to ho chi minh city.

Went here with a tour group I'm travelling Vietnam with. We spent just over an hour here, but I reckon I could have spent longer. Whilst I have some knowledge of the Vietnam War, there were some aspects I knew little about - particularly the extent and impact of Agent Orange.

I visited there just an hour before it closed and I don’t have enough time to see all. There are a lot of terrible pictures of the war, which we must not forget in the future. It reminds me that we must not repeat wars and must abolish them from the world.

The entrance fee is a mere 40,000 VND. The Museum is well laid out with several rooms on the ground floor and upstairs. The highlights for me were the room dedicated to showing US opposition to the Vietnam War and the room dedicated to the victims of Agent Orange. Some of the photos are gruesome, so beware of bringing young children along. The standard of English, and of the photos on display, is high. Outside the Museum are variety of tanks and warplanes.

One of the first things I saw was a huge picture on the side of the building. A photo that was used time and again in the UK newspapers to express the devastating consequences of war. Some may remember the picture of a very young, and bewildered, Vietnamese child standing in the mud in the middle of a forest that had been blown apart by bombs. The museum displays military equipment, photos etc. But I will remember the photo of the child.

Good amount of gear right here you are able to get up close with and next 5 6 distinct themed exhibits, every one of which had been fascinating. Exhibits are plainly slanted to the Vietnamese side (South Vietnamese jails, negative effects of Agent Orange, etc) but that's beneficial to find out. The structure is a tad tired but since the museum may be performed in ninety minutes, it is not an issue.

Lots of information about the Vietnam war and get to see the master pieces used in wars. One can spend hours and hours of time gathering all the information displayed in the museum. I would definitely recommend to pay a visit here.

The Vietnam war was a terrible time in Vietnam's history and America's. This museum does not hold it's punches. You start on the 1st floor seeing the effects of Agent Orange (which is still affecting 3rd generation Vietnamese and Americans), you also see/read about what the Americans did and how they acted during this war. It is hard to see. Then you go downstairs and see/read about the Americans who protested against the war in many different ways and then how the two countries worked towards reconciliation. It is a horrible business but you can not ignore what has happened - hopefully we learn from the past but sometimes I am not so sure we do :(

However this museum was very one sided, you could still learn a lot about the Vietnamese War. Especially the horrible heritage from "Agent Orange" was new for us Europeans. Nice detail: they play "Imagine" from Johnn Lennon over and over in the main hall.

Decent amount of equipment here that you can get up close with and then 5-6 different themed exhibits, all of which were interesting. Exhibits are clearly slanted to Vietnamese side (South Vietnamese jails, effects of Agent Orange etc) but that is helpful to see. The building is a bit tired but since the museum can be done in 90 minutes, it's not a problem.

War Remnants Museum is a must see attraction while you are in Saigon. The military equipment outside was amazing to see up close, and the third floor was the best. The only disappointing thing for me was the amount of time we were given to view the museum, as the G Adventures tour was keeping to a tight schedule. However allocating 70 minutes for this attraction is not enough, as you need two hours to really absorb the artifacts and stories behind this tragic war that started with communism paranoia. Although the US retreated out of Vietnam, no one truly wins a war.

The museum takes on a heavy bias towards U.S. atrocities and horrible effects the war brought on the Vietnamese people during the war.  Artifacts, pictures, stories, news articles are very abundant throughout the museum. Expect about 2 hours to view everything and 3 to 4 hours if you read all the inscriptions.  The agent orange exhibition is very sad and disheartening.

The museum gives an overview of the US weapons used during the Vietnam war - tanks, planes, helicopters, artillery, ... . Inside a large part is dedicated to the effects of Agent Orange on the civil population.

We aren’t really museum people, but this is well worth a visit for the photographic history of the Vietnam War. A very enlightening experience, particularly highlighting the brutally of the war and the ongoing suffering inflicted by Agent Orange. Certainly worth a couple of hours

This museum was very informative and one can learn a lot about the effects of Agent Orange during and after the VN war. Agent Orange has left generations of civilians severely deformed. Just when you thought the war was over, there would be pre-exploded bombs that one would accidentally step on. Lots to learn but also lots of tears to shed because of the graphic pictures in this wonderful museum.

We were on a tour so only had a limited amok of time to go through the museum. You really need a solid three hours to do it justice. The government has done a great job putting together the many stories associated with the Vietnam war. It’s heart wrenching at times but a great place, particularly for parents to take children to show them how senseless war is for everyone.

Great museum to understand the effects of Vietnam War on the ordinary Vietnamese who were caught up in the fighting. Particularly interesting and disturbing were the replica of “tiger cages” and the photographs of victims of Agent Orange.  Wish the whole museum is air-conditioned. The hot weather made the visit rather energy sapping.

You must visit this museum when you are in Ho Chi Minh. The museum illustrates the atrocity of the Vietnam war and the terrible suffering of the Vietnamese people. You can also see a wide range of US war bombs and war artillery.

This museum brings to life the Vietnamese story of the lives lost to the war. They made their point that in a war, nobody really wins. It shows exhibits of journalists who lost their lives, photos of atrocities and it leaves you feeling bewildered. Funny that it has a sombre recording of John Lennon's "Imagine". Worth seeing.

This gives you a great insight into the horror of war. Lots of information, some quite gruesome images taken by war correspondents of the day. We should never forget the sacrifices both sides have made. Well worth a visit to see some of the equipment used, tanks, helicopters, planes, bombs including a mock prison cell.

Visited this a Museum today and experienced the message behind the Vietnam War and the invasion and impact of the USA during the conflict. There are excellent exhibits outside and inside in good condition. There are confronting issues regarding any war and graphic images cement the reasons on not going to war.
I grew up only hearing one side of the Vietnam war and this museum gives a view from the other side. Well presented and at times chilling. Well worth a visit if you are in Ho Chi Minh City.

Worth a visit. This was a really eye opening and interesting place to visit. And really hits home how recent the events took place. I would allow around 2 hours in this place to read through the information as well as looking at the content.

This museum in the heart of HoChiMinh city is a must-see for anyone interested in the history of the Vietnam War and it's devastating effect on the Vietnamese people. It is well laid out, easy to navigate, and the exhibits are clearly marked in English so it was a very informative trip. A sobering and thought-provoking experience, no doubt.

History is always better to understand if you see it through the eyes of both sides. This is a must. I now know I have a more balanced view of the Vietnam war after visiting the Museum. Parents with little children can still attend as they have a child minding facility. That is good thinking.

This place is good in cold or cloudy days as most of stuff is outside and hard to enjoy on hot days. Kids love to look around those old tanks, planes and helicopters. It is in district 1 so you can just walk around area and visit many tourist places and this one is easy to find as it's outdoor.

Very cheap entry compared to Western establishments. Found it interesting especially as can just about remember the war being constantly on the TV news etc.  Not of the standard of say UK museums , a little repetitive and obviously biased against the French and Americans. But worthwhile visiting . Some good exhibits of ex US weaponry etc.  Exhibition of war reporters/ photographers was particularly good.

This museum was like going b back to my childhood as I am old enough to remember this all happening. The photographs, especially the torture ones brought years to my eyes. I know it happened to both sides but seeing it for real was unbearable at times. Sad but must see it and all the other photos and ammunition in the yard.

This museum is unlike many. There are not many exhibits - but rather it tells a story. The story of the American / Vietnam war. It’s heartbreaking in places but is well designed and really has an impact. It doesn’t matter what ‘side’ you think should have won this war - the impact on local people is the human story that is being told - and it’s told incredibly well.

Very cheap for admission. A really good informative museum with 3 floors of interesting videos, pictures and memorabelia. Just a word of warning though - for those of a nervous disposition be aware of the Agent Orange room. The outside area is packed with American military hardware and a fantastic mock up of a prison cell.

If you have ever been to Berlin and seen the commitment to remembering the war this is in a similar vein although from the perspective of those basically invaded. It’s a moving experience, there is a lot of reading but it doesn’t bother you and some of the elements around agent orange are particularly hard hitting. A terrible time in Vietnam history and one that it is important to remember.

You can make a line to buy and check ticket for entry. You should take some photos out side and go to the second floor to watch the historical by photo under French time, American time. on the first floor is talk about the Agent Orange and Missing of local people during the war. On the ground floor is some memory activity depend on "this day that year" or you can find some souvenirs for your friends.

Get's the point across - causes you to re-think what you've learned (or didn't learn). Walk away less likely to quickly judge others (other nations). It's small enough you can see it in an hour but leave time to read and reflect for the maximum impact. A clear statement of the sinfulness of mankind and how it's manifested at times in very evil ways. This race needs a the Savior.

A great museum at District 3 HCMC. I learned a lot throughout my visit. Some of the room looks new and all the room maintained so well. However, the staff on the gate were kinda rude to us. Maybe they should spoke in a more polite manner to their customer.

You learn a lot, and feel a lot… A MUST when in Vietnam, and especially in Ho Chi Minh City; however, I think the Museum could renovate itself to be a little bit more interactive.

Price is 40k VND
A must visit when going to Ho Chi Minh.  A lot of history, explanations and descriptions showing other countries contributions on what was going on during the Vietnam War.

Very sad and confronting.  We really took our time to look at everything.  The more and more we read it was a little disheartening.  War is never a great thing but unfortunately this museum is very one sided. They don't tell the whole truth. In one way I understand why they say USA invaded but for the South this was not true. The USA and Australians and other countries came to help the South but this is never mentioned. Even speaking to locals about this they agree and a very passionate about how one sided it is. I still recommend this place highly but have your own view on this. Apart from that, the photos are always etched in my mind.

Visiting war remnants museum in ho chi Minh City was informative as well as emotional experience. We saw the what war could do to the people. How tough and resilient local people were against americans.
Very graphic pictures of the horrors of the war.  It was a very heavy experience.  My wife was unable to view some of the areas due to the graphic nature of the pictures and sounds.

There is lots of pictures to look and weapon plus ammunition the one thing this place needs or that others usually have is a Thanks section where there give thanks or recognise the help form other countries eg ANZAC they only show enemies.

The War Remnants Museum offers a very interesting and well-curated collection of exhibits. The descriptions are well-written and available in English, providing valuable historical context.

The entrance fee (40,000 VND as of January 2025) is very reasonable for the experience. However, be aware that the section on war crimes is extremely graphic. It’s important to decide in advance whether this is suitable for you, especially since children have unrestricted access.

If you’re visiting with kids and want to avoid the more disturbing content, there is a costume and art area where they can engage in activities instead.

Overall, a highly informative but emotionally intense museum—definitely worth a visit!

It was exceptionally busy as we happened to go there on Independence day. It showcases an incredible amount of information. You do need to be cautious if you are sensitive to explicit scenes of suffering.

The outside artillery and planes are really nice to see. Not for children inside that's for sure..  lots of very graphic images. Worth the entry fee definitely. Only takes about 30 to 40 mins to walk through all floors looking at but not reading everything.

The Americans really tortured and killed millions of innocent Vietnamese.
I admire the spirit of the Vietnamese,they fought USA for 25 years. USA had to accept the Vietnamese peoples will and leave.
Using Agent Orange and Napalm bombs was disgraceful.

The War Remnants Museum in Ho Chi Minh City is an eye-opening and deeply moving experience that sheds light on the brutal realities of war, particularly the Vietnam War. Spanning multiple levels, the museum takes visitors on a chronological journey through the war’s causes, consequences, and aftermath.

Each level of the museum is thoughtfully curated with photographs, artifacts, and detailed explanations that provide a sobering perspective on the conflict. Some exhibits feature graphic images and accounts of war atrocities, so viewer discretion is strongly advised. These powerful displays, while challenging to process, underscore the human cost of war and the resilience of the Vietnamese people.

The outdoor area houses military vehicles, including tanks, helicopters, and planes, which offer a stark contrast to the human stories told inside. Inside, you’ll find exhibitions that highlight the effects of Agent Orange, stories of international support for Vietnam, and reflections on the impact of war on civilians.

Plan to spend at least two hours here if you’re taking your time to fully absorb the exhibits. It’s an emotional and thought-provoking experience that is not only a lesson in history but also a call for peace. While it can be overwhelming, it’s a must-visit for anyone looking to understand Vietnam’s recent history in a meaningful way.

Tips for Visitors:
• Be prepared for the emotional weight of the exhibits.
• Allow yourself enough time to explore all the levels without feeling rushed.
• Consider taking breaks between sections to process the content.

This museum is a somber reminder of the horrors of war and a tribute to the resilience of the human spirit.

This was a fantastic museum with lots of rooms to explore telling the story of the Vietnam war , very anti American. I was hoping it would tell the story of how the war began to set the scene to the exhibits but well worth a visit - very sad !

Lots of very interesting artefacts and exhibitions. Definitely worth visiting, however it does get pretty crowded. Found it hard to really absorb all the information due to the chaos of people around. Certainly not your typical "museum" atmosphere, but would still highly recommend.
There was a huge line for tickets stretching around the block, however I went with part of a private city tour and skipped the line entirely. Highly recommended doing it that way over just rocking up on a whim. As mentioned above, it was difficult to actually stand and read all the info, so having a private guide was certainly worth it!

Another reminder of the evils of war & human conflict. Nothing like unleashing the dogs of war to reveal the depravities of human nature.
The museum is well done in terms of the exhibits. Much thought and energy has gone into preserving the memories and highlighting the lessons learned. The public forgets easily.

A very somber but informative museum showcasing perhaps some lesser known truths about the war. To say that we enjoyed it is a bit of grey area as it’s not something to be entertained by but rather learn from. Our biggest disappointment was seeing other tourists posing with and taking snaps specifically of graphic photos particularly the ones with children in them. We thought this was incredibly disrespectful and inappropriate and showed a total lack of situational and self awareness and for that reason we think the upper levels where these displays are should be designated as no photo areas out of respect for the victims shown.

This place was very enlightening and eye opening regarding what happened during the Vietnam War. Within the building it didn’t seem like there is any A/C within the building so prepare yourself for a humid event. They had fans in almost every room but they didn’t do too much. There’s a small cafe towards the exit that had a few refreshments.
(We bought our tickets using Klook)

I think the War Remnants Museum is a must see at least once when you visit Saigon. Welcoming you with possibly used weapons of war (I can't really say for certain whether they were real or not) at the front, the place is a good reminder of what the Vietnamese ancestors went through to get where they are now. It was quite difficult for me growing up to understand the history of my Southeast Asian neighbors, so this place successfully filled the gaps. They also had audio guides for 200000 vnd available in different languages so you could go around the place at your own pace. It is full of pictures, stories, and Remnants from the Vietnam War and the years prior.

The only thing that made me cross off a star is the fact that it is quite hot inside. If there was an air conditioner, I didn't feel it. Yes, it could be because there were simply too many bodies moving about, but as a place continously being touted to tourists, it would have been nice if the place was kept cooler. I was with my mother and the humidity and the heat made her feel so uncomfortable that we unfortunately had to cut the trip short. Maybe if there were some vending machines nearby to allow us to refresh ourselves, that could have enticed us to stay longer. If there were any, we didn't see them.

Our visit to the War Remnants Museum was a sobering and informative experience. The combination of interactive exhibits, historical photographs, and captured military equipment provided a stark and multi-faceted look at the Vietnam War. The museum does not shy from graphic depictions, making it a powerful testament to the human cost of war. While emotionally challenging, it is a crucial visit for understanding Vietnam's complex history and the enduring impacts of conflict.

It's intense. Upsetting. But important

Unfortunately no airconditioning so very hot even with fans and was very busy.
Sensitive people be advised. Some images are gruesome. Very interesting and a good museum.

What I admire most about the Vietnamese people is their sense of nationalism & their commitment to remember their story & where they came from. The War Remnants Museum is proof of that.

Quite a heavy experience, but it makes you confront the hard truths about war & why a person should be anti-war. Allocate at least 2 hours to go through all 4 floors.

Very good museum, most of the rooms have ‘slight’ AC and the exhibits are good. Old planes and other machinery outside and photos/paintings inside. There’s also a recreation of the conditions people were imprisoned in outside but not well signposted so don’t miss it. Some of the agent orange photos are very extreme and hard to look at but important for people to see. Toilets on every floor that were decent for Vietnam.

As others have said,  a little one sided to be sure. Well put together museum with shockingly good pictures of various wars. A somber reminder of the tenacity of the Vietnam people and their history. Do yourselves a favor, start early and start at the top floor, it gets very hot and humid in there which detracts from the seriousness of the visit.  Good value.
This was a moving experience for me.  You must keep in mind that the Vietnamese will spin their version of the war.  The museum is large and clean.  The placards are in English also.

They explained the complete war history between Vietnam and US. They have artefacts from the war and the audio book explains in dept. you can spend around 2-3 hours easily listening to the audio book and exploring thoroughly

I'm glad I went. It is an eye-opening experience of the devastating effects of war. Many of the images are graphic and hold true to the reality of the times. The museum's point of view appears to be unbiased, showing facts.

The museum was nice and informative and it shows you what war felt like and what the Vietnamese people went through during that period. I think everyone should visit. In the hot weather period starting in ~late April, make sure you visit in the early hours as the heat can be torturous. Also remember to bring a fan.

A good place to spend a few hours learning more about the Vietnam War. The exhibit starts on the top floor, and you work your way down to the ground floor room by room.
The most moving part for us was the floor with all of the photos from the war...some of the images are truly shocking and the handy explanations of the shots allow visitors to better understand what was happening when they were taken, providing sombering context to the reality of what people went through during that time.
Be warned that there's no AC in the building, just fans in the exhibition rooms so it gets quite hot in the afternoon.

We couldn’t explore the museum in full. Different to museums in other countries, the closing time is really the closing time. They switched off the lights 1 minute after the closing time and usher out their guests quite aggressively. That was a bit disappointing.

The portions which we could see were informative and a good counter-balance to what is taught in other countries.
Pretty good, although some of the displays are very confronting.

Intense, raw and historical. Not for the faint hearted. Vietnam don't hold back and nor should they. But don't be caught out when you go in thinking they aren't going to tell their side of the saw in a raw and direct way. Be prepared if you are going to visit to take it in their history as they want to tell it. It was hard to take in parts.

A decent sized museum where you can spend a couple of hours. The exhibits outside depicting french war time prisons are quite impactful. The museum details the stages and impact of the wars well. No air conditioning which is not so bad.

The Ultimate story of Vietnam war and the suffering and spirit of bounce back of Vietnamese people.
Note: start from the 2nd floor section 1. Then start coming down floors.
The war museum has everything to portray the war story. There should be an audio visual storytelling documentary show. All the details in museum is in written form. I saw Many Many people just do a photo tour and doesn't bothered to read things. An audio visual story can be a really good feature for the world to know about Vietnam war.

Tips : If you are to read details you need 3-4 hours.  During the summer season manage the way you come out of the museum by 5.00 pm so you can take pictures with war machines like planes and tanks outside.

Try to avoid random taxis for your way back from the museum, Grab app is best or you can try Vinasun taxis .

We visited the War Memorial while here in HCMC.
Wow, it was a very hot day, walking around reading the exhibits and viewing old remants of war, was very touching and emotional.
I just can't quite believe this history not long past here for the vietnamese people, they have endured alot.
Reading about the treacherous conditions and how they were treated by the US.Army was just appalling, I was nearly walking around with tears in my eyes seeing all the suffering from babies to children, mothers, fathers, grandparents and completely innocent victims.
So Sad

An interesting if very one-sided view of the Vietnam War, painted firmly with the Communist brush.

Whilst it did a great job showing the atrocities committed by the Americans, it ignores the same brutality committed by the Viet Cong on their POW.

War is never good. That should be the main takeaway from this.

very moving museum with many interesting exhibits and different types of stuff to look at. the only thing i can complain about is the extreme heat and lack of ac inside the museum. everyone was walking around constantly and sweating. Cheap entrance.

The museum is well kept and easy to access. Unfortunately there was a man soliciting money inside, and while I don't really mind giving to a victim of the war he was extremely nosy and looked into my wallet to try and get more money out of us, even asking for euros which he thought he had seen inside. It was very uncomfortable and very intrusive and sadly had a very negative impact on the experience.
It is a great museum that showcases Vietnamese war history from the eyes of the communist party. Saying that nothing justfies the cruelty on locals and the millions live lost. It was very sad.
Really it is for the people to know their past. How they struggle and fought to gain their independence

A very beautiful place for all the history freaks. It takes about the Vietnam war and what the people of Vietnam have faced due to the war. The pictures are heart breaking and you will understand how hard they worked to build the country back together. You can easily buy a ticket in counter. Make sure you carry cash.

The War Remnants Museum in Ho Chi Minh City tells the story of Vietnam's past wars. It's a small place but teaches a lot about history and makes you think. Through its exhibits and displays, visitors gain a deeper understanding of the country's history, particularly its struggles during the Vietnam War.

Interesting museum but most of the exhibit consists of photos with description so if you already know about the French colonization period and the Vietnam war you may find it less interesting.
Some part of the museum were very touching and graphic, definitely not recommended for kids even tho I saw more than a few here.

Great place to study about the Vietnam war. Plenty of old US army aircraft’s. An eye opener on the atrocities that happened during the war. Free entry as part of the City Sightseeing Saigon buses ticket. Went during the day and it was very hot inside the main building.

A great synergy activity to the tunnel tour. A very vietnamese centred discourse with very graphical pictures (including photos of dead children) but each war has at least two sides of the story (and the other most of us already know). Not worth buying the audio guide as there are qr codes for cell phone access of the same audio content.

A sobering experience. I knew it would be heavy, but being from the USA and growing up in the war era, I wanted to see and learn. I took lots of pictures of war machines, tanks, and aircraft for my son, who loves history. I'm thankful I went and recommend.
You learn a lot, and feel a lot… A MUST when in Vietnam, and especially in Ho Chi Minh City; however, I think the Museum could renovate itself to be a little bit more interactive. …
