I think the War Remnants Museum is a must see at least once when you visit Saigon. Welcoming you with possibly used weapons of war (I can't really say for certain whether they were real or not) at the front, the place is a good reminder of …

Our visit to the War Remnants Museum was a sobering and informative experience. The combination of interactive exhibits, historical photographs, and captured military equipment provided a stark and multi-faceted look at the Vietnam War. The …
What I admire most about the Vietnamese people is their sense of nationalism & their commitment to remember their story & where they came from. The War Remnants Museum is proof of that. …

Very good museum, most of the rooms have ‘slight’ AC and the exhibits are good. Old planes and other machinery outside and photos/paintings inside. There’s also a recreation of the conditions people were imprisoned in outside but not well …

As others have said,  a little one sided to be sure. Well put together museum with shockingly good pictures of various wars. A somber reminder of the tenacity of the Vietnam people and their history. Do yourselves a favor, start early and …
It was very crowd unfortunatelly..

A challenging visit with many graphic and confronting images of an horrific event. So much wasted life and damage to so many people on all sides of the conflict. Definitely visit to try and understand what happened and the legacy it has left on the Vietnamese people and the soldiers.
Interesting museum with very good compiled history in both text and images
A must visit but what a sad story. It was very well put together and not totally bias but the section on Agent Orange was very graphic, be warned. A total waste of life on both sides!!

I visited here for the first time in 30 years. Foreigners 40,000 VND about 240 yen Now there was a Japanese audio guide for 80,000 VND about 480 yen. Since it was such an opportunity, I also took a tour with an audio guide. Credit cards are accepted.  Honestly, I thought the exhibition was more Showa-style than other museums of today, and not much had changed from 30 years ago. It was a reminder that Vietnam, which is not a fun museum but has developed with the present peace, has a sad past to forget.  The exhibition room also has a fan, but the air conditioning is weak for foreigners, and the outside of the exhibition room is quite hot and tiring to visit.  You can also buy water from the vending machines in the building, but the price is about three times that of convenience stores.

A lot of exhibits, some of them fairly gruesome from the war. Free tickets when you take the hop on hop off bus tour. Lots of pictures, but also large static displays like helicopters and planes.  Thought provoking and a great history lesson.  Was busy, so consider going early.

Sau khi trải nghiệm tour, em hiểu rõ hơn về cuộc chiến mà dân tộc ta đã trải ưua. Quá nhiều mất mát đau thương. Đảng ta đã lường trước mọi hiểm nguy từ Mĩ. Để tránh sai lầm ở hiệp định Genevel , bác Hồ ta đã quyết định tự mình định hình hiệp định Paris, nền tảng hòa bình vững chắc dựa trên chính sức mạnh đại đoàn kết dân tộc. Em cảm ơn bảo tàng vì đã tái hiện chân thật những gì mà ông cha ta phải đánh đổi để lấy hòa bình ngày hôm nay.
Historical remains and accounts from the American War. It is worth visiting but some may find some of the details disturbing.
It has a lot of real life model of war machineries including tanks, helicopters, and various other machineries. It also has lots of photos and stories during time of war

I gave this 4 stars because if its historical significance but it leaves a lot to be desired as a museum. Lots of photos but the English captions are quite small meaning it is difficult to get close enough to read if it is crowded. Perhaps a more organized approach going back to the origins of the war. I guess if you want to learn this you can read a book on it or watch Ken Burns’ documentary. But give this a visit.
I'm not sure if 'enjoy' is the right word for our visit to the museum as there is much in there which will get you angry. But it's important to see and the displays hold nothing back.

Easy to find in Ho Chi Minh. Only 40,000VND, (just over £1) to enter. Go to the top level to start with. You’ll see a number on each door and this guides you through the tour. The museum takes you on a journey from beginning to the end of the Vietnam war, with some other interesting sections including the use of Agent Orange. There is a fascinating section on the war photographers, their pictures are sad, dramatic and graphically accurate. Each room had plenty of fans blowing cool air around. Outside are tanks, artillery pieces, helicopters and planes from the war. It is well curated and definitely thought provoking. You’ll probably spend at least two hours looking and learning. Toilet facilities onsite as well as a coffee shop and gift shop.

On the way back to the hotel we visited this museum where we could park our scooter for a small fee. Inside, the Vietnam War and its atrocities were impressively depicted. The exhibition was very informative and offered a deep insight into history. In addition, there was war equipment on display, which made the visit even more impressive. In the adjoining cafe you could have a coffee, although it seemed rather small and a bit run-down – so a visit is not absolutely necessary.

The War Remnants Museum offers a sobering, often gut-wrenching look at the Vietnam War through the lens of Vietnamese civilians, with powerful photojournalism, preserved military equipment, and graphic depictions of Agent Orange’s lasting impact—it's not an easy visit, but a vital one.
It was quite good. Airplanes, tanks kept at the museums were quite fascinating. Exhibits kept were moving and emotional experience.
Much history in terms of machines, weapons and equipment. Very well organized and little people making it pleasant to visit.

There is a lot to see and take in here. Be very good to have a short overview presentation to put the whole war and the period into context.  Obviously, being based in Vietnam, a very one sided story, but what is presented shows what a despicable effort the USA made of the war.  Be greatly improved with one or more short video presentations.

I think it's worth a visit. A ticket costs VND 40,000. The museum is not big. It'll take two hours at the most. There are pieces of equipment, weapons. But probably 70% of the exhibits are photographs. All in Vietnamese and English.

It's a sad place to visit. Photographs, torture gear and captions from the small showroom and mock prison camp attached to the main pavilion are distressing.  If you're a religious person in this place it's depressing. Can't recall the interest in taking pictures here. May war rage and the souls who have died rest in peace.
Interesting with lots of documentation and photos only flat not translated into French. Rather visit the week because Sunday many people

The only Museum that I have been to that made me shed a couple of tears. Was an awesome experience apart from the business in there. So much to see and you walk out of there with more knowledge and compassion.

It is interesting to hear from another source what happened in the war. In the west, we usually only hear the American voice. With what you see here, you should take into account that this information is also one-sided. But pictures speak for themselves...terrible what people are capable of!

BEWARE! FAKE GRAB AT EXIT will take you for triple coast and abandon you in a 'deadzohe' with no Internet saying destination is around the corner. Terrible scam! Be careful. Museum itself is great and informative.

Firstly we didn't go on a tour - just walked in and bought a ticket - so I am not sure whether this is the right place to review!  As others have said this museum is not for the feint hearted. Its name is a little spurious as it is really a museum totally committed to presenting the horrors and atrocities the Americans committed in the Vietnam war. It presents only one side of the story chronicling what the Americans did and the impact it had.  That said, for those of us in the UK who were alive at the time and thought we were aware of the war, it provided a very sharp awakening to the terrible atrocities of war, especially the Vietnam war. We know the Americans largely escaped any retribution and it is clear that carried out some heinous crimes.  Some of the rooms, especially the photos of those handicapped and disfigured by the chemical weapons used are extremely hard to see and my partner had to leave the room part way through.  There was far more to read than we were expecting and it was too much to take it all in. One could spend hours here if one was prepared to look at everything. I think it could do with more videos - there was only one mainly describing the cages used to keep prisoners showing in the main hall.  Another challenge was that the main atrium is not air conditioned and on the very hot humid day we visited it, it became a dash into the various rooms to try and keep cool. Additionally, apart from in the main entrance, there are very few seats, no escalators to the upper floors and only one lift supposedly for disabled and older people. We used it and there wasn't a long wait.

Oh my, what a place! Heart rendering. Thought provoking. Saddening, sickening. Can people really get worse with what we do with each other? Each room was moving. I couldn't bare to go into the massacre room, having seen the torture and agent orange exhibits. This is a must see for everyone, and a reminder that powerful forces such as the USA should never be allowed to control the world. I think the Vietnamese people have been very kind in their exhibition in gently discussing the issues that other countries put them under for many many years. Sadly, I don't think the world has learned.

An interesting historical sight with mostly anti USA war material presented from Vietnams point of view. This a must see as it is very well set out and has descriptions in english or you can have a head set.,

Quite a harrowing experience all in all but they really should update it with better AV, more accounts of how it impacted people and dramatically improve their AC.  Really shocking what a superpower was capable of doing in such recent times - everyone visiting vietnam should see this  Rob
价格不贵 还行 门口的美军武器挺有意思 越南战争打了17年，太惨了。美国除了虐杀无辜还化学施毒（agent orange, agent purple....，残害越南人民和土地，真的是滔天大罪 哎
Shocking images with the raw true of one very close past. It’s very impactful and well executed museum. In my opinion it’s a must see in Vietnam

For those not familiar with the timelines related to Vietnam’s quest for independence, this is a good place to learn. Incomplete as it relates to US POW treatment at the hands of Vietnam and Viet cong but the other information represents an accurate timeline of events as I remember and documented in news and other publications.

Very nice museum. They showed several stages of the war. Everything from how the locals were executed to more massacres of entire cities with napalm bombs and the like, to how they worked diplomatically to end the war. They showed several videos and photos of local people that made a strong impression.  Several tanks, aircraft and weapons were exhibited.  Own exhibition for war reporters and their photos was also very nice - as well as that one saw why this war became so unpopular.  You should start upstairs and walk downstairs.

A "must see" in Ho Chin Minh. Admission costs 40,000 dong. The Vietnam War and its aftermath are vividly depicted over several floors through photographs. Pictures of the effects of Agent Orange and Napalm are also on display - not for the heavy-hearted. There are still military vehicles in the yard.

Small museum, but laid out fairly well. Very interesting to see the war from another point of view. The War Crimes section was very powerful and moving. The war photography section was also very good. Allow a couple of hours for a visit, easy to combine with a visit to The Independence Palace and then go across the road for food at Ben Nghe Street food market.

A must when visiting the city. A reminder. Educational structure, easy to follow. Some targeting of the US as then enemy, understandable. It would have been a bit interesting to see South Vietnam’s part of the story. But with open, you're aware of who's writing history here. I'm sure Agent Orange's department can get most people to stay and think about the madness.

A somber experience telling the story of the atrocities committed by the US on the people of Vietnam. It inevitably ignores the atrocities committed by the Viet cong (N Vietnamese army) on the US forces. That’s a shame because you leave wondering why young ordinary GI’s could become so barbaric and one of the answers is because the same was being done to them. Obviously some GI’s would have had a sadistic streak and this war allowed them to fulfil their desires while others wanted revenge for the death and torture of their colleagues but some simply were forced to comply. It really shows the callousness of the American leaders of the day who ignored and indeed didn’t care about the total brutality that was being dished out on the Vietnamese people at the time.
A good museum for those who love their own, war is scary and the civilized world does not need it. The prison layout is interesting too. Tech is mostly American

Very good as a museum, with an exhibition showing the impact of the war on the Vietnamese people. So, it's a very sad museum. I needed to see it in stages, and I recovered for a while until I entered the next room.
Very confronting and would say slightly one sided view of what took place however we are Vietnam where this all occurred and there is no doubt that many innocent people were affected.
This is an insightful informative excursion for very little money.  You get a good picture of the country's tough years. Many disturbing images are shown, one should be aware of this.

While this experience is difficult because of its graphic depictions of the American war, it is a “must- see.” The War Crimes exhibit was closed, but the Requiem exhibit of photojournalism and the photojournalists who were killed in the war was exceptional.

Visiting the War Remnants Museum in Ho Chi Minh City was an incredibly powerful and emotional experience. The museum provides a comprehensive and often painful look into the Vietnam War, highlighting not only the devastation it caused but also the resilience of the Vietnamese people.  The exhibits are deeply impactful, with thought-provoking photographs, personal stories, and artifacts that bring the harsh realities of war into focus. The pain endured by the people of Vietnam is genuinely felt throughout the museum. What stood out most to me were the displays about Agent Orange, which detailed the horrific long-term effects on civilians. I had also heard from local people that even today, in some regions, families are still suffering from its devastating impact. It’s a heartbreaking reminder of the ongoing legacy of war and the far-reaching consequences of such actions.  One of the things that struck me the most was the rawness of the museum's narrative. It doesn't shy away from showing the true horrors of war, and while some of the content is graphic, it serves as a necessary and sobering reflection on the human cost of conflict. The images, particularly those of innocent civilians, are haunting and difficult to forget.  The outdoor section, featuring military equipment like tanks, planes, and helicopters, adds another layer of realism to the experience. Seeing the scale of these machines used in the war only further emphasizes the enormity of what took place here.  As I reflected on the museum’s exhibits, I couldn’t help but think about how no country or region should ever inflict such suffering on another. What happened in Vietnam is a stark reminder of the destructive potential of war, and as humanity, we must ensure that such atrocities are never repeated. The stories told here offer a valuable lesson for the world, reminding us of our shared responsibility to promote peace and prevent such tragedies.  If you’re visiting Ho Chi Minh City, the War Remnants Museum is a must-see. While it’s a challenging experience, it’s one that will leave a lasting impression and provide a deeper understanding of Vietnam's history, pain, and perseverance.
As a couple, we were struck by the exposures. Photos and stories are powerful, but the atmosphere can be heavy. It was an experience that got us thinking, even if it is not a place to relax.

Well-documented museum that highlights the atrocities of the war, that of Indochina (against the “French slave trader” as is constantly recalled during this part) then that of Vietnam against the United States. A good part comes from photos and testimonies of international journalists, Then weapons (rifles, machine guns, mines,...) are on display. At the end of the visit, a very (too much at times) realistic part that shows the damage of Agent Orange much used by the US military during the war, as well as the devastation of napalm bombs. You will end the tour with a reconstruction of prisons and torture systems... In short, a museum that has the merit of showing things as they were actually lived at the time, but sometimes a little “gory”.

There are some photos that are a little exciting, but I think you should look at them at least once. It was nice to have headphones and receivers that could explain things in Japanese, but I didn't know I could deposit cash or passport.
A very well put together historical record of the war that affected so many, very moving and dignified, you can easily spend some time here deep in thought at what people went through

Local tells the history of the war, its stories, its victims and the situation that the people subjected themselves. Ends with the "glamour of war films", some people got bad with the photos and the place was full, some rooms without air conditioning to make it worse. Worth a visit,

A must see for anyone visiting Vietnam. The audio was an unnecessary purchase as everything was well labelled and documented. It raw and confronting to learn about what the people of this country have been through.
Very sad and moving, well put together. Horrific photos, the journalists room was so moving. Missed the torture room. Air con was off, apparently broken, absolutely sinking hot.

We are on a OAT 24 day tour of all of Vietnam  The War Remnant Museum is interesting but mainly a propaganda tool (possibly rightfully so).  Even after the US pulled out of Vietnam, we continued to send armor and supplies to the South Vietnam army for years where they continued to battle on, and much of it is now on display here at the museum with US markings applied.  This is a very depressing museum. There are photographs of which only about 10% of which us back in the US ever observed on TV or within periodicals. Yea some would say lots of Communist Propaganda, but no denying it = the photos are VERY effective of what hell looked like when it comes to war!!! Asian peoples can be somewhat brutal to one another, and when American solders see what is going on they can get right in there with the brutality, especially when they lose friends in battle. There is a separate section which shows the birth defects effect consequences from the spraying of the defoliant “Agent Orange”.  This is one museum, Americans should see, but NOT a location I would want to revisit!  The photos were shot by photojournalist, of which many lost their lives in the conflict. I am not too sure X US Vietnam vets would like to view what is displayed within the inhumane consequence of war that is on display, that some of our troops got involved in.

Great place to visit. Very moving displays to wander around and look at. A look at the wars that have affected Vietnam from a totally different perspective to what we are told in the west. Would thoroughly recommend visiting this place.

Learn about the history of Vietnam from French rule to the Vietnam War. Be careful if you're not good at Vietnam War-related exhibits, as they clearly show the damage caused by dead leaf agent and napalm, as well as brutal photographs. I can't help but feel the gratitude of peace and the folly of human beings.

This is a very depressing museum. There are photographs of which only about 10% of which us back in the US ever observed on TV or within periodicals. Yea some would say lots of Communist Propaganda, but no denying it = the photos are VERY effective of what hell looked like when it comes to war!!! Asian peoples can be somewhat brutal to one another, and when American solders see what is going on they can get right in there with the brutality, especially when they lose friends in battle. There is a separate section which shows the birth defects effect consequences from the spraying of the defoliant “Agent Ornge”. This is one museum, Americans should see, but NOT a location I would want to revisit! The photos were shot by photojournalist, of which many lost their lives in the conflict. I am not too sure X US Vietnam vets would like to view what is displayed within the inhumane consequence of war that is on display, that some of our troops got involved in. What we observed matched the Four moves you may have seen (quite well): • Apocalypse Now • Full Metal Jacket • The Deer Hunter • Ken Burns “The Vietnam War” At the end of the war, most South Vietnamese wealthy individuals left the city, thus the government gave their property / homes / apartments to high-ranking North Vietnamese military officers.

During my visit to the War Remnants Museum in Vietnam, I was deeply moved by the stories and exhibits that revealed the remarkable courage of the Vietnamese people in their struggle for freedom. The museum’s displays, particularly those detailing the horrors of imprisonment and the war crimes committed by the U.S. during the conflict. It was a sobering reminder of the resilience and strength that the Vietnamese people demonstrated in the face of unimaginable adversity. Witnessing these accounts firsthand brought a profound understanding of the sacrifices made for their independence.

My wife and I visited the War Remnants Museum in August 2024 and it was a very interesting experience. Exhibits include the defeat of the French at Dien Bien Phu, recreations of typical French and American prisons, photojournalism from the Vietnam War, military vehicles and the impacts of Agent Orange. Beware that many of the exhibits are confronting and may not be appropriate for young children, however they offer an extremely interesting insight into the Vietnam War from the perspective of the Vietnamese people. One other thing to watch out for - there is a scammer operating around the museum who is really friendly and has a yoke/carrying pole with coconuts. He is really friendly, strikes up a conversation, then distracts you while opening coconuts and then tries to extort you 150,000 VND per coconut. Steer clear of him.

I travel through another shame of human beings: the war in Vietnam. In addition to military vehicles, there are also prisoner cages, bombs and lots of photos. An impressive journey but to do to understand more of the history. Although, to be honest, I will never understand the need for these massacres.
There are military tanks,artillery,and aircraft outside the museum, and also inside there are photos, and weapons inside.

Very interesting. You see the view of the war from the Vietnamese point of view but without having the impression that it is propaganda or anti-American. It helps you get a sense of the suffering and courage of Vietnamese people. It is worth going to the beginning of your stay in Saigon
Very exciting to see and read about the history of Vietnam. It's about the Vietnam War. Recommend going here if you are interested in history.

This museum is very impressive and completely worth it. It is truly aimed at encouraging peace by showing the horrors of war to all participants.  However, it can be too strong for sensitive people and for children or even teenagers.It is important to know that some of the images on display are very explicit and show terrible situations of violence.

The war museum could not be missed during our visit. Like many other war museums, including in Europe, the glory of the victor is highlighted here. The horrific war acts of the French and especially the Americans come to us through photos and texts. Little is visible of any reflection on their own contribution to the violence of war. Inside and outside the building, the museum extensively displayed the war equipment that the Vietnamese had captured, from small arms to tanks, airplanes and helicopters. It was remarkable that there was a lot of attention for deceased (international) war photographers. Attention was also paid to the international support, including a photo of a Vietnam demonstration in Amsterdam and a poster of the Netherlands-Vietnam Medical Committee.

A very detailed account of the War. Don’t waste time and money booking a tour with a guide or through your hotel for the museum. Take a Grab (their version of Uber) to the museum - request the audio guide (they will give you headphones - that you can play according the plaques or floors in the museum (multiple languages as well - all pre-recorded). You can go at your own pace and skip if a certain segment if needed. To enter is about $1. With the audio guide - will work out to around $4.
A must visit when in Saigon unbelievable what really happened some graphic photos but very interesting, lots of military equipment on show which was amazing to see Definitely a eye opener
You need strong nerves! Still very worth seeing! Best to combine it with the Reunification Palace as it is right next door.

What else can you expect in a war memorial museum than war equipment and related material? To ask the question is to answer it. On the forecourt there are tanks and armored vehicles, helicopters and fighter jets from the Vietnam War. That included a scout tank M41 'Walker bulldog' in which I received my military training as a tank commander in 1969. I can tell the bystanders that there were four of us in that little thing. In one corner you will find a collection of all types and sizes of bombs that were dropped in their thousands across Vietnam. Inside, an extensive collection of shooting equipment. Revolvers, pistols, rifles, machine guns from small to large caliber. Of course you also get to see less pleasant things. Photos of atrocities committed, people disfigured after using Agent Orange - the chemical defoliant - etc.

We all enjoyed our visit to the museum which compared to some is old fashioned however it is confronting on some many levels even thou it seems very biased which is understandable considering. Minimal ac so things can heat up quickly and personally I wouldn’t take young children due to the graphic images etc

I nearly gave this the full 5 stars as it is a very interesting and thought provoking museum and definitely a must do. However, it is just too skewed to an anti-American rhetoric when, as somebody else pointed out, the Vietnam War was a civil one with the Republican South happy to ask the US to step up their involvement against the Communist North. The fact this museum used to be named The Museum of Chinese and American War Crimes shows its leanings. I know it is a museum in Vietnam so, of course, expect everything from a local angle, but something to be very aware of. That said, it is a very interesting tour with plenty of fascinating photos and exhibits to see. Be warned though that it does get very graphic.

All I can say is this is confronting. Of course it is presented from a Vietnamese point of view, but having been an anti-war protester in the 60’s I wasn’t concerned about that. There is an amazing collection of weaponry of war and tanks and planes etc. outside. There are stories of local heroes and heroines who helped the war effort and during the civil war that proceeded it. But the most compelling and confronting section is the one named ‘War Crimes’. Seeing the brutality of the war inflicted on the Vietnamese it is amazing that only two US soldiers ever got charged with war crimes. Even an officer who subsequently become a US Senator, has admitted to bayonetting three innocent children hiding in a sewer pipe. How can you live with that? The after-effects of Agent Orange and unexploded ordnances are also clearly exhibited (Over 40,000 Vietnamese have been killed by unexploded bombs and mines since the war ended!). If you have a couple of hours spare make sure you go, and although we didn’t know about them, it is probably worth hiring the audio guide headphones.

Its an eye opener to the horrors of war however one i feel sees one side of the story just the same war is a tragedy no matter where and when and by seeing this one can only hope human nature betters itself all be it the world still fights wars.
This is a tough museum. The reality of the political situation and the effects on the Vietnamese people is hard to see but important. It is lightly air conditioned so plan to sweat.
ATTENTION! DO NOT BUY the bullet-shaped key rings because they confiscate them at the airport, even if they are in your checked luggage!

Both informative and sad. For anyone who feels discomfort at hearing criticism of the US, don't go. Not to say the US is painted poorly just accurately. While the displays are dated and simple, the story told is poignant.

Very interesting, through the photographs, mostly American and Vietnamese photographers, in a graphic way you get to know the horror of war. The resistance of the Vietcom guerrillas. And the American attempt using chemical weapons to paralyze the communist invasion. With stories of the victims of the Napal bombings and Agent Orange. How people were transformed into monstrous images and how all plant and animal life was destroyed with terrible consequences for future generations.
A moving and educational experience. At times challenging as it confronted the sadder aspects of the negative impacts of conflict.

You can see the dark side of the Vietnam War. It's worth a visit. However, the second floor is so stimulating that even a doctor who visited for an international conference was surprised. If you are sensitive to stimuli, it may be best to avoid the second floor (and the outdoor facilities).
This is a must visit attraction. To understand what happened is vital. Even now the actions of those involved are still affecting the country, you need to go to understand.

Interesting to get an insight into the Vietnam War seen from a Vietnamese perspective. The teenager thought it was exciting to see tanks, planes and other American military equipment on display. The exhibition with photographs from the war correspondents was what attracted us adults the most. Strong images, strong stories and not for the faint of heart.

A war and atrocities museum, just to remind everybody how bad it is. Clearly a painful part of Vietnam’s history… just a reminder of what Russians a re doing in Ukraine now, have done in other places lately…

At times visually harrowing but interesting perspective on military history including aggression by the French and Americans. It's told from a Vietnamese point of view so this should be remembered, that said the information on "defoliants" their use and lasting side effects is difficult subject. I found the photo journalistic section brilliant and helpful to bring elements alive in the mind.
Well spent money! Alot of reading if you don't have a tourguide, so take your time. The facility is alittle bit small for the amount of people that goes, so that is good to have in mind.
The museum gives a very good insight into the conflict from the Vietnamese point of view. A set of very graphic exhibits make this interesting museum quite hard

It’s a sobering experience to see the effects of war on a country, regardless of who owns the narrative, so to those who complain it gives a biased account I would say “these are people doing unspeakable things to other people” and it happened in my lifetime. With the exception of the outdoor exhibits, this museum is largely comprised of photographs with explanation, many taken by western photo- journalists. After a while it becomes somewhat repetitive and tiring in the fuggy heat. With regard to the room dedicated to dioxin/agent orange you get the picture quickly. With two wars in progress as I write, little seems to have changed and no doubt there will be War on Remnants museums in Kyiv and Palestine in future.

Interesting but harrowing display of artefacts and photos from the Vietnam war. While the information display can be one-sided, and from the eyes of the victor, it's nonetheless a grim reminder that no matter which side, humanity suffers in war.
Visited here during the early evening of Dec-26,2023 and explored the various photos and captions on display. Learnt about the use of agent orange and its consequences.
Not a suitable place to take children - lots of graphic pictures of dead / maimed bodies - soldiers, civilians and children.  Useful to get a different perspective on the American / Viet Nam war.
This was a sobering experience. While clearly shown from the view of the Vietnam government, it still makes one reflect on the involvement of the US.

Good explanations of the living conditions of this war. Great pictures. On the other hand, be careful, some photos are horrible (impacts of chemical warfare on the malformation of children!). Reconstitution of prisons.
Must see all angels- but be prepared for impacting you emotionally. Also be aware of some rooms very difficult - so not recommended to children.
A summary of the Vietnam Wars and the horrors on both sides. A presentation of war crimes and the damage caused by Agent Orange. Part quite difficult to visit
We visited the museum as part of our full day to visit HCMC. Good collections of things to see about the Vietnam war. A must place to visit!

Visiting this place that bears witness to the cruelty of wars makes you feel truly sad. I am 54 years old and to think that during my first years of life, loved and pampered by my family in this part of the world, many children died or experienced these atrocities. But the saddest thing is that despite this wars continue. God save us Visit this place and form your own opinion. You will be less biased towards the heroes they have served up to us in various films.
I visited this museum today. It was very interesting looking at the old war relics. The pictures inside are very graphic but tell a good story. All the large weapons outside are interesting to see.

The last time we visited HCMC this "museum" resembled a scrap yard so I was sceptical of a re-visit. How things had changed, this is a full on comprehensive coverage including those in the media who also died. No holds barred & an eye opener of the political environment at the time including some not so well known actions of US politicians such as Kissinger et al & his communist domino effect ideas. Asd they say "history repeats itself" & it sure did with the Gulf War.

Really worthwhile activity to learn more about the wars happened in Vietnam and see first hand what some of the battleground looked like. I found the War Remnants museum to be very informative but deeply affecting.  The museum contains a variety of artifacts from the war, including photographs, weapons, and personal items. The photographs were particularly powerful, showing the devastation of the war and its effects on the people. The weapons and other objects, such as tanks and aircraft, were also impressive.

This museum is not for the faint hearted. It is filled with objects of the wars that were fought by the Vietnamese people especially the war against American imperialism..... incredible to see what brutality is unleashed by a nation on another just on ideological grounds... A must visit but deeply disturbing
Heart rending, but unmissable. Do not bring children! All I can say is that we left in tears before visiting all the displays.

Really worthwhile activity to learn more about the conflicts in Vietnam and see first hand what some of the battleground looked like. I found the War Remnants museum to be very informative but deeply affecting. The Cu Chi tunnels felt like it bordered on a playground for tourists. I didn't like that there was a shooting range on the site.

Good representations ..very political.. good displays. Though some displays very confronting, they depicted the Vietnamese version of the Vietnam Civil War (AKA Vietnam war or American War). The layout of the displays were a bit everywhere and hard to follow a direction of events in order. Overall worthy visiting
A must visit if you are in HMC but not for the faint hearted.  We spent a few hours here before flying out and some visitors were in tears.

The destruction wrought by the B-52 bombers and the enormous loss of life and property are shown at the war museum. But the Vietnamese have made great strides since then, mostly as a result of their dedication to their nation & labor!

It is a museum that I believe is essential on a visit to Saigon. It gives you a perspective on a war that lasted 17 years and the horrors it brought. Torture rooms, weapons, tanks, helicopters…. And above all photographs where the suffering caused by the horrible war is starkly seen. You can see the consequences of orange gas and many other things... Where the bombers took place, how they were tortured and the war itself. Of course, it is from the perspective of the winning side, but that does not mean that this is totally real and that it helps us learn and that this will never be repeated again (but we already see that it is not, as in the case of Ukraine). It honestly brought tears to my eyes.
This museum depicts how a war was fought by Vietnamese people with US to save their integrity. Many innocent lives were lost on both sides. This museum aptly defines it.

Very informative museum, especially for the historical facts part. I found the part on war journalists a little long and too many photos on the impacts of Agent Orange (crucial to tell it and even to put a photo of it for the collective memory, but the number of photos 'children with disabilities is really exaggerated). Otherwise, the audio guide is a must. The visit is definitely worth it. Allow approximately 2 hours.

While this museum was not included in our trip itinerary, we chose to visit to explore the history of Vietnam and the wars. Be prepared for very graphic photos and articles. I could not read/look at all - but made it through enough to get a grasp of how horrible the wars were. I recommend it to anyone who wants to know more about a really tragic time.  Tips: The exhibitions are definitely slanted toward the North and against the US, but since most of what I learned was probably a little slanted in the other direction, it helped me have a more balanced and comprehensive understanding.  Tickets are purchased with cash VND only - no cards or USD. There's a guy hanging out by the entrance who will exchange USD for VND at a significant cost, but since we were short on time, we chose that. Wish we'd known ahead!  Also, there are disabled men who will approach you when you enter if they believe you are US and attempt to start a conversation. From other reviews, this has gone for at least 6 years - he and other veterans ask for $ to buy 2 books and are aggressive - and the museum seems to support this behavior. It felt a little like exploitive to us, and we did not have time to spare. Again, just be advised.
War museum visit takes around good one to two hr . Display of aircraft, war tanks and photos of war impacts .Vietnam war history

Museo de la guerra de Vietnam. Nada más entrar, previo pago de 40000 dongs (1,50€), en el exterior se pueden ver aviones, tanques, helicópteros y diferente tipo de armamento usado en la guerra. Ya en el interior hay varios piso con salas donde se exponen fotografías y nos cuentan los años de ocupación por parte de Estados Unidos y el conflicto armado. Algunas fotos son muy crudas tanto de la guerra en sí como del impacto que tuvo sobre la población, en especial la Sala Naranja que trata sobre las consecuencias del uso de armas químicas a lo largo de los años, de ésta salí con muy mal cuerpo, la verdad. Hacia mucho calor pues carecía de aire acondicionado y únicamente había algún ventilador en las diferentes salas. Recomiendo llevar agua (también la venden en la planta baja del museo) y abanico. Si se está en Ho Chi Minh merece la pena ir.
I suppose it is a must when visiting Saigon (Ho Chi Minh) The exhibition is confronting, but appropriate. The history needs to be told.

Excellent, informative and thought provoking. We enjoyed reading about the war years and the lasting effects on Vietnamese people. I would recommend this as a must do. Infact all politicians should be forced to visit. War is awful, the pain and suffering endured by all must be avoided in the future. I could not view the Agent Orange room which was a step too far for me, as I found the cruelty of war on display overwhelming.

Good description of the war from the Vietnam side and view of it, which we in the West may not always set A lot of pictures, and stories, and some preserved military equipment to look at. Even a replica of an old French prison is on site

The museum has some heavy equipment like tanks, aircraft, hepter and gun shells, used by the US Army. It also has many photographs depicting the war atrocities. The locals generally do not visit it, but schools organise trips for their students to educate them of what their country went through during the war.

I visited this museum with my parents, my daughter and her now husband in March 2019 (can't select this date above). It was a fascinating but distressing visit, my daughter and her husband didn't get as far as the Agent Orange room and I don't blame them for not wanting to see it. What humans do to each other is unbelievable and the Agent Orange room shows that victims are still being born 50 years later. I firmly believe that if you travel somewhere on holiday and there are places like this you should visit - learning about the past is the only way to avoid repeating it.

After visiting the Cu Chi tunnels in the morning, we decided to continue learning about the Vietnam war by visiting the War Remnants museum. There is an excellent display of various tanks and aircraft etc used during the war in the forecourt. Most of the displays downstairs were as you’d expect but the Agent Orange room was confronting and the war crimes room was too much. Some of the photos are graphic and it’s unfathomable to believe crimes like this were committed against children etc. it’s best to go with an open mind and an understanding that war is cruel and you’re going to see some confronting images.

Một nơi rất tuyệt vời nếu bạn muốn tìm hiểu về lịch sử chiến tranh Việt Nam. Nơi đây lưu trữ nhiều hình ảnh, cũng như hiện vật có được trong cuộc chiến. Khác với những bảo tàng khác, bảo tàng Chứng tích chiến tranh thể hiện cho ta thấy được rất nhiều góc tối của cuộc chiến. Nơi đây không dành cho những bạn yếu tim. Hiện vật và tranh ảnh trưng bày nhiều thứ khá đáng sợ. Đặc biệt là tranh ảnh về những nạn nhân thời chiến.

The audio guide is expensive but useful for the 1st part of the exhibition, which gives historical context and development of the “Vietnam war”. Retrospectively I would have started with this museum before any other sites in Saigon because so much is explained by colonialism and history that led to the war. That said, the exhibition is old and the photographers are sometimes of very bad quality. Some parts are also very difficult, not advised for children under 12-14… like agent orange, war crimes and the jail… can’t be avoided tho

This was a disturbing museum. Please prepare your children or leave them at the gift shop. The images are graphic and horrifying. They are unsurprising in that they tell the story from a Vietnam perspective but it’s a good reminder that war is horrible and needs to be avoided at all costs. It’s strange tho that all the military vehicles are US and you can buy US army shirts in the gift shop!

Schwer verdauliche Kost, allerdings dennoch ein Pflichtbesuch wenn man in der Stadt ist. Ein Punkt Abzug gibt es dafür, dass keinerlei Einordnung stattfindet. Es ist eine "einfache Aneinanderreihung" von Gräueltaten.
Lots of information and opportunity to learn the parties/countries battles over the years. Well presented and easy to find.
thật tuyệt vời khi được tìm hiểu về lịch sử hào hùng của chúng ta , rất ấn tượng xúc động .................

War is terrible. This museum documents the horrors of the Vietnam War and shows the terrible logic of the American campaign.  The museum contains lots of artifacts in the form of pictures, documents, weapons (everything from pistols to massive tanks, helicopter, and airplanes. There are videos and movies to view.  The museum also contains graphic depictions of the cruel and murderous torture to which prisoners were subjected.  This museum is well worth a visit in order to get a deep understanding of the Vietnam War.  It took us about two hours to go through the museum

Museum depicts lots of very confronting and graphic photos of injured and deceased adults and children. Not for the faint hearted and empathetic. I did however find the photos, descriptions about anti war opposition and protests around the Western world interesting. When I entered the agent orange room I found images too distressing so left after seeing two photos and had a coconut drink at Cafe.
Museum that remembers with photographs, sometimes really atrocious what the war is and what the Vietnam War was. Experience that confirms the horror of all war conflicts

Basically three floors of mostly photographs which depicted the atrocities that the United States put upon the Vietnamese people. To be honest, I did not want to talk too loud to avoid possible negative conversation with the other visitors. The atmosphere is very quiet and depressing, which you would expect from the material presented. It’s very one-sided, but also quite an eye-opener.

Impressive. Authentic pieces, like the big planes of the ricins, and quite a lot of archives, some of which very shocking. A must-see passage from Saigon. It takes at least 2 to 3 hours that said to have time to do everything.
Some of the old equipment, to show my young son, the hellcopters, tanks and ammunition is interesting.
Een bezoek aan dit museum is zeker een aanrader. Alle facetten van de oorlog worden er belicht. Ook veel indrukwekkend fotomateriaal en diverse tentoonstellingen.
I think everyone who is visiting Saigon should visit this place. Some pictures are heartbreaking....
Well laid out and comprehensive history of the "American War" - a must see experience, although be warned - some of the agent orange exhibitions are very confronting
Nice museum with enough things to do. Be mindful that the prison and picture section are quite explicit (for young kids).
記載越戰的種種，揭示戰爭的殘忍，除了殘殺、酷刑，還有使用生化武器的後遺症。內容沉重，不是令人開心的地方，但令人反思戰爭的意義。
We had an hour scheduled to visit the war museum and ended up staying for two hours, which could have been longer. Highly educational and emotive.
Outside display vehicles attrect to go in.
空港からバスで途中降りて歩いてすぐ。直接行きました。ロッカーが無くて大荷物持ったままの見学になってしまいました。外に米軍がほかっていった戦車等多数有り。中は悲惨な写真が多いです。予習して行きましたが、衝撃です。見応えありました。

Very interesting collection of artifacts and some very detailed and lengthy exhibition pieces when we went especially the one about photo journalism during the war as I had no idea how many where killed during the wr.  Good to see the history of the war presented from a Vietnamese perspective as long as you are aware of he bias that is shown e.g. lengthy stories about VC and ARVN soldiers being tortured and mistreated against the Geneva Convention but nothing about similar being meted out to captured US pilots.  But all in all a good place to spend a few hours as you will learn a lot from another's perspective and pretty cheap entry tickets. Cafe was good as well for a cold soft drink during the visit.
It provides a good view on the war in Vietnam. Especially what the Americans did in terms of chemical warfare was mindblowing and left us in silence and tears. War, what is it good for?

Este mueso está dedicado a la guerra de Vietnam. Tiene gran variedad de equipamiento, tanques, aviones, armas militares entre otros para ver. Además tiene una muy buena exposición sobre la historia de la guerra y las terribles consecuencias humanitarias de esta, incluyendo el terrible efecto del agente naranja utilizado por Estados Unidos, y los terribles efectos en la descendencia y los pobladores. Es crudo, pero permite conocer esta terrible realidad.  TIPS: -El museo es muy bueno y muy informativo, pero es muy fuerte, con realidades muy crudas e imagenes rxtremadamente fuertes, pero expuestas con un sentido y respeto. -No recomendado para gente de caracter sensible ni para ir con niños.

As expected, a one-sided look at the Vietnam conflicts. Very graphic pictures, but well curated with English translations to every exhibit. Interesting array of American war vehicles in front of the museum.

My friend and I did this full day tour from HCMC. We had a great time and highly recommend it. The Cu Chi Tunnels park is about a two hour drive outside of the city center, so be prepared for lots of driving. However, the complex is very big and developed and was way cooler than we even imagined. We were pleasantly surprised. The War Remnants Museum was very emotional and a must see for every American visiting HCMC. Our tour guide was great and we had a lovely time.
This was one of our favorite experiences in Ho Chi Minh, it is a very interesting museum to learn about the devastating effects of the Vietnam wars.
1-3hrs at this museum is enough, an hour if you want to browse and maybe 3hrs if you want to read everything in depth. Sobering and informative.
War and its aftermath are disturbing. The photos, the things they have showcased is heartbreaking. Tanks, planes, cannons are displayed here.
An easy, affordable day activity. Very educational and eye opening. I did not realise the gruesome extent of the Vietnam War. The exhibits are confronting and do not shy away from the truth of war.
We were brought back to our history lessons about the Vietnam war and felt really sorry for the Vietnamese then. No wonder Vietnamese are so clever and hardworking in protecting their country!

See the war through the eyes of photojournalists and the US military. The agent orange exhibit is confronting, as is the war atrocities exhibit. It’s a good companion to the Hanoi museum. I found it very interesting that US politician “Bob Kerry admitted 15 years ago that he and the team of commandos he led in the Mekong Delta in 1969 killed innocent women and children during a midnight raid in the village of Thanh Phong. Survivors of the attack said 20 civilians were killed, including 13 children and a pregnant woman. Mr. Kerrey was awarded a Bronze Star after his squad falsely reported that it had killed 21 Viet Cong guerrillas”.
Photos weapons and stories of the conflict of particular interest to me because it was happening during my time as a young man
很有意义的一个博物馆，但是受限于越南经济发展，条件太简陋了，布置毫无美感，而且展览厅内才有空调，越南的天气，实在受不了。面积也不大。
Educational, raw and sometimes quite intense. So many people who've died and suffered because, as in most wars, the so-called ideals of people in power.

El museo de la guerra de Vietnam (War Remnants Museum) es cosa aparte. Es el museo más visitado por los turistas occidentales. Me impresionó bastante y pienso que no fui la única que estuve en shock durante la visita. El silencio que hay dentro dá mucho respeto. A mí, me “engancharon” las fotos que había expuestas. Está claro que las duras imágenes no pueden transmitir lo que vivieron ellos, pero os aseguro que hacen sentir bastante. Fuera están expuestos los vehículos blindados y las avionetas que, aunque en menor medida, también impresionan. Raconets.

This museum provides a clarification of the war. What happened and what consequences are still visible today.  Afterwards, we were silent about this. It has matured and not so long ago.  If you are interested in this I would recommend

Interesting museum, but obviously very biased. A lot of voyeurism (in my opinion) a little useless (at the 12th photo of a child deformed by the after-effects of Agent Orange, the message got through...) Must-see
One of those have to do trips, get to see the opposite view of the usual viewpoints. All clean and safe and able to see up close the exhibitions.
I big display of US and VN weapons as well as pics that may stay in your mind. Information about the result of agent orange not for the soft hearted.
Local onde nos é apresentada a versão do vietname sobre os acontecimentos, assim como a evolução da guerra nos media ocidentais com várias fotos que foram prémios pullitzer.
an amazing place , I forgot to finish my review when we were here , but I still can remember how awesome this place is. lots to see , history

It was a day spent with colleagues/friends at this museum and I was impressed to see and learn so much about this period of history. There were strong imagines but sadly all real. Is a must see museum.
Nơi lưu trữ chiến tích kỉ vật chiến tranh , nơi tưởng niệm quá khứ . Rất bổ ích cho giới trẻ luôn nhớ công ơn ông cha
Nhiều hiện vật lịch sử của các cuộc chiến tranh được lưu giữ và bảo tồn tại đây. Các hiện vật được bảo quản khá tốt.
Le plus interessant c' est la salle du dernier étage ou sont toutes les photos des atrocités subis par les vietnamiens durant la guerre
The museum is a must-visit in Saigon. It is a good presentation of historical facts of the Vietnam War, as well as a place to contemplate.

Each time I visit HCMC I like to come to this museum to be reminded of the sad history of the Vietnam War. On this occasion (early March 2020), because of the coronavirus, there were relatively few other visitors to the museum so it wasn't quite as crowded as usual.
Great place to learn about how Vietnam has been able to survive as a independent society.Interesting war relics.
Das Kriegsopfermuseum ist ein MUST SEE , sehr bewegend Es führt über mehrere Etagen und zeigt teilweise sehr deutlich die Auswirkunfen des Krieges

Although the museum gave a some what one sided view of the Vietnam War with the USA, there was some truth to the information. It demonstrated the senseless aspect of wars esp. the sequelae of Agent Orange and the Napalm bombing. Worth a visit.

Auf drei Etagen findet man hier überwiegend Fotodokumentationen aus dem Indochina- und Vietnamkrieg. Viele Fotos meist amerikanischer Kriegsreporter zeigen schwerpunktmäßig die Gräueltaten des Kriegs und die Folgen der Napalm- und Giftgasangriffe. Dazu gibt es viele kommunistisch-vietnamesische Kriegspropaganda (früherer Name: Museu der amerikanischen Kriegsverbrechen). Auf dem Hof sind Panzer und Militärflugzeuge ausgestellt sowie nachgestellte Gefängniszellen. Sehr bedrückend!

Visited this museum as part of a TUI tour of Saigon. We were given about an hour and a half to ourselves here and you probably need two hours to do it justice, we were hurrying at the end to see and read everything. The museum is fascinating with an obvious Vietnamese slant on the war but informative non the less, there are some harrowing photos and texts but this is war! Excellent exhibits that you can get very close to for photos, good little cafe on site for a cold drink as it was very hot during our visit mid afternoon. Recommended!

Ein Muss in HMC - auch, oder gerade wegen der etwas einseitigen Darstellung. Sehr informativ aufgemacht mit einem Gefängnis zum "anfassen" und vielen Ausstellungs- und Inforäumen. Wer sich vertieft, merkt nicht wie die Zeit verfliegt. 2-3 Stunden sind hier gar nichts.

When you are able to go to, now get it done. This particular museum is loaded with history, photos (some distressing), movies, footage, along with authentic cannons, aircraft, army tanks, guns, helicopter and other things. Allow two to three hours. You will find a cafe area and also a bit of store. The museum is nicely presented and about five entry fee. Worth a trip.
Μικρό το αντίτιμο εισόδου, αξίζει να το επισκεφτείτε, πολύ κοντά στο κέντρο, μαθαίνεις με πολύ ρεαλιστικό τρόπο, τη σειρά των γεγονότων και την ιστορία του πολέμου με τις ΗΠΑ.
This is a very interesting museum, but feel it maybe slightly one sided in the information they provide

We were in our early teens when the war was being fought and remember some of the harrowing pictures in the newspapers but nothing quite prepares you for what is on display here. I did not manage to make it all the way round as I was too distressed but my husband stayed strong and finished the tour of all the exhibits.
After your visit of this museum you are disgusted with the war, any war for that matter! Hopefully mankind will learn lessons from it.
Tres intéressant et émouvant On recommande pour comprendre l histpire de ce pays . On ne ressort pas indifferent face au photos .

Interesting visit to learn American view of Vietnam war. Difficult to read many harrowing stories of mass murder and see photos. I found it difficult to look at the tanks and guns that are used as I understood how many died at their hands.

Had a very enjoyable day at the museum, Lots of military equipment including planes, helicopters, various big and small guns, bombs, I learned a lot from the exhibition, Many graphic photos some upsetting,! A must to see when you are in Ho chi minh city.

An excellent museum, a reminder of what unreasonable actions a person does. Millions killed, maimed, what for? However, politics sometimes has different views and preferences. To never happen again .. Great picture, movie and article displays.
will learn how the Vietnamese people perceive the war. there is a certain amount of propaganda here which paints a picture of how wonderful the Viet Cong and the Communits were.

I found the museum very informative and touching. I was a teenager at the time of the war and very passionate against it. The people of Vietnam were so kind regardless, but I still felt so awful about everything that happened that I had to smoke a cigarette though I haven't been a smoker for 35 years.

A strange way of doing things but once understood it makes sense. The museum is based on the photos captured by the war photographers and the graphic examples of the war as it progressed. It is very moving.
some of it is pretty horrific. but it tells you the whole story of the war and it's effects. good way to spend a couple of hours

Un résumé objectif sur une guerre qui a fini par concerner le monde entier, il faut compter au minimum une heure si vous êtes pressés mais mieux vaut prendre beaucoup plus de temps car il y a énormément de photographies, pour la plupart signées par de grands professionnels. Il est clair que le regard sera différent en fonction de l'âge que vous aviez à cette époque.

The cost is very little and I was able to walk from my hotel, Grand Hotel - less than 1/2 hour, one way. No matter how much you know about the War, when you see the displays, you will be emotionally affected. I was glad to see many teenagers viewing as they were not alive during this war.

I am pleased I visited here but at times found it most distressing, especially the agent orange room and many of the photos. It was interesting to learn that many of the US military who have fought in Vietnam started to question why and, I felt the same. The place is solemn and sad but it is history and something we need to remember.
A good museum. Excellent exhibits. Some really superb photos which show what a crazy war this was. Well worth visiting. We all need to know.
A solemn and sad place. Interesting to see a different perspective. Worth a visit if you're interested in the country's history.

Entgegen den Ankündigung war das Museum nur mäßig gefüllt und wir mussten auch am Eingang nicht lange warten. Aus meiner Sicht kann man sich diese Ausstellung gut ansehen, um diesen Teil der vietnamesischen Geschichte einmal sinnlich zu erfahren.

Not easy to digest the museum. Initially you are looking for the ideal start ... you should start on the top floor! War in terrible images is depicted and we wonder why wars still exist. You must have visited this museum to understand the history of Vietnam from the perspective of the Vietnamese!
On connaît les dégâts causés par les guerres, mais là, les Vietnamiens sont factuels, avec des photos très dures (attention aux jeunes enfants). À voir absolument.

Niespełna 2 lata temu byłem w w Muzeum w Hanoi. Jest tam więcej eksponatów ale wystawa zdjęć z używaniem przez Amerykanów napalmu robi niesamowite wrażenie.Oglądanie wystawy nie dla wszystkich (niektóre zdjęcia okropne) Biedny naród wietnamski.

We spent 2.5 hours here and it was a very interesting and eye opening account of the war with some staggering articles, pictures and weaponry highlighting the events but you do get the feel the museums offers a slightly distorted view. 3 floors and some US army vehicles and tanks outside so well worth taking a look.
At the center of Saigon \ HCMC = a place to remind you what war can effect your life. War never be positive!
Interesting collection of Vietnam period war posters & photographs from Journalists from many countries - Some horrific including the effects of agent orange on the environment as well as the people.
Ein gut aufgebautes Museum um sich über die Geschichte Vietnams zu informieren. Es ist allerdings ziemlich bedrückend dort die Bilder der Opfer zu sehen.
Greit museum der det var brukt mye bilder. Godt forklart på engelsk både inne på museet og på utstilt krigs-materiell ute. Mange sterke historier.

This museum is a must do but not for the faint-hearted. Many moving and upsetting photos from the Vietnam war and the impact on 4th generation children still. Lots of very interesting stories and pictures from our memories.
The War Remanants Museum was very interesting - very neatly put exhibits, but keep in mind that the war is told from the North Vietnam side

Can recommend taking a walk in the city and just go into the museum for an hour or two. Entrance fee is only 20k dong and it is really worth the money. Terrifying, but interesting and real pictures of the Vietnam war. It shows the real victims of the war and the crimes committed with agent orange and the actual cruelty of the war.
Retrace bien les évènements de la guerre avec une forte suggestion anti américaine ... les habitants ont le sentiment d"avoir été abandonnés

It’s a beautiful museum with a lot of picture of victims of the war. But at the end of the trip we felt like very sad about al the pictures.. I think 75% were pictures of injured and mutilated people (children) 😢
Very interesting and a good way to learn more about this war experience from the Vietnam perspective. But also confrontational at times!
A place which showed the war weapons and photos of war victims. To someone who are eager to learn more about the history of Vietnam war. It is a good place to see.

I would give this 5 stars, but I just don't feel that the kind of things that happened to create this museum should have 5 stars. It was extremely well done, thorough and complete. It was well presented and displayed. It was also disturbing and horrifying. The brutality that people inflict on each other is somberly documented in thought provoking ways. It's not for the faint of heart, but it is a necessary and worthwhile stop.

As a Marine Corps veteran and a veteran of the Vietnam Conflict, I found the War Remnant Museum very interesting. That being said understand that displays in this museum are presented from a different perspective than one might have understood before. Differing points of view make for honest evaluation. Worth the trip.

Al vaker het nodige gelezen over de oorlog in Vietnam. Dit museum is een grote aanklacht van wat Amerika in Vietnam in die oorlog heeft gedaan. Of daar nu wel of geen goede redenen voor waren laat ik hier maar in het midden. De toegepaste middelen waren in ieder geval gruwelijk. Met name de chemische oorlogvoering heeft tot vreselijke gevolgen geleid. Daar geeft het museum een indrukwekkend verslag van. Maar wel bezien vanaf een kant. En dat mag naar mijn idee. Iedereen kwam stil naar buiten. Indrukwekkend en confronterend.

Learnt that Vietnamese kids and family is still suffering very badly from agent orange today. Been looking and learning more agent orange and about US military outside US after visit... and its not looking good...

We are family of 4 (daughters 17 years and 9 years). And we don't like museums. But, all of us liked this and had our respective opinions and reactions. What I can say convincingly is that this museum will definitely touch you emotionally in some manner.  Firstly about the museum. It has 3-4 floors. The first tip is that you have to start from the top most floor and then go by the room numbers serially. So, you go to the topmost floor and start with room 1 and then room 2. Once you finish with all the rooms in a particular floor, you go one floor below and follow the room numbers serially. This is a must else it will be completely as waste of a visit for you as you cannot understand anything. And the real story comes alive if you follow this way.  The museum is located very centrally. It is just a 10 minutes walk from the hotel we were staying at - Hoang Lan Hotel (please see my separate review of this place). It has 2 gates. The main gate has the ticket counter and the entry gate. It also has an exit gate. Then there is a 2nd gate which is only for exit. Both the gates are near each other so don't worry about landing up at any gate (this is specially for people who use maps or e-hailing cabs).  There is an outside compound which has many war air crafts, tanks, etc. This is very good for taking photograph. And then the main museum is indoors. Each floor of the museum has toilets. There is a lift. Then there is a cafe too on the ground floor near the 2nd exit entrance. The museum is nicely air conditioned and therefore becomes a good activity to do during a hot day. Btw, many reviews said that it becomes freezing cold inside. For us it didn't and was quite comfortable.  Now about the sections. From many reviews you would hear about the Agent Orange section being very somber. Well for me it was the War Crimes section which was more effecting. In fact, my 9 year old daughter was also so effected. She was ahead of me in covering the sections. And once at the War Crimes section she came to me and said - Papa, you must not go ahead because what you see is really bad and terrible. Imagine! Yes, this section is quite bad. I sincerely feel that the western forces did kill and mime this poor communist nation for not very valid reasons. Just because it is located strategically. No matter what people might say it was very stupid of a powerhouse like the USA to come and plunder a weak nation like Vietnam. Just don't understand this. Yes, the museum might be making the Americans look bad because the Vietnam government must be wanting to pass that kind of a message, but I still don't see any answers to the above question.  But in life there is hope. And Vietnam is gearing up to become a great nation. It is progressing rapidly in the region. And the country has very good people who are kind, well mannered, very intelligent, very tolerable. They also have a great sense of humor. They don't have any grudges. Also, for tourism purpose, they deliver the best service I have come across among all my travels. So, hats off to the people of Vietnam. They are really committed to go the extra mile to give you amazing service.  This is a good place to spend at least 2 hours. You can easily reach this place by Grab (e-hailing). We reached at round 4.30 pm and left at 6 pm when it starts to close down. The ticket pricing is very cheap and worth it. You don't really need any guide as everything is well exhibited with very appropriate and interesting notes. From the India perspective, there is a section whereby they credit India with supporting the movement against USA. And guess which state of India has been shown to give support? Ha ha. It is quite appropriately West Bengal considering the communist government which ruled the state for so long!!  About Ho Chi Minh City (HCMC)  - Frankly it is just like any other city - There is nothing much to do here. So, out of the 3 places we visited - Hanoi, Da Nang and HCMC, I feel the first two are worthy. Hanoi because of Halong Bay. Da Nang because of Hoi An and Ba Na Hills. Please see my detailed reviews of lots of places in all the 3 cities - restaurants, attractions, etc. - The only other place we found interesting in HCMC is Cu Chi Tunnels (please see my separate review of that). It is really good. But the only problem is that this requires almost 7 hours (8 am to 3 pm). We did 3 activities in one Day - visited Cu Chi Tunnels which is outside the city, then had lunch back in the city and then visited War Remnants Museum in the afternoon. Then we went back to the hotel and rested a while before coming out for dinner at Rice Field Restaurant (please see my separate review of this place). Phew! It was quite a hectic day for us. But, we really enjoyed and it was worth it. - Like Hanoi, there is lots of traffic in HCMC. Lots of motor bikes. And they come from all directions and you will feel as if they are going to go all over you like insects!! But, in reality it is ok. You get to handle it. And we Indians are quite used to it so it didn't bother us much. Traffic is like it is now-a-days in any city grappling with old infrastructure. HCMC is no different. So, any average place in HCMC takes anything from 15-30 minutes. So, please plan accordingly. Traffic around the Malls can be very bad. We had lunch at Pizza 4P's @ Saigon Centre Mall. It was horrific to reach that mall. Also, we got terribly delayed at Pizza 4P's because of their slow service. - We happened to cross the square near Ho Chi Minh City Hall in the evening while take a ride from our hotel to Rice Field Restaurant. Must say that this square is a very happening place in the evening. Very nicely decorated with lots of shops with bright lights and with the middle of the square open to walking without any vehicles. It is a must visit place. We had no plans to go there, but because we liked it so much while crossing it we decided to walk down to the square after our dinner. Rice Field Restaurant was just walking distance from the square so very convenient. We spent a good 1-1.5 hours at the square. We also had very good cheese cake ice cream at the square. Forgetting the name of the place. - The weather at HCMC is towards the warmer side. Actually when we went it was quite pleasant more towards the warmer side. I think it remains warm all through the year unlike Hanoi which gets quite cold in December. - Distance from the airport to other parts of the city is an average of 30 minutes. In the morning before 8.30 am traffic to airport is quite reasonable. But, in the evening you need to be careful a bit. Because evening traffic like any other city is quite busy. - Grab (e-hailing) is easily available. The car will arrive at worst after 5 minutes. Also, the pricing is very reasonable compared to other Grab cities like Singapore, Malaysia and Thailand. So, you can readily use Grab here. - In fact, pricing for everything in Vietnam is very reasonable. You can easily have a lavish meal without like going bankrupt. In fact, after some time you will stop bothering about the price at all. Only thing is that their currency Dong is very small in value. So, you can easily have a meal which will be worth VND 1 million which is worth USD 43 only! So, in Vietnam you can be a millionaire without making an effort.
Ett måste om man är i Ho Chi Minh. Mycket intressant och samtidigt fruktansvärt. En del riktigt hemska bilder. Men man kan inte blunda för vår historia.
I found this museum pretty interesting and sad! The photos shocked me and all the information. Cheap entrance so worth a look in Saigon! 1 to 2 hours all you need.

W 2018 widziałem Muzeum Wojny w Hanoi Jest tam więcej eksponatów uzbrojenia samochodów itp Ale w Ho Chi Minh wrażenie robi wystawa zdjęć o użytym przez Amerykanów napalmie Niektóre zdjęcia są wstrząsające i napewno nie dla każdego.

One should definitely visit the museum when you are in Saigon. Just because there is little else to see and the history of the country has been shaped by it. Visited with my wife and we found the museum very interesting. There are english information cards beside every exhibit and photo. En effet c'est un musée à faire quand on est de passage à ho chi minh. Des photos sont dures à voir mais c'est la réalité ...
En fruktansvärd historia som behöver få synas. Hemska bilder för yngre barn men nyttigt med informationen. Vissa bilder valde vi bort för 10-åringarna ändå. Väl värt ett besök!
Vi havde en god oplevelse og museet kan besøges uden guide. Dog kræver det, at man kan læse engelsk for at få noget ud af besøget.

Very well done and certainly worth a trip when visiting Ho Chi Minh City. Some of the displays are very harrowing and hard to look at. Difficult to come away without feeling emotional about what went on and admiring the resilience of the Vietnamese people. I have given it 4 out of 5 because I would have liked to see some acknowledgement of the cruelty inflicted by the Vietnamese on the captured American troops. Certainly there is no doubt of the barbaric nature of the American strategy, in particular the use of napalm and agent orange and the toll this is taking even now, decades later in terms of birth defects. The lack of reference to the Vietnamese acts leaves a gap – a strong people should be able to admit to this.

museo che riassume la guerra con gli usa . tutto molto vero e alcune foto fanno veramente male , nonostante siano passati tanti anni. di parte perchè è visto solo con ottica vietnord e presenta anche cose inverosimili. comunque un pezzo di storia da ricordare e vedere
We had day trip to visit arround Ho Chi Minh city.And War museum is one of nice place where worth to go.

Having knew nothing about the war before my visit it was all a bit of a shock! The pictures and photos really show the harrowing truth plus all the exhibits (if you go outside past the various aeroplanes you will see what happened in Phu Quoc). There is a lot of reading so it's not hugely interactive but it's really informative and well worth visiting. It is sad though that we seem to learn nothing from these wars and shocking how cruel the human race can be towards each other.

Zunächst was mir nicht so gefiel. Es zog sich kein direkter roter Faden durch das Museum, der einem ein wenig vorgibt, wie man es strategisch am besten durchlaufen sollte. Zudem hat der Hintergrund der Kriege gefehlt - warum, wieso, weshalb. Eine "Einleitung" wäre nicht verkehrt gewesen.  Sehr gut war die Anschaulichkeit, wenngleich auch grausam und deprimierend. Wie die angeblich schlauste Spezies imstande ist, solch Gräueltaten zu verrichten, ich verstehe es einfach nicht.  Es werden viele Kriegsfotografien gezeigt, man sollte also nicht unbedingt mit Kindern dorthin gehen.

I found our tour of the War Remnant Museum very interesting. There is a yard filled with American battle remnants including tanks and jets. All of these were left behind after the war. Inside are several rooms with Vietnam War photographs and displays that are quite well done. Keep in mind this museum is run by a communist government and only portrays the evils that the Americans did during the war (which were many) and makes the Vietnamese side of the story look like they were complete saints. It is fascinating to see communist propaganda up close and personal.  As long as you understand the nature of the information there is much to see here and the photography is amazing. It is really sad to see the atrocities that were done from the Vietnamese point of view. I think a great way of balance is to watch Ken Burns Vietnam War documentary. He's a straight shooter and doesn't candy coat anything. Hopefully we can all learn from this history because it tends to repeat itself.
Shocking because of the politics and terrible loss of life and long term and on going effects of the Vietnam war. It is well laid out and needs a couple of hours to go round

Let me summarize: the Diem and Thuc governments and the French/US forces fighting in Vietnam were evil while the Vietcong were angels. Clearly, not all birth defect and medical condition during/after the war were caused by agent orange (although some likely were) and the My Lai massacre, while an atrocity, was an isolated event (although about 1/20th of the museum is devoted to it). Nevertheless, those who forget history are doomed to repeat it. No matter your nationality, the museum raises good questions that we all need to keep considering in our current environment. I overheard a older US gentleman (maybe fought during the war?) say that the US lost the war due to the close news coverage ignoring the corrupt S. Vietnamese governments (Catholic actively suppressing the Buddhist majority) and the dedication of the Vietnamese people to reunite their divided country. These are conversations that need to continue to avoid similar mistakes. The War Remnant Museum encourages that conversation.

Das Museum zeigt in beeindruckenden und bedrückenden Bildern die Grausamkeiten des Vietnam-Krieges; ergänzt um viele Berichte und Fakten. Nach meiner Einschätzung sind insbesondere die Bilder und Hintergrundinformationen die "wichtigsten" Eindrücke um sich ein Bild der damaligen Kriegssituation machen zu können. A good museum, but not very good at giving information about why the war started or happened, who was involved etc. Also didnt explain much on the Vietnamese methods, just focused on the Americans and the bad they did. cool planes and tanks outside though.
