Na zewnątrz czołgi, samoloty i repliki więziennych cel, wewnątrz 3 piętra wystawy składającej się głównie ze zdjęć. Większość traumatycznych. Nie wychodzi się z tego miejca w dobrym humorze, ale nie można go pominąć.

Highly informative. Very poignent. Some horrible photographs and stories. A "no-holds-barred" exposure to the attrocities of the war. Only critism is that the exhibitions are a little disconnected. It's a little hard to keep track of the chronology.

Fighter jets, tanks, helicoptors, guns guns guns ..... There was a lot of stuff left behind by the retreating Americans. I was reminded of the scene in the movie " The Deer Hunter" where the last heli rises from the US embassy compound. Even so, the commentary on the exhibits , which were well kept and presented , had a local " we won, they were bad and also losers" flavour . Well liberation wasn't on the US agenda. The story of the conflict lacked a real history to anchor the actual reason and its evolution. The hardware was evident, as was the cavalier attitude of the US to Vietnams neighbors shown in maps of the carpet bombing which took place. The local atrocities on the other locals was not mentioned. Overall I was educated , reminded of the tragedy of war, shocked by its brutality , given enough to see how much hypocrisy there is in politics and left to wonder at how the future will unfold.
This is a huge reminder of the affect that the war had on everyone involved. The pictures and history make it worth the visit.

spend an hour or so looking through this museum. Largely based on photos and propaganda, very interesting

To visit, not to forget that peace has no price and to remember that man, however good he may be, is capable of the worst atrocities in the name of an ideal not always very clear.

This is my second visit to the War Remnants Museum in Ho Chi Minh City, and I found it just as emotionally stirring and poignant as my first visit 5 years ago.  The reality of the devastation of the Vietnam War is laid out using hundreds of photographs - there are also stories of friendship and international support within the exhibits, which is very much needed as a lot of the images and stories are chilling. War Remnants Museum  Ho Chi Minh City  Voldsom men god oplevelse  743696482  Dette er et MUST for alle der besøger Vietnam. Interesting museum with various military vehicles to see. It’s not air-conditioned so I found it a bit hot even inside.
The exhibits were informative and often very emotive which is to be expected given the subject.

There is zero consideration of the opposing forces view point or mention of any of the wider issues.

Inside, are mostly photographic records of the atrocities committed by US troops and the use of chemical agents (orange, Purple etc) and the continued effects of these chemicals on the people, even decades following the war. A good place for the younger generations to visit and learn about the struggle their country has gone through under occupation of other nations. Well maintained with some of the arms and ammunition used in warfare on display.

However, I felt this place was not for me due to the numerous graphic images and photographs (corpses, dead babies, tortured prisoners of war, and blown-up soldiers) covering the walls, while information about the war is quite minimal. Cheap entry and we learnt a lot about the war. We found it very upsetting,  understand that war happens but how people treated each other was horrific.

This is a very strange place for Americans to visit. It was put together by the Vietnamese government to showcase the American War Crimes of the war. I would have liked to see info showing how the Americans became involved but it is a very one sided viewpoint. Definitely worth checking out but do some research before you go here to be more informed. It’s difficult to describe this place. As a US citizen I was taught a certain version of history. I’ve always been interested in the other side of the story. I was very excited to come see the war remnants museum. But I believe there is a lot of anti-American propaganda passed off as facts here. There is an array of amazing history with tanks, aircraft, helicopters, mines, guns, and grenades. But the historical facts seem a bit twisted. There was a lot to see here and the price was very decent. But I absolutely felt like an outcast here. There’s a lot of anti American photos, documents, quotes, and artifacts. Whether they are 100% accurate or not is up for opinion. I’m not tying to be biased with my review because I am American. I’m not here giving my opinion about the war. Whether it was right, wrong, or indifferent. I am simply saying I came to this museum for the other side of the coin and maybe I wasn’t ready for it. Just be warned as an American, you will feel like a bad guy here. But the museum itself was laid out nicely. Trying to give this an “average” score in attempt to not influence its overall rating. This place is definitely worth a visit, but I must admit it was very emotional. I am glad that Vietnam are stoic, able to present the horror they went through, but it is very confronting. It’s a day for thick skin.

**The Ho Chi Minh War Remnants Museum: A Masterclass in Guilt Tourism with a Side of Irony**

Let’s be honest: the **War Remnants Museum** in Ho Chi Minh City is less a *museum* and more a **photo album of American regrets**, curated with the subtlety of a sledgehammer. If you’re here for a balanced historical narrative, you’ve stumbled into the wrong gallery. This place is Vietnam’s ultimate flex—a triumphant, unapologetic shrine to “*Look What You Did*,” complete with enough graphic imagery to make even the most stoic visitor question humanity.

### 1. **"Exhibit A: Your Atrocities, Courtesy of Your Own Cameras"**
The museum’s pièce de résistance? Its *extensive collection of American-sourced photos* documenting war crimes. Who needs original artifacts when you can weaponize the enemy’s own snapshots? From the iconic image of the napalm-scarred girl fleeing naked to jars of formaldehyde-preserved deformed fetuses (thanks, Agent Orange!), the curation screams: *“We didn’t even need to Photoshop—you did this to yourselves!”* . It’s like a morbid episode of *Antiques Roadshow*, where every relic comes with a side of moral indictment.

### 2. **DIY Trauma: Low-Budget, High-Impact**
Forget climate-controlled galleries or interactive displays. This museum runs on **sweat and existential dread**. Visitors huddle under wheezing fans, chugging water like it’s holy communion, while toddlers nap obliviously next to photos of dismembered soldiers. The lack of aesthetic finesse? A *feature*, not a bug. Why bother with fancy exhibits when a rusty Huey helicopter and a few propaganda posters can do the heavy lifting? It’s the *IKEA of war museums*—assemble your own trauma! .

### 3. **Agent Orange: The Gift That Keeps on Giving**
The museum’s crown jewel is its **Agent Orange exhibit**—a cheerful reminder that chemical warfare is the gift that keeps on giving (for generations, literally). Learn how the U.S. turned Vietnam into a science experiment, spraying enough dioxin to make Monsanto blush. The pièce de résistance? A diorama of limbless dolls and jars of deformed fetuses, because nothing says “*Never Again*” like a formaldehyde-soaked nightmare. Bonus points for the *subtle* caption: “*Thanks for the mutations, Uncle Sam!*” .

### 4. **War Tourism with a Side of Schadenfreude**
This isn’t just a museum—it’s Vietnam’s **victory lap**. While American tourists shuffle through, muttering apologies under their breath, the subtext is clear: *“You came, you bombed, you lost.*” The museum’s original name, *“Museum of American War Crimes,”* was softened for diplomatic PR, but the vibe remains unchanged. It’s like renaming a haunted house “*Spooky Fun Zone*” and still filling it with jump scares .

### 5. **The Irony of Humanitarian Heroism**
Amid the carnage, don’t miss the **Buang-Ly exhibit**—a token feel-good story about a South Vietnamese pilot rescued by a *noble U.S. Navy captain*. It’s the museum’s cheeky nod to American exceptionalism: *“Sure, we dropped 80 million liters of poison, but look how nice we are to this one family!”* Cue the violins and strategic amnesia about the 500,000+ birth defects still plaguing the countryside .

### Final Verdict: **A+ for Audacity, C- for Nuance**
The War Remnants Museum is less a historical archive and more a **theatrical indictment**—a place where nuance goes to die, replaced by visceral, guilt-tripping spectacle. It’s the ultimate “*Well, well, well, if it isn’t the consequences of my actions*” experience. Come for the shock value, stay for the existential crisis. Just don’t expect a balanced perspective—unless you count *“America: Bad, Vietnam: Victorious”* as nuanced discourse.

**Rating**: ★★★☆☆ (Would lose a star for lack of air conditioning, but the moral high ground is breezy enough.)

Interesting to see the impact of the Vietnam/American war however felt as though it is a very narrow focus on the impact of the war on ordinary people. The museum shows in a heap of detail the effects of such a horrible war and has on display some of the full size aircraft and tanks a long with multi level museum building covering the stories of soldiers and the effects the war had on the population.
It is very graphic and mostly showing one side of the war.
If you are into the history of Vietnam it’s certainly an eye opener and worth some time .
The cost to enter is quiet cheap .

War Remnants Museum in Ho Chi Minh City in Vietnam is one of the many options to go to when you're in this city. Here, you will see how peace prevails over hate and strife and how the Vietnamese people continue to safeguard peace in their land.

You can spend an hour or two inside the museum before heading to your next stop. Do not miss this place when you're in HCMC! :)
Depressing but important reminder how any war really looks like. Mostly photos expositions. You can easilly pay with cash or card for your ticket.
You can finish the museum within 45mins. Not really something I’m expecting but you can drop by if you have the time.

It is interesting to see weapons, tanks and plains from the Vietnam war, but don't waste time reading and listening to what the vietnamese government says, they also talk about their side of the story and don't say all the truth.

The coolest is probably the tanks and the choppers displayed on the exterior. Within the building, talks about the war, one-sided - of course. Ken burns documentary on Netflix - watch that, it’s amazing lol

I like those military arsenal at the courtyard in front of the museum, be it Chinnock helicopter, F5-A fighter plane, or artillery.

The mock up of the jail is also interesting.

Within, there are mainly photos and news clippings spreading over 3 floors. Need patience to read through all. Otherwise half an hour is enough to go through.
While there are many soul searching exhibits, the content is mostly propaganda. So much more could be done to explain the geopolitical context of this most horrific war.

Is a very sad place to visit and see hundreds of images of the consequences of war. Was to much mental pollution for me. Things that never crossed my mind i saw there. Is difficult not to cry after been there

I was expecting more of a focus on the weapons and technologies of the war however the focus seemed more on the effects of agent orange and war crimes of the Americans worth while of anyone to see was very interesting
A lot of info in form of pictures.  Some war-used equipment and tanks. Not a photo person so dint enjoy much .

Learn bout the suffering Vietnam people had to go through during war. It's really sad. The american basically uses chemical warfare which in effect, lead to the suffering of many generations to come; birth defects, poisoned land. Suffering can still be seen today
A nice museum to understand more about the vietnamese war. The museum holds a good collection of planes, tanks and other artifacts from the vietnamese war and the first indochinese  war. Recommended

I wish I could give it a better review, but there was not a lot of information about all the  artifacts and some of the buttons on the  informational videos didn't work or were only 2 seconds long... So it was difficult to read.  But I would still recommend visiting the museum just be ready to do some web searches if you want more detail.
Outside had some exhibits, but inside mainly 3 floors of somewhat biased pictures.

A real mind opener for anyone who visits Vietnam. It really drives into what happened and there is a lot to learn. However the information available doesn't describe much to anyone who knows nothing, so I'd advise you do a small bit of research or watch films prior. Entry is 40,000
Interesting that the museum, admires the US for it's freedom of speech, even during war(except notably for the Kent state killings).

Well worth a visit but not for the faint hearted. More of a pictorial account of the war than I expected but more informative for it. We took the audio tour which perhaps had too much info about some aspects such as the photographers and if you wanted to listen to it all you’d need a whole day.
A truly touching experience however and a valuable insight into a resilient nation that has been through so much.

It's kinda meh.

You walk around, look at photos of variuse things, read text associated to the photo that's filled with propaganda. Then you can walk around and can look at some tanks and planes. And then you get hussled for money.

It's horrific what happened and they do get the message across. But it could have been a lot better shown. Just a timeline of the war would be useful, and more artifact to look at would be more interesting.

Watch any random 3hour documentary about the war if you actually want to learn anything.

It is able to listen to ‘Imagine’ and watch anti war protest on the screen on the ground floor again and again

It is not big museum, only three stories building but it has tons of agony,sadness and heartbreaking grief ... and history that should not happen again ...

But need to make better facility to look around easy

The museum gives an overwhelming overview of the intervention of the USA in the Indochina war. There you can find very explicit photographic material from the war, and also samples of machinery, weaponry, and war gear used by the USA troops during the war. There are many interesting facts and information provided about the conflict.  However, if you are looking for a more holistic and comprehensive vision of the conflict, that is not the right place. There were many other actors involved in the war, who also committed many crimes, which are not mentioned anywhere in the museum.
Not for the faint of heart. Some extremely graphic photographs. Definitely not for children. Not inaccurate but tells a very one sided narrative.
Detailed museum on the impact the US had. However, it is biased and lacked information such as timeline of events, the stories from the other side, etc
Place was good with all the remnants being showcased. There need to be more helpful staff possibly explaining the exhibits.
Very interesting and sad, with loads of information and exhibits. However, not very well air conditioned so was quite hot.

A place to read and see the atrocities of Vietnam War and Indochina War. Let's hope everyone who visits this museum learns that there is never right in a war. As a civilised society, it is our obligation to ensure that history does not repeat itself.

Most of the exhibits in the building is photography and story about vietnam war, with some displays of weapons used in the war. In the courtyard there is collection of tanks, fighter jets and helicopters of the us army.

Shows how Vietnam fought the war with usa and what damages they suffered .mostly represented in form of pictures .out side they displayed vietnam side tanks and aircrafts one side .other side US air crafts used . It will give idea about war history and Vietnam sufferings during the war . The rooms are air conditioned and wash rooms available and ticked entry around three dollars .
Historical weapon of US. This museum is built on the famous pogoda of Vietnam.

The war remnants museum has done a very good job of  preserving history and sharing an often hidden part of history. The air conditioning however was not working and the museum itself was like a sauna. There were also no “trigger warnings” at any point, only after we paid did we discover the museum was not suitable for young children.

There was however an air conditioned children’s playroom, which was convenient but I probably would have avoided paying the entry fee and done something else with my younger child had I have known

Will be much better when the AC is fixed.

This is a busy place and to have 40 people in a non AC room it is crazy hot.

Good museum and the audio guide is very good but let down by the current maintenance activities

A very sad place.

Not sure having people begging inside the compound is really a good hallmark
Nothing really excited see here. Just lots of pictures
photos on 3rd floor were the best part. only a few of the rooms have AC. as you would imagine, story told from Vietnamese side of the Vietnam war so biased in that way

Lots of information and gives an insight into the Vietnam war. Not particularly engaging but the war vehicles outside were cool.
40,000 Dong per person.

Other than outdoor weapons display, the indoor exhibitions have too much to read and too little to watch. I doubt many visitors would have time or passion to stop and read through. A media room with big screen showing war histories would be much more interesting and attractive.
An insightful exhibit about the horrors of the Vietnam War. Worth going once. Graphic images of Agent Orange and War victims however.
No air conditioning, crowded, dated presentation. But moving and important to see

Remember, it is always the story of the winning side of a war that is told. This is a pure propaganda museum. But still very interesting. Both sides did awful things to each other. This was a civil war and both sides had their "sugar dadys". The South vietnamese army had USA and the winning side north vietnam had Sovjet union.

Hot. Very hot

They are making tonnes of $$$ why don't they install some aircon
The museum is basically a collection of extremely graphic pictures to illustrate the war crimes by the US soldiers in Vietnam. I went on Sunday afternoon, and it was waaaaay too crowded (see picture)
Very biased, but expected it to be. Every war will blame the atrocities on the other side, forgetting everything that they did. Don't take dollars or credit cards for entry!
Really interesting place lots for I for action that really hits home for the people of Vietnam

Not so big.
In 30min you have seen everything.

My personal preference would be to skip this while on vacation the last thing you need to do is to be reminded of the glorification of War and the America's participation. This is a beautifully laid out Museum however I had issues about what I saw it took me out of my happy place
Interesting place to find out all about the war, can get busy

A lot of interesting information about the war. But I think the image that you get after visiting this museum is that North Vietnam was only a victim in the war and the USA was only doing horrible things. I don't know the whole story and all the facts, so it could be true. But I left hungry for other points of view.
So if you're looking for a lot of informatio, this is a good place, but be aware it is one sided info.

Must see museum on the Vietnam war, with one side vision of the conflict. The museum is very graphic with some powerful photos so some parental discretion is advised. Overall you shouldn’t miss it although the museum itself feels a little old with very little use of more modern tecnologias to improve the experience

This is worth seeing but it’s also a bit disappointing.

It’s relatively small as museums go and was pretty crowded on a weekday. Some of the exhibits are heart wrenching, some are unfortunately difficult to make out (small, blurry).

I am not an apologist for the Americans - they did some pretty atrocious and unforgivable things in the war - but the museum left out (whitewashed) the role the Vietnamese government played in the war. This was in stark contrast to my recent visits to Hiroshima and Berlin, where a full and unabashed accounting was made.

Museum is worth a visit. I would have spent longer there only it is not air conditioned and there is limited seating and restaurant areas to sit and take a break. I was surprised at the lack of facilities given this is one of the top attractions in the city.

It was interesting and is engaging for a couple hours. Don't expect too much on the actual cause and timeline of the war though. Not sure I left much more educated on the war. And approach with a critical stance - seems pretty bias with strong Anti-America leanings as well as a complete dismissal of any wrong doing by North Vietnam and the communist party. But some pretty evocative photos and heart-rendering stories.
Museum exibiting artifact of Vietnam War. Tourists attractions.

The content of the museum is really good. It is worth a visit for sure. My 3 star review is based on the condition of the museum that clearly needs more funding to be upgraded and put inline with other museums quality. This will for sure increase the quality of the content and the experience for the visitors to bring back more information out of it.
Pretty meh… I found it was just basically a museum that slams the US but doesn’t really talk about what terrible things the other side did. Not a lot of stuff to look at either.

Decent place to see if you're in Saigon (now known as Ho Chi Minh). You can see captured American planes and tanks and all. Also you'll see how dioxins (Agent Orange) were used extensively on the Vietnamese by Americans and how they're still facing the consequences of the same.
Not so big as I expected! But needs to visit if you goes to Ho Chi Minh

It's okay. They tell the war story from the communist party perspective, be ready to go and swallow their narrative. Should investigate elsewhere to have broader perspective.

They have audio guide as well that complement nicely the gallery. Although the gallery consist mainly of photos and text.

The old military vehicles in the yard look grat and give a nice insight. So does the part about the imprisonment procedures. However the central part is does not feel to well structured and a bit jumbled like an endless stream of school posters put up across three floors, which just is not as immersive and exciting as it could be given the context.

Compared to war museums in other countries, especially those in Europe that experienced WWI/WWII, this one is small, and only has a very limited amount of artifacts. It consists of mostly photos, many of which can be seen on the web. It tries to be educational but if you are already well versed on this subject matter, you might see a lot of propaganda. On the flip side, this is not crowded and some parts have AC.

Too many info to read. Will take you a long time if you are interested in history BUT I doubt anyone would be there for more than 3 hours due to the place is very hot. The air conditioning not cooling enough. No elevator only stairs so it's kinda tricky for old people or wheelchair bound to reach other exhibitions on higher level.

I was hoping for a more informative narrative on the Vietnam conflict.  Perhaps mention the role of the French colonisation, WWII and the communist vs western ideologies.  Instead we seemed to just get told how evil the Americans were.  The museum itself only had airconditioning in some rooms which really struggled under the heat.  The displays of aircraft, tanks and other armoured vehicles outside was the best part for me.

A lot of information, quotes and photos but the timeline of the war is not really talked about so if you do not already have a good knowledge of the war you will be slightly confused. The information is also clearly written from a Vietnamese stand point, painting the Americans in a very bad light.

Great collection of images, artifacts, and information about the war. Have rarely seen more informative museums. However, the museum has a practice of closing at 11.30am and reopening at 1.30pm so if you're in the middle of your tour you have to get out. The museum staff don't tell you that in advance and neither does the timing mentioned outside the museum.

Understand that staff needs to have lunch in which they could do shifts or advise on the time in advance and not let visitors in after a certain time.
Do Not Expect More than a Photo Gallery and Very few Artifacts of Vietnam-USA War time. Tanks and helicopters outside are good for Pictures. Only good for History Lovers.

I liked it and I think you should definitely visit and read up on the history. I did though think it was a little bit hard to understand the English and from which viewpoint I was reading from. I think you probably need to read up on it a little bit before because I didn’t know anything and therefore it was hard to understand and as said, the English and choice of words in the text.

But it is relatively cheap so definitely visit!
Could be more educational, the structure is not always clear. Pictures play more a role of emotional impact.

Not the best museum I visited. It mainly has photographs, some of them very disturbing. I was expecting to see what weapons were used, how the war evolved year by year and physical artifacts of key moments. I know war is terrible but I also don’t think it’s a good idea to show photos of hundreds of mutilated people to get this point across. There could be better ways to convey a message of peace.

Its OK ..
For those interested in Vietnam history it's very good ..
For others it's OK.
Learnt about Agent Orange which I didn't know .. it's devastating and depressing to see the results of this ..
Just depends on your interests ..

If you don't have a history of the wars in Vietnam, you may want to read up on them before visiting. The exhibits jumped right into details with no background or explanations for why certain countries were involved in various disputes, which made it hard to understand the context until I took a break and did some background reading. Overall a rather grim museum, but still interesting and well curated exhibits. Be sure you are comfortable standing for at least an hour as the chairs to rest are not located inside of any exhibits. All exhibits and materials has English translations, though the entire museum frowns upon the US involvement in all capacities.

Lot to see and read about. Can easily spend about 2-3 hours but the place is so stuffed and no enough ventilation, due to which we had to come out midway. It was very suffocating and stinky. Something needs to be done for good air inside the museum.

This place is not recommended for young children. It is quite a boring museum, except for if you're really interested in the vietnam war and if you have a lot of patience. There are many impressive stories and photos in this museum. Entry is not that expensive, and you get to look at some cool airplanes, helicopters and howitzers. It shows the vietnam war from the side of the Vietnamese people, and it's very impressive

Would be more suitably named ‘The Vietnam Anti-Imperialist USA’ museum? The role of South Vietnam during the war is sort of erased from the exhibits; the narrative being that the USA decided alone to wage war upon Vietnam. Highlights are US military hardware with no Vietnamese remnants to speak of.

Overall, still worth a visit for the cultural and histroical significance as much as anything.
It's pretty good overall - worth a visit if youre in Saigon. I wouldn't make it the main thing though.

The museum was interesting to visit. The captured American vehicles and tanks were very impressive. And the photo collection was very interesting but a bit gruesome sometimes. I would not recommend that you bring your children here.

This war was for sure a nightmare to Vietnamese people, but this museum is pretty one sided and shows only the US and their allied Vietnamese being bad and committing war crimes. There is almost no information about the Vietcong side, as if they wouldn’t have killed anyone. So it seems it is quite propaganda driven. Nevertheless it still shows what humans are able to do to each other…. and the huge problems with unexploded ammo until today. Tanks and Airplanes outside are interesting.

Museum was fantastic, however the staff at the ticket booth, 1 particular lady is extremely rude. I have witnesses and heard from multiple people that the woman grunts at you as you try to pay and throws any change at you. It is not welcoming for tourists.
Nice collection of left overs (remnants) of USA military aggression across the globe !!

Although the exhibits were meaningful and full of information, I thought the museum could have more of a flow to it. By which I mean that if you go here without a solid understanding of the war, as far as I could tell, the museum doesn't give a good story to the war and tell you what happened step by step, but rather gives information about various cross sections of the war such as the war crimes. With this being said, we only spent 45 minutes or so in the museum so perhaps with more time to fully explore all of the exhibits, I would have gathered a better story of the war.

A moving experience and you'll leave with a very good understandingof the Vietnameseperspectiveof the war. A very old school type of museum, with little to no air conditioning so bring water and a towel, and all static displays and photos - not your modern museum. Outdoors, it is rather Smithsonian-esque with the tanks, howitzers, choppers and jets but that's where the comparison ends. Not a sophisticated museum but made a tremendous impact. It's a must visit.
A great place to see how humankind is so bad to one another

It's nice that such a museum exists and it contains a lot of information that the Americans try to hide about what happened as a result of the war with some very graphic photos. However this museum is more than 90% photos on walls, not much else. A well made website could easily replace the experience unfortunately.

Very very graphic, and a lot to take in at once. Each room has so much detail from a Vietnamese perspective ( rightly so) but its overwhelming amount of information.

Wouldn't recommend taking children here.

Some good, some bad. Mostly pictures, which are very moving but not enough text to contextualize some things. Really shows the brutality of war but doesn't give much information on the causes or background of the war, nor the resolution. Would highly recommend reading up on the Vietnam-American war as well as the First Indochina war before you visit in order to understands what you are seeing. Wikipedia filled in a lot of knowledge gaps the museum left behind.

The museum succeeds in presenting the barren side of war and is not glorifying it. On the other hand they definitely have taken sides in presenting the different participants of the war. The French and Americans were bad exploiters, and the destruction and cruelties caused by them are well visible. Instead, the museum does not show Vietnam’s part in the war too well. There’s a lot of, I mean a lot of, cruel and sad pictures from war in the three floors of the main building. Be prepared to ve disgusted. We thought that there were way too many similar pictures and we got tired after first floor we scoured through.

We also hoped much more stories about the history and the motives of the wars. There were some introduction to the pre Vietnam war time but that was pretty much it. The rest of the texts were more about describing the pictures or quoting some important persons.

The museum tell the story of vietnamese war (between USA and Vietnam). I think it still need some improvement to make more attractive. The museum not inform us the story systematically so i a bit confused with the exhibits. And better they need to have like dioramas to show us how the ambiance of the war.
Static, old-fashioned, but intrested. Without interactivation.

The museum itself is interesting and eye opening. Prepare yourself for a heavy experience with lots of very graphic images. Outside you get to see all the tanks, planes and choppers.
Once you step inside the building is divided in 3 different floors and each floor has a few rooms, all of them with different themes.
I found this way of displaying things unorganized which made it hard to follow a certain route. In my opinion there should be a little map explaining in which rooms you go first instead of having only people wandering around all the rooms

Still found it highly politicized. Was expecting that tho. There seemed to be an overlap with dates back to 1955 which would be the French occupation. I did find the section about the Journalists interesting. Many pictures of children with Downs syndrome and other maladies which I felt would be seen in any cross section if any country. Not so sure it's a consequence of Agents orange. Regardless, war is a terrible thing. That's why we should avoid it at ALL costs. Vietnam deserves it's freedom they have paid dearly for it. Ho Chi Minh's Declaration of Independence was most touching. I remember seeing it in a documentary years ago. Bless❤️

Harrowing photos and stories about the war, learnt a lot and definitely agree with the museum's main purpose to keep the horrors in our memory so it never happens again. The first floor (and a fair number of captions) about support for North Vietnam was extremely biased, I didn't think it was necessary to see photos of mostly communist parties from around the world showing their support and it was a bit dull after the first 50 photos. It was pretty busy when we visited on a weekday, think allowing photos of graphic exhibits/photos is strange (the people wanting a photo of a dead child are the strange ones though) and also blocks up the flow of people. Worth a visit.

While not being factually wrong the museum neglected to show or mention Vietnamese violence. The photos and stories are interesting but trying to hide parts of the truth to appear as the good guys is a serious wrongdoing. Go here if you want to see some of the horrors of the war bit not if you want the WHOLE story.

The "non-CNN" edition of the Vietnam War..

Very intense experience. Takes an objective look on the Vietnam War from the viewpoint of the homeland (ie. Vietnam). Fair representation of the tragedy and suffering on both sides, not to mention the war crimes against the local civil population.

Exceeds all expectations.

Shall be a must for educational purposes for all generations.

Educational experience to see the war from the Vietnamese side. Downside of the story is that there is a staff member who had his limbs blown up by a mine when he was a kid. Instead of just being a cool guy and speaking about the museum, he told me to give him money because I was American - implying that it was my fault he lost his hands. This was upon entering the museum after 5 minutes. That threw me off for the whole museum visit.

A museum based mainly on photographs. It was interesting, but felt rather repetitive with only reading short descriptions and looking at photos.
We felt we wanted more information about the war, reasons it started etc.
Good for the price.

I stopped in here to duck out from the rain storm.  It was an interesting place to walk around for about an hour or so.  Having grown up in the US, it was interesting to learn another perspective on this influential war.  I particularly enjoyed the photography by Phillip Jones Griffith.  I don't believe there was a fee for the museum.

Was hoping to read more about the south vietnamese perspective on the war but there is little to no information about them since it still is a communist country. Still very informative on the war and it's effects on the people and country of Vietnam.

Very interesting visit, but for people who have an awareness of history, it is easy to note that the Museum negates to include certain atrocities committed by the Vietnamese. It seems very one sided, which is understandable, but it would be much more educational if it pesented a non-biased approach allowing visitors to come to their own judgement. Nevertheless, it is very sorrowing for those who are no aware of the Vietnam war and the struggles faced by the people of Vietnam still today.

Newly renovated with refurbished outdoor exhibits of military aircraft and armoured vehicles. The museum building has little ventilation and no air conditioning, which is uncomfortable in this climate.

The planes and tanks outside are well preserved and give a great sense of scale. There is a photo exhibition dedicated to photographers who lost their lives during the war with some amazing shots.
I would say images of trauma and birth defects are overrepresented. I understand their significance but the way they're presented is extremely blunt and confronting, especially when children are freely admitted.

Reminder of the pointlessness of war and the cruelty humans are capable of. Although familiar with the Vietnam war it would have been useful to have a time line so that the images and artifacts could be placed in some sort of context. Clear English translations help understand the displays

the museum is interesting if you like history.
there are a lot of pictures from the war… the museum is not hot they dont ve aircondition so make sure u drink enough before u go ;)

Interesting place with loads of information about very difficult times of Vietnamese history.
Some (if not majority) of photograps are very graphic and drastic - be careful if you want to take your children with you.
And finally, all information is provided with far too much of pathos and in far too propagandist style.

As an American in Vietnam, this museum provided a glimpse into the other side of the narrative shared by the Western world. Regardless of the politics, motives and history, this museum did an excellent job of portraying the attrocities of war. It is sad to see the violence human beings are capable of engaging in. The reason for only three stars is because many of the exhibits are badly worn down and the translations and the narrative of the history is pretty bad. It helped to have Wikipedia on hand to get a better understanding of the history of these wars.

An interesting journey through a blemish on mankind's history. The museum looks at the Vietnamese side of the war and there is some propaganda but mostly historically factual information on the destructive nature of war and how it still affects the people to this day. Not for the faint hearted. Preferably don't take your kids as there are a lot of graphic images.

Some interesting information and shocking photos from the war. The layout however makes it difficult to manoeuvre around when there are large tour groups. The information is in long prose and written in small font so it's hard to read if there are more than a couple of people looking. The helicopters, planes and tanks outside are interesting to see.
Important collection. Lots of photographs and helpful captions. Quite busy. Most air conditioning out of service.

Really interesting museum with thought provoking content and photos. The only reason I give it three stars is that the experience was not enjoyable in the heat. Only the top two floors have some air conditioning in seperate rooms and where they do have air conditioning there is only one seat per air conditioned room.

Ok, hard to give a review becouse the atrocities of the war ,but it is a please to remember that war is wrong. also a lots of photos, very hard to watch ,Vietnamese people do enter this s buildings very often, now I no the reason why ,I do not want camelback here ever again! but I'm glad I did
He told me something . World peace

Less a museum, more a catalogue of American atrocities. It isn't very educational and it certainly isn't balanced, but it is extremely eye-opening. Most of the exhibit is photographs of the war and some are extremely graphic, especially regarding the effects of agent orange after the war was finished. The language used in the museum is very patriotic (a Vietnamese photographer killed on the battlefield is referred to as "patriotic martyr-journalist"), but it does give you a feeling for how the American campaign is viewed in the country. All in all quite a grim place.

Nice propaganda museum.
The museum has a nice display of American tanks, planes and howitzers. But the inside of the museum contains a lot of shocking images of South-Vietnamese and American cruelties. The never mention even once the cruelty inflicted by the North-Vietnam Army.

Entrance fee is 40,000 dong for adult.
Majority of the exhibit are post war photographs, some may be disturbing to see. But a read on the explanation would give you a good background of the Vietnam war and the role of America in the war.
The prison on the left of the main building exhibit how the army tortured the inmates which is cruel.
We can be realized that US  is so stupid country.
Interesting but sad history. Definitely not appropriate for young kids cause too many gory photos but the planes are cool for kids!
Worth a visit. But beware of horrible picture's and beware of super one side propaganda.

Interesting display outside and  also inside the building.

WARNING !! please read.
if you have Children with you ,
you may want to enter  THIS  room (upstairs) marked 'War crimes' on your own.
I refrained to photograph some of the pictures on display.
(dismembered bodies.....and similar photos)
===============//=====

Visit INDOOR FIRST ( multistory Air Conditioned)
I spent a long time outdoor first, doing my photography.
As a result, I was a little exausted  because of the warm weather.

You need to pay an entry fee, 40000 dong, $2.50 AUS, before entering the museum.
Close to the entry you will see on the right side the Cafe.
Great coffee  and food at 'Highland Cafe'; which is located within the museum area.
Think about what you see -
and form your OWN opinion
Enjoy the experience and YOUR OWN thoughts.

.

It is important to visit, to understand the history of the country and the damage caused by the War.  Mostly photos and war equipment.  Due to its importance, it should receive investment to modernize and make it more interactive.
Nice place. Even Vietnamese people can get a lot of knowledge. But the museum is quite small, decoration do not follow any order. It's very hard to understand Vietnam war following time

The War Remnants Museum is an important place for people to visit whilst visiting Ho Chi Minh City. Some of the photos are very graphic and probably not appropriate for younger children. It's very informative though somwhat one sided. A great place to learn about the brutal realities of war, though it's helpful if one keeps in mind it is an exhibit authorised by the Communist Party of the Socialist Republic of Vietnam and ultimately an exhibition coming from their perspective.
Worth a visit for sure.
The museum told story of vietnam war cruelty in vietnamese point of view. The place was a bit old, hot and nothing really special and mesmerizing.

I think my rating is hard but fair, that said you should visit. The War Remnants Museum is an important place to visit and provides perspective on the war in Vietnam.  Start from the top and work your way down to learn about the history of the war in Vietnam.

Heartbreaking stories, lessons for next gerations are well delivered. By the way, personally I wish there were more explanations about process and development of war. Thank you Vietnam for preserving precious history.

Information is biased and the museum is very focused on personal tragedies more than actual history. Even so I consider this is a very intetesting point of view, especially if you are not familiar with what the Vietnam War meant for the people of Vietnam.

This is not just about tanks, and helicopters is also about the massive number of people who died on both sides. See the weapons but take time to read the captions under the pictures. If I'd dose not make you think then you have no heart.

Museum offers a lot of information on the American War in Vietnam. Due to the way of the presentation it's sometimes a bit difficult to understand everything. Also the museum is super crowded. The collection of photographs is very good.

I expected the world class museum, I got a decent one. Im
I missed a clear narrative and stories there, exhibitions rooms don't easily connect. It's full of great photos, connects with a social work. Good.

A lot of history about the vietnam people struggles during the liberation war, but one sided only. No information about the involvement of  Chinese and Russians (Soviet Union at that time). Wide verity of weapons, aircraft and tanks. Photos of journalists from different countries.
super hot. the rooms are without AC. this makes everyone sweated and smelly until you feel too dizzy to enjoy the exhibition.

Interesting, sad, worth seeing but biased and doesn't really give the whole story behind the war. Many disturbing photos that you would not expect a museum to show, interesting display of American military vehicles, guns, and aircraft from the war.

The propaganda is real. It is a true one sided story with a lot of anti-us and anti-french colonies propaganda that dilutes the stories that could have been told, because you never know where truth ends and lies begin.

This is a hard one to rate as it is so horrible some of the images, sights and things that you see here. Do not bring children would be my advice! Good place to visit to get a history lesson, as with other war museums, set out fairly good. Most people know the back ground and this just reiterates the need to keep out of the affairs with others and look at your own back yard, again i guess war is politics and money. Good realistic exhibits and the staff are very helpful when asked questions, visitors when we were there were very respectful and were from all parts of the world which is encouraging, i do think that they should raise the price slightly to expand on the museum

With the amount of documentation that exists on the Vietnam war this comes in as a mediocre museum. It's limited to a vast amount of black and white photos and some artifacts such as weapons and equipment. In terms of presentation and infrastructure it is amateur at most. The building itself desperately needs better air conditioning. Bathrooms need improvement. For anyone from the "latin" part of the world there is no support in Spanish or Portuguese. Overall, there is a lack of interactive material and even wrong information (as in the case of a photograph from Argentina identified as Cuban and with a completely wrong translation). I1ve been to at least half a dozen war museums that this one could have used as a framework for theirs. It was a terrible and unjust war, this museum comes far from doing history justice.

The tunnels were worth the experience. The shooting range is an absolute waste of money. Minimum 10 bullet 600000 Dong. And you have no way to check your shot. Absolute waste of. The guide was just rushing in and out of everything. And although there was only 4 on the trip. He kept shouting. Hurry. Hurry. I really had enough of him. If u can tak taxi or grab to tunnels and enjoy the area. I would not recommend this trip. I gave it 3 stars out of respect for what the people endured. But only 1 star for the guide and trip

I doubt that this museum was built by Americans, because its exhibits seem to downplay their brutal war crimes. There are some fighter planes and tanks on the ground floor, and its exhibition hall only mentions how Europeans and Americans opposed the Vietnam War. Part of the exhibition hall on the first floor shows photos of Vietnamese children with disabilities after the US military used chemical weapons, and the second floor shows photos taken by American photographers. The content is all about telling people about the US military on the battlefield, etc. I personally think that this is completely looking at things from the aggressor's perspective, not the victims.

Firstly the museum has some wonderful exhibits and is something of a must see.  But...  If you took the story told by this museum as a factual cccount you would be mistaken. Its erronious by omission. The term Vietnamese only means those who followed the Communist North, absolutley no mention is made of the huge number of Vietnamese from the South (many who moved South on partition in the first place) who fought against them and whos ideology was opposed to them. This is portrayed soley as an American agression resisted by all Vietnamese, which it was not.  There were attrocities, not least among them the number of bodies unearthed around Hue post the Tet uprising, upwards of 2,800 following a massacre of unarmed civilians by the Communists. This is an example of attrocity erased from history in this museum at the expense of other attrocities.  The museum is fascinating as an example of how history can be used for political purposes. As stated there are some absolutley fascinating exhibits and items, and there is a powerful story to tell..... and in many respect this collection is part of that story. Not an unbiased objective part but a part of the story nonetheless.
Interesting, but the brutality of the photos is not for weak minds. But it's important to be informed about it.

The War Remnants Museum in Vietnam offers a deeply moving and educational experience with its comprehensive exhibits on the Vietnam War and its aftermath. The content is well-curated and thought-provoking, providing valuable insight into a significant period of history. However, the lack of air conditioning is a major drawback, especially considering the tropical climate. Visitors often find it unbearable to stay in the indoor sections for more than 5-10 minutes, which detracts from the overall experience. For a museum of such national and historical significance, improving the facilities should be a priority for the Vietnam Government.
This should’ve been a highlight for me but it’s 32degrees outside & there’s no air con!!!! I had to stand at doorway of each exhibit beside a fan - not ideal

I felt very sad while visiting this place. I am against war. Why is humanity so cruel? Memories flashed before my eyes. It can be visited as an example, but I saw that some people enjoyed it, and I was very sorry.

The museum is fair - some powerful imagery and information, and the vehicles are impressive, but it does not offer a balanced view of the conflict. Also, the headphoned audio tour is confusing and doesn't me match what you're seeing very well. The tunnels were worth the visit - harrowing and illuminating. However, I did find it in bad taste that they allow tourists to shoot era weaponry on a range onsite while visitors explore the tunnels.

It is a museum showcasing the war and history of Vietnam. The war it’s effect atrocities etc . It opens at 8 am and closes at 5 pm. We went on a weekend and there were lot of visitors. The museum is built across 3 floors and you purchase tickets for 40000 vnd per person. You get an audio guide too which makes seeing faster else reading takes time. It takes about 1.5 hrs to see the entire thing with a mix of reading and audio. It’s hot inside the museum. There are some table fans inside no fan or AC. Carry your water bottles.

Ok. This may seem harsh. I have NO PROBLEM with their editorial position, their messaging, or their delivery. Indeed, I think their messages need to be part of the mainstream conversation about the Vietnam War... namely that the US tried to throw all its might at this tiny poor country, and STILL got their assess handed to them. That is not the problem.  The problem, its a lousy museum. Not all of it. The outside portion with the Tiger Traps and tanks, and jets, is really cool. But the inside is BLAH. What saves it is the audio tour. To be honest, I walked through the exhibits and then sat through and listened for three hours. The exhibits were just a bunch of photos with limited context. Of 6 areas, two were about agent orange.  There was no air conditioning (who has every heard of a museum without air conditioning).  What I REALLY wanted to see? Was how the war was fought, and messaged, and thought about from the VC and NVA side. It was like the US were fighting themselves. Also, the coverage of the French portion of the war was context only for the US portion.  Hell, a telling of the story of the war in some linear progressive manner would have been awesome too. Its like they assumed you went to another museum about the actual WAR first, and just wanted a museum of American evil.  As I said, the message was fine, I just wish it were a better museum.
The best part was the helicopter and plane etc outside. There were some good displays but we would not have missed it if we did not go. Lots and lots of reading

The aging museum (unfortunately) shows very clearly who the victims of the wars were and are. Various war machines are on display outside. Inside, the history of the French period and the conflict with the USA are presented very vividly on three floors in various mostly unair-conditioned rooms. There are elevators and toilets inside the building. Audio guides were not available in German. At the time we were there, admission cost 40,000 VND per person and could ONLY be paid in CASH. There is also a small café inside.

Left feeling quite sad, didn’t see all of the place. Again, the atrocities of war, photographs, many of them. What one human being can do to another was beyond us. That said, generations that didn’t grow up during the Vietnam War need to learn this horrific history to ensure it never happens again.

Layout of museum was relatively good and was very informative regarding American war crimes and the tragic effects of agent Orange. As expected, no mention of VC or PAVN war crimes, giving the impression that it was only the Americans committing war crimes.

During very hot day isn’t easy visit all exhibitions. Outside is fresh but hot air , inside exhibitions rooms is air quality poor. Exhibitions were done by order a few years ago and need rebuilt for nowadays When do you plan visit thinking about morning time

Let's get down to the basic, fundamental truth here: this museum tries (and succeeds) to re-write history by presenting not just a biased, but a completely one-sided view of what happened during the Vietnam War. There isn't a single mention of the Viet Cong or any of the atrocities they committed in the name of Ho Chi Minh. I  t's a complete whitewash of the events by a Communist country that doesn't want its own dirty washing aired so instead goes for the easy, imperialist target. The remnants parts are interesting but the over-riding political propaganda and dogma means you should go into this tour with both eyes wide open and never forget every coin has two sides. This museum fails in that aspect.
To be honest, there is nothing interesting there. But you can visit for show. There are a lot of new buildings, the cafe is expensive and the cigarettes are sold by the wrong people

The museum has a lot of good things but like other museums in Saigon it is without organization and without a restoration that is greatly needed. In the different sections such as weapons or photography, all the items are very crowded together, which makes it not comfortable or fluid to observe them (crowd of people in the same place, very little fluidity) and the lighting in all the rooms is disastrous.

Tough going. There are some graphic and truly upsetting images of war on display that are not for everyone to view. My wife was left feeling physically sick. For my part, I wondered what the museum sought to achieve other than to point the finger of blame. Yes, war is awful. Yes participants on both sides of conflicts act inhumanely. Yes, politicians on all sides, are disingenuous and deceitful. But if you need to see a photo of one human being holding the detatched head of another human being to understand that, then you have led an unbelievably sheltered life.
The War Remnants Museum is a small but well set to collection related to the American War. It is fairly graphic and is quite moving, reminding us all of the futility of war and stupidity of mankind.

Do NLT get the audio guide. It's is useless. It rarely says anything about what you are seeing and it only talks about some pictures and rarely artifacts. There is no storytelling. No personal stories - it is a British woman reading up a bunch of facts and numbers that are hard to follow. The museum itself is unfortunately such a missed opportunity. It could be really good, but they are missing some storytelling to contextualize what you as an audience is seeing. Still, there are some things that are worth seeing. The agent orange room is insane. And the tiger cages seems interesting, even though it's har to understand what they really are.

Not many remnants… it’s mostly just pictures and text on the wall, which could’ve been put in a book. I’m an American all the way in Vietnam; I was hoping to see something more special. Like I said I could read all the things on a wall in a book instead. There were fetuses affected by agent orange in some kind of container which was crazy to sort of see but the container was clouded. That was most surprising to me. I wanted to see more objects vs read text.

I was already expecting some level of propagandist bragging but it was a bit way too over the top.
The entire exhibition is monotonously biased and lack necessary objectivity. The sole focus is on the defeat of US and their alleged ‘war crimes’ without mentioning any atrocities committed by the North Vietnamese regime during the war as a context.
I was utterly bored after ten minutes. The museum is packed with visitors, some of whom had rather foul body odour.

A few interesting pieces of US hardware outside but the majority of the items are inside and mostly just photographic.
The Hanoi military museum is vastly superior and highly recommended to see.

The museum showcases a very one sided account of the war with a lot of misinformation which is clearly used for propaganda purposes. When you encounter some clear misinformation it makes you question the integrity of any information given which is a real shame. Atrocities were committed on both sides of the war and to really learn from these they should be documented in such a museum.

The museum is fine, with a lot of morbid descriptions of what happened in the war. Very very detailed and thorough.

Many storeys in the building.

I must warn all tourists though. There is(are) a(a few) coconut scam artists around the entrance or the area who would try to strike up a conversation with you and then open a coconut on the spot (as if they are being nice and offering to you for free) then charging you 300,000 dong for it! This is crazy and ridiculous! We hope that no more tourists fall trap to this anymore. 👎🏼👎🏼👎🏼

Very disappointed by the behaviour of these scam artists stationed around the area of tourist spots and soiling the image and reputation of Vietnam.

Please be warned that there are very disturbing and graphic images in the museum. Inside the museum, there are no signs of trigger warning of images you might see. Please be mentally prepared, I went there with no clue, assumed with an open mind that it was a museum with lots of information and knowledge about the Vietnam War. In the Agent Orange part of the museum, they play an audio of the sounds the guns make in the guns area. Children are allowed inside in this part. We cut our trip inside the museum short, because I was on a verge of a mental breakdown. Do not visit this place if you have a history of trauma, PTSD, or highly empathetic.
It’s a very simple museum mostly with pictures about the war. There are other better places to visit in Saigon.
Lots of photographs, not a lot of actual remnants. Interesting to view the war as an American through the eyes of the Vietnamese.

The History of American war in Vietnam is not even explained. Only 4 explanations panels.
More detail, a movie would have been appreciated.
It’s more a museum of pity than a real museum. If you wanna be sad and do not learn anything, it is the perfect place.

Only one comparison panel comparing figures between WW2, Korean war and Vietnam war is really interesting.

Terrible experience. There was hundreds of children running around and being super loud during a school visit (and it was a Saturday!) I was not able to properly see the museum nor listen to the audio guide. Two whole rooms were focused on the agent orange and had very explicit images.

I good museum that shows the effects of war on a country and the people involved (active or as innocent civilians). All guts and no glory. Lots of tough photos to see and storys to read about which is very sobering. Personally I didn't like the layout of the museum, if was very crowded due to its setup so you could not really take the time to read everything. Not sure I would recommend but definitely a place to go to neutralize all the pro war poeple and families.
Nice museum but not the best for takings War! If you want to see more planes go to Hanoï war mesum.

This museum should be more somber and reflective but that intent was distracted by rowdy guests and tourists hanging off the tanks in the front and generally apathetic people around. The admission fee was very affordable which may be why this is the case but I think the museum should enforce against some of this behaviour and ensure guests are appropriately respectful

Mostly pictures and text only. Rooms are ventilated with fans only, not AC, so it was pretty hot.
Everything seemed like a gallery with pictures, that's it. Pretty unspectacular.
Couldn't experience propery without being pestered by people trying to sell books
Super crowded and long queue for tickets. Better to visit early in the morning as the museum opens around 7:30am, or book tickets one day in advance on Klook app.

I was really looking forward to seeing this museum. I've heard good things about it and I love learning about history through a new lens. My problem with the museum is not one of perspective. I fully expected a pro-Vietnamese anti-imperialist examination of this beautiful country's numerous invasions. My issue is that the presentation is static, outdated, and frankly boring. The museum is basically a long line of photographs and newspaper articles. I would have been better served by a Ken Burns-style documentary. I found myself restless and annoyed by the repetitive display of the photojournalist section. This was made worse by the audio guide that I purchased, which spent an eternity describing every last detail of these photographers lives even if there were only 2 or 3 accompanying photos. By the time I reacheched the gore and heartbreak of the Agent Orange and War Crimes exhibits I had lost patience with the process. Sadly I left earlier than I was intending so as to spend more time with the lovely people of this marvelous country.

Need more story telling about history of Vietnam. Only focused on Vietnam war vs US. Rooms were too hot and smelly. If you know even a little bit about the Vietnam War, I don’t think you need to visit.

If you are aware of the war and how bad all of it was, you don't need to go.
In case you have no clue, go and get it rubbed into your face, but don't expect it to be pleasant.
It's a very sad story either way.

The war museum is very interesting with a lot of information… but only about atrocities from the South & U.S.

Absolutely no mention of atrocities from the North. Many mentions of attacks against the North, mentions of the North being “patriots”, etc.
It’s very disgusting how they purposely ignore what the North did.

Any pictures or information that’s about the South that may be good or depict the North as bad, they don’t mention it at all & has you assume “Good, so must be North” or “Bad, must be South”.
I mean they glorify dictators & communist leaders who praised & were on the North’s side ffs.
I eventually stopped reading the information the more I noticed that this was a propaganda museum.

I heard many ignorant tourists actually believe the one sidedness of the museum, so their propaganda is working. I mean, just look at the ignorant reviews. Absolutely terrible.

Also, not uncommon to hear white, male European tourists laughing at things like the guillotine when passing by what the POW conditions were like. So that’s something else you may encounter that’s also disgusting.

Personally I didn't enjoy this place at all and get far more out of watching a documentary.  The images were very graphic and although I love gorey stuff I wasn't prepared for my Miss 9 to see it.

It is also busy so there are not many good photo opportunities in front of the planes etc.

If you are a complete war history fanatic then you may enjoy but maybe not.

Interesting but too much to read and not interactive. Audio guide would be great! Specially if language options apart from English were available! Pictures are quite good though and it shows how horrible war was for everyone. The weapons used by the US army were not only lethal but Sadic. Cheap entrance fee.

The bias in the information makes it hard to read many of the writings in the exhibits without feeling like it’s been filtered to only highlight the wrong doings by the US and to scrutinize the US participation.

The museum makes no mention of the additional support for the South that came from Thailand, South Korea, and Australia. It barely discusses the fact that the US was there supporting the South Vietnam army and not just there waging war with their own agenda.

It also doesn’t mention anything about the North Vietnam army being backed by the Soviet Union or what that involvement looked like. All of the exhibits could basically be summed up as “look what the Americans did.”

I support facts and I don’t dispute any of the information that was on display at the museum. However, the whole story is not on display here and many important details have been omitted.

In this museum you can find the story of the Vietnam war mostly spread out over about 10 rooms. It consists of mainly pictures on the walls along with alot of texts. What happened is horrible and I think it is good to take a second to think about. That said I didn't like the presentation. A lot of focus was on all the damage done and the weaponry that was used. What I personally really missed was a red line in the story and more explanation on how the war started, developed and ended. After stepping out I still didn't feel I learned a lot about what happened and how except that it was all horrible. For this reason I would not say it's a must visit. I hope this museum can become more accessible in the future.

In my opinion, I didn't really enjoy it that much. This museum has lots of information but I feel like they only tells the story on Vietnam side, too much information and pictures of the victims, they should be more neutral.

I prefer Hoa Lo prison in Hanoi, that one is fantastic

The museum displays a few airplanes, tanks and weapons but is mostly a collection of photo galleries. While the photos carry an important effect to make people understand the horror (of war) - they are only one element of telling the story of the Vietnam wars. I would have expected more structure and more explanations of the history behind the war (and the pictures). Perhaps, pictures are not enough.

I felt as this , as informative as it is was very one sided. What you get most is how horrible the US was and how we came here and did unspeakable things.
I have learned way more of both side by watching documentaries. As I walked through and read all the displays is was clear that the US was "the bad guy" according to this place.
I have not been to war. I have talked with many Vietnamese people in my short time in Vietnam and the views of this museum dont seemed to be shared by the masses. War is a horrible thing. And horrible things happen in war.
I just feel that the aim of this museum was to only point out the one side of what happened. Making the US the only perpetrators of wrong.

I love museums but now I like to appreciate the good sides of history. This museum does not make me feel good. I want Vietnam to acknowledge that its present prosperity is due to the helps of the enemies of Communism.

Propaganda, but interesting propaganda.

In Vietnam, you can be jailed for merely criticizing the Communist Party.  That alone should tell you what level of bias to expect from this museum.

Some things were a bit silly, like giving Ho Chi Minh sole credit for repelling Japanese occupants in 1945.

That said, there are some very powerful images here.  I’d recommend going, just go in with some common sense knowing that it’s more of a propaganda museum than a purely informative one.

This museum didn't give much information or opportunity to learn much about the war. this place is basically just a whole lot of photos from the war and People affected by it. also there ia no nice place to ait down as HCM is extremely hot and the only air conditioning here is in the exhibit rooms which offer no sitting places

I'm not too sure I understood what this museum was about. A whole lot of painful images on walls, with captions that are meant to be biased in its journalism and references. With as many graphic and gory images that were posted up, the last thing you want to see is little kids to view them - however, there is no age-restriction here. The mood in the air was very sombre and that of sorrow - but I did feel that it was being shoved in our (tourist) faces quite bluntly. I do not discount the lives lost and the destruction caused in Vietnam, the history is very sad. However, I did feel the museum is designed purely for western tourists (which defeats the purpose).

Museum is completely lacking in nuance. Showing horrific photos of victims to sell half-truths is in poor taste and disrespectful to the many who were impacted by the war (on all sides). Worth a visit, but it's hard to rate a "museum" as more than 2 stars when more about the subject matter can be learned in 10 min from a quick Google search.

Any exhibit on the Vietnam war in the West is going to include reference to the undoubtedly terrible things perpetrated by the US-led side, as well as those committed by the Viet Kong/N Vietnam. The problem with this exhibit is that it is from beginning to end a propaganda-machine. I'm not sure that it's even legal to show the other point of view here in Vietnam(?) For me that opens up the whole question of trust: Are the photos of people who were "shot moments later" all genuinely that? Is every deformed baby the result of agent orange, rather than a collection of normally occurring deformed babies? Is every picture of an innocent farmer, just that, or were they killed because they were about to kill? The answer to these questions may well be yes, but such an obviously agenda-laden exhibit has to leave questions in the mind of any thinking person. If you are able to see beyond the propaganda, then the exhibit is a potent reminder of what we are capable of in our worst moments.

Not must see. If you are not fanatic of war topic it’s could be boring. In my life enough information about war in my hometown in Ukraine with Russia, so I didn’t like the place it’s just weapons and a lot of photos with stories about them.

Very one sided account of the American involvement in the Vietnamese Civil War/Vietnam War (if you're American)/ American War (if you're Vietnamese). A great example of how history is told by the victors and definitely a highlight of any trip to Vietnam.
It did make me very sad to see the carnage the war caused, how home field is never an advantage in war and how the people of the land of the war are the ones who truly pay the price.

Knowing the importance of never forgetting the past, this museum shows one side of the coin. There was no information desk to ask for an english guide however I enjoyed looking at all the photos. Allows for 2 hours time. If you have to miss it, it is totally fine, you likely find the same info on the internet.

We were troubled by this museum, because it isn't objective. Yes, what the Americans did in this country was abhorrent, especially when considering Agent Orange. However there is no mention of what the North Vietnamese were doing at the time, and this museum goes as far as to suggest that most of the world sided with the Northern Regime, which simply isn't true. It also cites agent orange being responsible for fleshy tumours and cancers in some cases, which yes make terrifying photos and propoganda, but this clearly isn't objective as tumours and cancers aren't necessarily related to Agent Orange at all. Interesting to see, but almost pure propoganda.

It mentions nothing about the Northern campaign of civilian targeted roadside bombs. It mentions nothing of the Hue and Dak Son massacres. And it mentions nothing of the gunning down of targeting of fleeing civilians on highways headed to the south. It mentions nothing of the North Vietnamese gulags. It would be nice of the victims of these atrocities were also acknowledged. You can find out all about these just by quickly googling it - so why isn't it in the museum?
The exhibition areas at levels 2 n 3 were warm and stuffy and there was sweat odour. Didnt stay long as the environment wasnt pleasant.

The best part of this museum was looking at the photographs. The articles had no substance and it's literally filled with propaganda. Now I'm not saying u.s. are saints but to say atrocities were only committed on one side paints a fairly obvious picture about state of neutrality in this museum. This was a civil war where Russia and US decided to have a proxy war.

Felt very emotional even with the small number of artefacts and pictures. For a main city war Museum I was expecting a much richer display of artefacts of the 17 year and quite recent war.  Hope there will be additions of other artefacts soon rather than mainly photos.

This is a museum of the winner. Don't be sad when you visit there as the country's history doesn't go on one way road. I myself value the Vietnam's History Museum inside the city's zoo much much more than this fancy, highly-invested one.

Not a good museum. You learn more about the war and its complicated background by reading a Wikipedia article. Mostly photographs on 3 floors showing a very biased view on the war. Some photographs especially on the second floor taken by journalists / war reporters with a bit more background information were interesting though. However, some photographs were very graphic (e.g. birth defects caused by Agent Orange, soldiers holding the remains of people that stepped on landmines, etc). Not sure if these horrific photographs belong in a museum that is open to public with children visiting. The people on the pictures with birth defects felt also like on display.

It would be a nice experience for history lovers. But it was not interactive,  you had to walk by the paintings and read yourself. It was very depressing given the atrocities of war. I personally would have liked it to be more interactive.

US GIs are presented as mad dogs willing to go to Vietnam to vent their frustrations at the expenses of poor paesants' lives; no mentioning of the war crimes committed during and after the war by Viet Congs are given; no mentioning of the involvement of and pressures by South Vietnam to use more and more Orange agent is given; no analysis of the (true) reasons and of the aftermath of this "warm" period of the Cold War is provided.

Das Museum ist methodisch unzweckmäßig aufgebaut, eine historische Einordnung geschieht beispielsweise erst im letzten Drittel der Ausstellung (3.OG). Auch sind die Bilder zwar mit Beschreibungen versehen, diese sind allerdings meist lediglich beschreibend, eine sinnvolle bzw. sinnstiftende Kommentierung sucht man vergebens. In den seltenen Fällen, in denen eine Kommentierung stattfindet, ist sie unsachlich und polemisch (ein amerikanisches M1A1 Gewehr wird bspw. als Waffe beschrieben, die vor allem zur Bekämpfung von Demonstration und bei Verhören eingesetzt wurde).
Das führt zum eigentlichen, wenn auch nicht überraschenden Manko der Ausstellung: sie ist unfassbar einseitig und undifferenziert. Nordvietnam gut, USA böse. Eine solche Darstellung instrumentalisiert die Opfer des Krieges zu Propagandazwecken und wird ihnen nicht gerecht.
Insgesamt ist das Museum eine große Propagandashow, die sehr schnell sehr ermüdend wirkt, vor allem weil die Bildunterschriften so nichtssagend sind und jedes weitere Interesse zunichte machen.

Eine Ausnahme stellt die Fotografieausstellung im 3.OG dar. Diese ist merklich von einem anderen Kurator zusammengestellt und wurde bereits international ausgestellt. Diese dokumentiert die Arbeit und Schicksale der Kriegsfotografen und bietet ergreifende Eindrücke des Kriegsgeschehens.

Der Informationsgehalt ist mittel bis gering. Wenn man sich nur ein wenig über das Thema informiert hat (seien es nur TV Dokus und Wikipedia) erfährt man im Museum kaum neues.

Sehr schade, da das Thema sehr viel Potential bietet, über die grundsätzlichen Gräuel des Krieges zu reflektieren und eventuell Schlüsse daraus zu ziehen. Diese Chance wird hier leider nicht genutzt.

Bof bof bof. Voilà, j'ai résumé mon avis. Mais pour en dire un peu plus :

- On est loin de ces nouveau musées 'wow' comme le musée Solidarnosc (à Gdansk, en pologne). L'information est présentée ici de manière très linéaire, et toujours sous sa forme la plus scolaire (salle de photos, salle des armes, salle des posters ...) Le musée n'est pas interactif du tout et ne vous surprend a peu près jamais.

- Les textes accompagnatifs  sont parfois douteux. Je veux dire, c'est bien que le gouvernement vietnamien donne son avis sur ce conflit et le comportement américain (on a tellement vu cette guerre sous le prisme US), mais on est parfois gèné par le manque de neutralité grossier dans les commentaires. Plusieurs exemples me viennent à l’esprit, mais celui des mines antipersonnel est assez représentatif. On vous explique que les américains ont miné le territoire, et qu'aujourd'hui encore des gens peuvent marcher dessus _ avec moultes photos émouvantes et commentaires. Sauf que la quasi totalité des mines encore disséminées au VN ont été posées par ... les viet minh, qui ne les ont pas répertoriées ! Mais pas un mot là dessus, ces mines là sont magiques et ne font pas de victimes même si bien plus nombreuses.

C'est un endroit visitable si vous avez quatre jours entiers ou plus à HCMC. Si moins de temps, je vous conseille de zapper. Au delà des points soulevés et même si la guerre du vietnam vous intéresse, les ressources présentées n'ont rien d'immanquable. J'aime bien me faire un bon musée, mais celui-là n'en faisait définitivement pas partie.
