I did find the photos taken by the was correspondents interesting and quite enjoyed seeing the planes and other equipment outside, but did not feel that this museum should be included in a tour itinery and should always be optional

Lots of war photographs, depicting the atrocities of the war. It’s informative, though one side as one could imagine.  However, given the subject matter, this museum deserves a bit of respect. Instead, it’s a hellhole. Looking beyond the old infrastructure and lack of airconditioning, children and toddlers run around, hitting artifacts, shouting at the top of their voices. Other tourists also shouts loudly. Guards are not there to observe decorum, they are unfortunately lazy.  What is supposed to be a highly emotional, illuminating and emphatic experience, turns into a loathsome experience.  I was left wondering if I would not have been better off reading a book instead of visiting the museum.

Firstly do recommend going. Secondly acknowledge the atrocities of war are terrible and the American (and West) involvement on foreign soil was questionable. However the credibility of the museum is low. You leave with more questions than answers. No information on: What was the context of the conflict? Why was the north attracted to communism? What was Russias and Chinas involvement- seems bizarre the American and the wests war effort was detailed down to the person, ton of ordinance, individual machines and yet zero information on communist support? Etc etc It would be a much better museum if the whole picture with some balanced view was provided. Maybe not ready for that yet?

We were contacted in the morning to confirm pick-up time (8.15am) as per every trip we have been on in the last 6 weeks, we were outside 5 mins before the pick-up time to ensure the bus did not have to wait for us. Over 20 mins later, there was no sign of the bus or any contact from the tour guide, so I phoned the company, who then called me back to say they were on the way. A further 5 minutes had passed before the bus turned up, not a great start!!  We were meeted by our tour guide and bus and were welcomed aboard and given 2 bottles of water (this was needed as the temperature was in excess of 30°).  We arrived at the first stop, the Palace, and we soon realised that our guide did not speak very good English (his English is better than my Vietnamese but the whole idea of having an English speaking guide is for us to learn about the history and culture of Vietnam). There were a few members of the group who also had difficulty understanding the guide. The Palace was an OK stop, but I wouldn't visit here again (personal choice).  We then left for the Central Post Office, I purchased some postcards to send home but given we only had 20 mins and the Post Office was very busy we had no time to write and send the postcards so this will have to be done another day.  Then, finally, we left for the War Museum. This was the main reason we booked the tour!! We arrived and were advised on which rooms to go in first by our guide, and then we were left to our own means to read the articles (this was absolutely fine as everything was bilingual). However, the museum has a plethora of information about the Vietnam/America war but we only had 45 mins to read it all, again, this felt extremely rushed and we didn't manage to get into two rooms or visit the tanks outside as the time had elapsed.  I would not recommend this tour as it seems rushed from start to finish. We later found out that some of the group were going to Cu Chi Tunnels in the afternoon, which is why I felt we were rushed on the half day, as they had to travel to Cu Chi! Next time, I would visit the War Museum without a tour company and just pay the admission fee as you would have more time and not be rushed to get around all the information boards.  And lastly, after the museum, the guide explained we would now be dropped off and the remaining group would go to Cu Chi, so he would drop us off at the Market as our hotel is visible from the market. I quote, "You can see your hotel from market". Great, no problem. Given that we have no geographical knowledge of HCMC, we took the guides word as he has lived in HCMC all his life! Little did we realise that when we got off the bus, our hotel was 16 minutes walk from our hotel despite being told it was around the corner! It was 34° and we were dropped off nowhere near our hotel! We paid approx. £62 GBP for this trip, and we feel it was not worth nowhere near this amount! Hotel pick-up and drop-off are stated. However, if the drop-off is 16 minutes walk away like in our case, I would not class that as hotel drop off! I feel extremely let down and disappointed and out of all the "day trips" we have done on our 6 weeks travelling, this unfortunately, was our worst trip.

This is on the “must see” list of attractions in HCMC/Saigon. We took the English audio tour and found it very tedious. The museum is predominantly photographs and the audio predominantly talks about the photographers rather than what you are seeing. Others we met enjoyed the museum but didn’t take the audio. I suspect that’s the way to go. But we had learned a lot of what the museum had to say from other tours around the country.  If your time is limited, go to Cu Chi. We learned so much more there.

We went here given it was one of the biggest tourist destinations in Ho Chi Min. The former US military planes and bombs, etc are really good when you walk in to look around and interesting- no audio guide needed. There is a small cell complex with some interesting things to look at and experience too. However the rest of it then became a little boring and some what annoying - 3 floors full of rooms with just photos and stories of US atrocities. Some of the stories and photos are real and quite graphic and certainly thought provoking re chemical weapons, torture, etc. However the whole museum seems devoted to telling you how awful and horrific the Americans were with not much fact telling. I anticipated a little of this with it being Vietnam but it was so over the top. It would be good to see some other hardware or things to look at other than endless historical photos of torture and abuse and “genocide”. I have been to Auschwitz and heard of the nazis and the holocaust but according to this museum the US was much worse ?! I don’t think I’d go again.

From a completely objective standpoint this is a poorly organized and maintained museum. The building itself is not well maintained. The museum rooms have very poor or non-existent air conditioning. In the sweltering heat of Saigon, air conditioning is essential in this environment. The outdoor exhibits are better than the indoor exhibits, of which the latter are poor. They are primarily reproduced photos with text. You can get this in a book. No immersive experiences and very few artifacts. I've been to national museums around the world run by governments. This is the least impressive I've seen.

The museum gives a very biased view of the Vietnam war by focusing on the terrible actions of the American army (which may be true) . They do not mention their own atrocious actions but focus on the good will and caring nature of their army in looking after the locals. I was very disappointed in the biased view and walked out after about 30 minutes.

Museum is setup nice; however, being an American, I found this a slam against my country and those who served in Vietnam. This was a propaganda museum against the US and everything inside was very negative to the US referring extensively to US atrocities against Vietnam. Atrocities were on both during the war (i.e. The Battle of Hue where the North Vietnam side massacred 1000s of civilians). I felt sorry for any Vietnam Vet that would visit here since most Vets did not participate in the massive atrocities show inside.  There is a floor on Agent Orange which is very detailed and that was the only place I felt they had a right to criticize the US.  On a more positive note, if you like war machinery and weapons, there are some items outside like a Huey Helicopter and other weapons worth seeing (best part of the museum other than the cheap cost).

I found it excessive and actually not very informative, more a freakshow of horrors than a museum on the reasons for the war. I understand that for the Vietnamese people the thousands of deaths are still an open wound and I don't want to be disrespectful, but there is a way to present the information in a museum. To give you an idea, half of the second floor focuses on deformations due to the use of Agent Orange, photographs of malformations, deformed fetuses... is it really necessary? And then once you leave you still have to go and look for information on the war because in concrete terms how the conflict came about we haven't understood.

Museu interessante e importante para entender a guerra de outro ponto de vista. Contudo o local carece de melhor estrutura, ventilação, conservação e curadoria, poderia ser bem melhor. Do lado de fora há equipamentos militares da época da guerra. Se vai a Saigon, é difícil não ir, mas prepare-se para certa desorganização e eventuais maltratos. Fomos em um dia chuvoso e o museu oferecia guarda-chuvas, porém um senhor uniformizado na entrada no museu impediu-nos de usá-los enquanto permitiu que TODOS os outros visitantes vietnamitas ou brancos usassem, o que achei extremamente preconceituoso, ainda mais vindo de um asiático. Por isso não recomendo.

A small entry fee paid on arrival AUD$2 per person. We walked around the yards and the displays inside, and it took us a total of about 10 minutes. There's not really a lot to see, and hardly anything about the war. A pretty disappointing war museum and the only things relating to the war are the helicopter, jet, and cannon outside

After going here, I feel the blatant propoganda really takes away from what could be a great museum. This is not a museum where you will learn the facts/history of the Vietnam war. It is effectively 9 rooms of propoganda. There are some moving parts, in particular the Agent Orange rooms, but overall, it is disappointing.

Heartbreaking I couldn’t stay very long because it focused a lot on the torture of the locals. It’s just very sad - devastation for all. Agent orange also had lasting effects on other soldiers. It was a one sided story.

We visited this whilst in Saigon and was really looking forward to learning more about the Vietnam War. When we arrived we were met with seeing paraded US military vehicles such as planes and tanks!  We then made our way into the building and were then met with a one sided museum about the war and communist propaganda! I know the American GIs did disgusting things during this war from Agent Orange to torture but christ it was so one sided and was zero mention of the atrocities that the North Vietnamese caused.  History is meant to be told from both sides and have a fair chance to understand what truthfully happened, just like WW1 and WW2.  I do advised going to this museum to just witness my points of view!!  Please just be truthful about history and what actually happened!

A disappointing visit after high hopes. Multiple photos, many graphic, of a savage conflict. But very poor explanation of key historical moments that influenced the conduct of the war. Gift shop is an embarrassment. Badly in need of a makeover.

Outside the building are a selection of old US Air Force equipment, a Chinnock, Huey, Skyraider, F5 and a Cessna observation aircraft. These are not in the best condition, but are easy to inspect. Also outside are a number of tanks and military armour and a river craft. Inside the main thrust of the display, which is supported by very few artifacts, focusses on the limited support the war received from the US public and statements from deserters and military personnel that refused to fight. This of course was a sad time in the history of Vietnam and for that matter the US, but the main thrust of the display is the lack of support from within the US, and particularly within the troops/airmen. It is a must to see, but don't expect too much.

Les vietnamiens ont subi la guerre mais eux n'ont rien fait de mal. Les américains sont des méchants et honnêtement la salle sur l'agent orange ne devrait pas exister. Dommage que les vietnamiens ne donnent pas la vérité sur les deux côtés de la guerre. Je regrette de l'avoir visité.
Mit einigen Schautafeln und wenigen wirklichen Ausstellungsstücken eigentlich keinen Besuch wert! Insgesamt keine sehenswerte Aufarbeitung des Vietnamkrieges.

Went to see this museum as it is the most highly-billed in the city. The outside display of old vehicles and equipment was fine and the Agent Orange section was humbling. Compared to heavy bombing by the US Air Force, the use of agent orange was truly awful and the pictures of deformed children were dreadful to look at. Aside from these 2 displays, this museum had the same characteristic of many of the others we saw: junk exhibits with a blatantly propaganda message attached to each. It was so completely over the top and one-sided (even compared to other museums) that we almost walked out after a few minutes. By all means go to see it; it certainly isn't expensive, but be aware of what you are going to see.

This had the potential to be a really good museum – interesting artifacts and pictures that were well presented. Unfortunately, they ruined it by writing such ridiculously biased texts. As I am Swedish I have no connection with any of the countries involved with the conflict so I am not being defensive. I just felt insulted by the blatant propaganda and so disappointed that there was not a more balanced account of what happened.
Overall worth it. You have to remember that you are getting one side if the war and not the US side. Sobering, some propaganda.
Would be better with audio or even a guided tour. We were not given a map so we really had to guess our way around.
It used to be called “The House for Displaying War Crimes of American Imperialism and the Puppet Government” for a reason.
Just junked helicopters and other stuff posters of Americans protesting against war ... visit if you have nothing better to do and 40000 dongs to throw away
We wasted our time!
The museum was highly slanted to reflect the war from Vietnam's viewpoint. It's in their country, so they get to choose what to show and say. I thought it was very one sided and a lot of propaganda.
de tand der tijd is verborgen met een grote verfborstel. De restauratie is gedaan door een huisschilder. Spijtig
I have expected something quite bigger as this should be the main exposition dedicated to the war. Expositons were quite nice, the English description was sometimes missing.
Museum was only around $1 AUD entry however very uninteresting and very poor ventilation which made it unbearable to stay in th building for long on a hot day.

Avevo letto molte recensioni entusiastiche, ma francamente come museo non mi ha soddisfatto. Nel cortile c'e un esposizione di mezzi bellici, ma senza particolari spiegazioni o ambientazioni. Il resto del museo consiste in 3 piani di sole foto. Pochissime ambientazioni o ricostruzioni. Nulla che non avessi già visto su YouTube o nei film di guerra più famosi. Da un museo mi aspetto che arricchisca la mia conoscenza, ma di fatto dopo un ora e mezza di visita ne so esattamente quanto ne sapevo già prima. Nulla di più. Lo consiglio solo a chi è totalmente digiuno di storia.

As someone born after the war, I was hoping to get more of a history lesson. As a lot of the other reviews state, the museum is extremely one sided. I wish it gave more information. It points the finger at Americans as being the aggressors but there are always two sides to every story. Unfortunately this museum only tells one side. Only American weapons and photos of Vietnamese people being victimized are on display.  It was a very sad time in history for both sides but the museum could do a better job of factually showing both perspectives. The entry price is inexpensive. The photos are very graphic.
호치민관광유명코스라기에 갔는데…  저는 별 흥미가 없어 그냥 둘러만보고 나왔어요.  ~~~~^^  근데 사람들은 진짜 많았어요!  바이크 구경은 실컷…

Echoing what others have said it is definitely not for young children as the photos are extremely graphic. The commentary is understandably biased but it’s interesting to learn how history is constructed.

Interesting to see some of the exhibits and the display of equipment outside. Otherwise move on quickly as it’s about what you would guess from the current regime. The tour spent too much time there. Many other more interesting things to see in today’s city.

Jag tycker att det absolut är värt ett besök men det är i stort behov av en renovering. Det är mest som en fotoutställning om krigets fasor. Idag förväntar man sig ett mer interaktivt upplägg av en sådan utställning.

It is a decent museum, although it is completely onesided. “America is the enemy and Vietnam did nothing wrong”. Maybe if you did some research beforehand it would be good. Some of it was exciting, although it felt like we were being fed propaganda and tried brainwashed

There are some old vehicles and guns and lots and lots of photographs many of them extremely unpleasant. It was a wrong and horrible war but it would have been interesting if there was something explaining how and why the Americans got there and what the French did before them. Without some balance history is just propaganda!

De manière générale plutôt déçu par ce « musée », qui est en fait une très grande série de photographies et de posters aux légendes un peu lourdes, sans aucune interaction.  Certaines images ne vous épargnent rien, et choquent de par leur violence. Incompréhensible qu’il n’y ait aucune mise en garde par rapport à ça ... A ne pas faire avec de jeunes enfants.  Aussi les explications sont clairement subjectives et manquent de recul et de contexte. C’est compréhensible, étant donné les atrocités commises par les GIs, mais certaines légendes étonnent quand même ... par exemple l’opposition des photographes de guerre Nord vietnamiens « héros-martyrs » et les « autres ». J’imagine que des violences ont été commises dans les deux camps comme toutes les guerres, et donc aussi par les nord-viêtnamiens, mais ce n’est jamais mentionné ...  Point positif : certaines photographies de guerre de l’exposition « Réminescences » au premier étage sont poignantes et impressionnantes.  En résumé, on en sort un peu choqué et sonné, mais sans avoir appris grand chose de plus sur la guerre. Dommage.

Inexpensive short tour of war relics. Place is in disrepair and is rusting away. Helicopter and jet plane with some rifles and explosives. A gazebo with some facts about the war. They have bathrooms !!!
The museum itself is very informative and clean. But the content was sad and the history is very depressing. War museums in general do not excite me.
Bien , mais plus un centre d'information sur les effets de l'agent orange utilisé pendant la guerre du Vietnam.
Some interesting stuff to see for the price. They have some old military aircraft and artillery pieces to check out as well. Just be prepared to see a lot of propaganda on the inside.
Very one-side revisionist history

Con gran expectativa visitamos este museo, con la esperanza de encontrar muchos objetos y una narración acerca de la guerra de Vietnam. Gran decepción ver que básicamente es una exposición de fotografías, con algún objeto y avión o tanque en el jardín de entrada.

Museo imprenscindible decían. Yo me encontré con un Museo sin audioguía en español y muchas fotografías de supervivientes malparados y gente ytotalmente deformada como consecuencia de la Guerra de Vietnam. Ver a la gente fotografiando este tipo de escenas me produjo mucho malestar. El sitio reconendable.
It’s a very interesting issue but the museum is really boring There is a lot of pictures ,movie and few vehicles from the war but it doesn’t show the war as we expect
Bảo tàng bao nhiêu năm vẫn vậy, không bổ sung nâng cấp thêm vật tài lực, cứ bao nhiêu đó làm đi làm lại.

This is not a good place to get your first exposure to the history of the war. While there is a great deal to be learned from the exhibits, there is also a much more complicated story that is ignored. The story here is told as if the current government was the only option for Vietnam. In that way, this has a heavy dose of propaganda along with a lot of truth about the suffering of civilians.
Just a big collection of pictures. It should be shown in much more interesting and creative way. It’s better to watch “Vietnam war” series on Netflix if you want to know more.
アメリカ軍が残してしまった戦争の跡。 かなり赤裸々に。。。 枯葉剤がその時だけでなく、後々まで人々に与えてしまった影響。

The heading for this tour reads "HCMC City & Cu Chi Tunnels Full Day or Half Day Trip". Notice it says city AND tunnels for full day OR half day. I purchased the half day option where the details clearly describe what's included: Entry/Admission - Central Post Office; Entry/Admission - War Remnants Museum; Entry/Admission - Saigon Notre Dame Cathedral; Entry/Admission - Cu Chi Tunnels. Lunch is also listed as an inclusion on my booking details. The tour began okay with a friendly local guide. We visited the listed HCMC sites as advertised. Then, shortly before lunch we were told our tour was over. Despite my booking confirmation that listed lunch and the tunnels as included and despite a phone conversation with the tour company, my family was asked to get off the bus. They did at least return us to our hotel. For $100 USD for a family of 4, I believe I paid too much for this tour of the city attractions only. It definitely was not worth the money, and the description of our tour was misleading at best if not outright dishonest. Be very careful to make sure you know exactly what you are booking before booking this tour.

Manque de contexte, il est nécessaire de connaître la guerre du Vietnam pour que la visite soit intéressante. Images choquantes et minutieusement choisies, l'objectif semble être de montrer les atrocités de la guerre. On est ressort mal à l'aise et sans information.

Was my 2nd visit to the War museum and it is good to visit for an hour or two and appreciate the pictures and history.  However my major complaint is the amount of scam taxis who sit directly outside the exit and they will rip you off. After catching one in the rain back to my hotel i noticed the meter going full speed and this was now costing 5 times the amount of going there by taxi. I told the driver to stop, gave him half and told him to F off. Only take a Vinasun and never take a cab that has a meter but no driver ID card. These taxis must work with corrupt museum people so the sooner the government stops this directly outside the museum the better.  This is why u gave it a 2 Star.
Usual hodge-podge mishmash of collected junk, in a filthy environment, with little to no coherence to the experience. Interesting for about 20 minutes.

Some interesting pieces of US military equipment, with thoughtful explanations. The real treat comes inside where we “discover” the real facts of the war. The people of the South were forced to participate against their will in a capitalist society, vexatiously denying the true course of the Vietnamese people. Ok, if you don’t take the propaganda too seriously, it’s almost funny. Having also been in Hanoi recently, and visiting their war sites, you are immediately slapped in the face here in Saigon with rhetoric not found in the North.  One can only assume that this is punishment for those living in the south, for failing to be “good communists”. So, pls, do not presume that this place, and it’s views, reflects the opinions and attitudes of the Vietnamese people. They all seem to have collectively forgotten most of the whole affair. Something that us Westerners have been loathe to follow their example.

I didn’t enjoy this museum. The high ratings on here are from those who say it’s important to see. I’ve been to Auschwitz, remnants of war should be preserved and witnessed. But here you don’t have a recount of the war, it’s a very uncomfortable 4 rooms of name calling, finger pointing and shock imagery. The planes out the front and the display of guns were interesting.

This used to be called the American war crimes museum ... no mention of the viet Cong war crimes of course But what it does is show how awful war is and was .There is also a lot of old war junk alongside torture paraphernalia.., and graphic material

There's a lot of interesting information to read about. But some rooms we didn't let the kids go in since the pictures were very graphic and disturbing. If you have younger kids it probably would be too boring with all the reading or too graphic for them.
Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam
This is a tourist trap and the pictures are very sad. I would skip this if at all possible. There is a static display of some of USA’s planes and equipment.

I didn't expect it to be an even handed view on the war but it was much worse than I could have foreseen. Riddled with easily verified factual errors I don't think it meets the standard to be called a museum. It had to the chance to be a wonderful museum but falls more in the category of self serving propaganda. A visually interesting place to visit but if you are interested in learning there are many first hand accounts to give you accurate first hand accounts of the war.

I’ve visited this museum before maybe 12 years ago but l was affected much more this time.  It tells one sided view of the War with USA as you would expect. It’s basec on photo journalistic accounts.  The pictures are hard hitting and not for the faint hearted.  However l found it crass l found it disrespectful to the victims to the dead on both sides Tours groups herding around like cattle taking pictures of absolutely everything no time to contemplate or show respect. I found that sickening  The floor which portrayed victims of agent orange if you believe the propaganda was too much l know not what photographing these poor wretched people achieved. It was however heartbreaking- whatever the cause.  I doubt l will ever be back in HCMC twice in a lifetime is enough for me but if l do l won’t visit this museum again

Very graphic photo essays re American-inflicted casualties and torturing supported by reports and photos of anti-Vietnam War protests in Western capitals at the time. Certainly gripped the crowds. I visited Cu Chi tunnels yesterday with a great Onetrip guide who was able to give a balanced account of the war and its background and showed the vicious maiming/killing Spanish Inquisition-style man-traps used by the Viet Cong in the tunnels and jungle. Both sides used terrible tactics. I'd recommend reading up on the war before visiting either site so you have some perspective beforehand and can cut through the War Museum propaganda.

It was to much propaganda and the museum only tells one side of the story. There was some old US helicopters, tanks etc. outside but in total I felt it was a little wast of time. Old US war stuff like planes, chopters, tanks etc. is possible to view many places and the story inside the museum was told in the eyes of the Hanoi regime.
it ia very cheap， need time to see every picture and read the words； if you want to know the War more，then it is a good place to visit.

Layout poor, not signposted to start at the top floor and then work your way down to ground floor. Story of what happened, lead up to how war started and the war Itself could be organised and told better.

If you’re into propaganda you’ll love this ‘museum’. Essentially all the photos inside are of ‘patriots’ that have been killed by ‘evil’ America.  With themes as in my photo it’s hard to believe this museum is subjective. Some very gruesome photos that are definitely not suited for children.  The outside area is pretty good though with lots of planes, tanks, etc. as well as a few display of what the French had brought to Vietnam.
Too much fake news in this place (like photos of Saigon population welcoming with smiles the Viet Cong arrival in the city). But the photos of victims of agent orange and napalm are really moving.

Trop à charge pour être vraiment intéressant. Une succession de photos, assez horribles pour la plupart, des engins exposés à l’extérieur. Dans une guerre, les monstruosités sont en général bien réparties, mais les traces de ce conflit étant assez vives au Vietnam (en particulier du côté de Hué qui garde les séquelles des bombardements chimiques) on ne peut pas attendre d'objectivité de ce musée.

Visited here as part of a Vietnam full country tour and unfortunately this is not a museum but a propaganda site. It is unquestionably the most biased and subjective 'museum' I have ever been to. There is a famous saying that 'winners write history' and this is a perfect example. What makes it worse is that reading the reviews most people seem to have been taken in.

A quick search of any history page indicates 1954 - 1975 as the duration of the Indochina War. Yet this propaganda site only focuses on the years of the American involvement and the inhuman use of chemical weapons.  The one-sided presentation omits or skews information. The Tet Offensive is not mentioned where the VC massacred Vietnamese people, in "a large-scale purge of anyone friendly to American forces".  The Girl in the Picture, Kim Phuc, is displayed as she runs screaming from the napalm burning her back. Yet, she later defected and became a Canadian citizen, and even spoke in the U.S. to forgive those involved in the air strike.  Located outside of the main building, one finds a guillotine and tiger cages which are not included in the principal display likely because they would detract from the late 1960s antiwar messages.  So, interesting but totally unbalanced.

A lot of terriable photos show in the second floor, please make sure you can accept the bloody scene. And I find the original awarded photo of bansky artwork.  We walking to Ben Thanh market after the war musueum. It was closed at lunar new year, we got bag snatching in the market outside. It is very terriable. My wife drag at the street for one meter and bleeding in arm and leg. Be careful everyone, no local people help us in Ben Thanh Markert. only got some help from tourists, I regarded it happen everyday.
放了很多照片只是想要說明美國對他們的傷害：死了多少人、造成多少殘疾。但看完整個博物館，我還是沒辦法了解整個越戰的歷史。

Surprisingly and unapologetically biased against the South Vietnamese / American cause. I’m surprised I didn’t get stabbed while I was there because at face value the museum makes Americans out to be evil imperialist “aggressors” that just wanted to kill every Vietnamese man, woman and child. With two highly respected Vietnam vets in my family I should have skipped this.

Das Kriegsopfermuseum ist für einen deutschen Besucher eher eine gewöhnungsbedürftige Erfahrung.  Man betritt das Museum im Erdgeschoss in dem sich ein Foyer mit Shops und dem Treppenhaus befindet. Über dieses Treppenhaus kommt man zu den Räumen, denen einzelne Themenbereiche zugeordnet sind. Das Haus ist nicht beschildert und wir wussten eine Weile nicht recht wohin wir gehen sollen.  Die einzelnen Räume bieten eine große Ansammlung von Bildern, die die Grausamkeiten und Spätfolgen des Krieges zeigen. Es findet sich auch ein in Formaldehyd eingelegter Säugling, der vom Kontakt mit Agent Orange gezeichnet ist.  Ansonsten finden sich Vitrinen mit verschiedenen Minen und anderen erbeuteten Waffen.  Ich halte das Museum für eine Zumutung und keinen Besuch wert. An dieser Einschätzung ändert sich auch dann nichts, wenn man berücksichtigt, dass in Südostasien eine ganz andere Kultur der musealen Kuration gelebt wird. Ich habe bestimmt keine angemessenen Infotafeln erwartet und auch keine geordnete Struktur in der Ausstellung. Über diese Dinge könnte man allesamt hinwegsehen.  Ich werde nur einen Eindruck nicht los: Ob es nun um Toul Sleng in Kambodscha, das Gefängnis auf Phu Quoc oder eben auch das Kriegsopfermuseum geht, so scheint es ein verbindendes Element zu geben. Diese Orte dienen dazu Backpackerinnen in weiten Hosen das Gruseln zu lehren. Das ist im Grunde vergleichbar mit dem Besuch mittelalterlicher Folterkammern, die ja offenbar auch bei vielen Besuchern einen wohligen Schauer erzeugen.  Nachdem es sich aber hier um Ereignisse der jüngeren Vergangenheit handelt, finde ich diese lüsterne Geisterbahnmentalität abstoßend. Meinem Dafürhalten nach liegt darin aber der Hauptzweck dieses Museums.

Gloriously biased to the point of barely being worth the visit... the most honest exhibition was the dedication to international war correspondents and their work. That’s probably the only part worth the ticket value - there are some stunning photos in there. The rest has glaring omissions to history - You’d get better history and facts out of a Wikipedia article. In fact, you should probably brush up on actual history before coming here so you can temper the narrative a bit. War is full of atrocities on both sides, but here you won’t get a full picture.

This is a very good museum and should be on everybody's list if they are visiting Saigon. The museum clearly demonstrates that war is not a pleasant thing and we should not glorify it in any way. The museum rightly points out the atrocities that the Americans committed but is in no way even handed.  There is no mention of the fact that it was a civil war and it lays all of the blame at the feet of the United States. The currators point out the massacres that the American forces committed but makes no mention of those that were committed by the North.  Finally, there was an aircraft painted in US livery in the courtyard that never actually flew in the US air Force.  So as a museum that clearly does not glorify war and shows all of its horrors and why we as humans should not participate, the exhibitions succeed. If however you are looking to see a well-rounded perspective on the American war you will be severely disappointed.

This museum is interesting, it has a lot of historical pictures & artifacts, but it is definitely one-sided. I know. I understand why, but as an American, I feel you should not go here if you’re not prepared for huge amounts of anti-American propaganda. If you can let it roll off your back, fine. But if you are thin-skinned, avoid. Every war atrocity is magnified & blamed on the “American Aggressors”.

Basically you will just find a bunch of rooms with photos and totally biased subtitles. For a museum with this notoriety, was expecting some movies and more in depth information than what I came across.

This was a must for me, but it turned out to be a cruel example on the winner writing the history. But besides being a place for blaming the americans for being imperialistic suppressors, it is also an exibition that shows the horrors of war in general and remind us of avoiding armed conflicts.

Nuestra intención era no visitar este museo, pero al final lo hicimos. Confirmamos nuestra impresión y es una pena. La guerra de Vietnam, presentada exclusivamente como una agresión americana y sin mención alguna como guerra civil entre el Norte y el Sur, fue algo demasiado sangriento y lamentable como para hacer un comentario que pudiera interpretarse como una banalización de lo ocurrido. Es una guerra que no debió existir e impedir la extensión del comunismo no justifica tantas muertes; pero la presentación propagandística que se hace en el museo, solo con la visión de una de las partes, debería ser corregida. Ganaría en credibilidad. Y sería la mejor forma de que concienciar para que no vuelvan a cometerse los mismos errores.

The story of he war is a horrible, no doubt. Still, I would have wished for more background information about the vietnamese side. The museum is mostly about war crimes committed by the Americans, which should be common knowledge outside the US but I was hoping for more information about how the defending troops organized their war and finally succeeded. The tone of description is always coloured by propaganda. All in all necessary to watch if you're American but nothing new to learn for the remainder

Very interesting to see, but extremely one sided government propaganda that is largely anti-US. If you are an American Citizen, especially one that served in the military, you will be offended. The truth is WAR IS HELL and both sides committed atrocities, however, this is only a one-sided view.
we went and saw but feel it was soo overcoming and brought home reality , would not go again, compared to aussie museums needs alot more to be put into it

I can't say I've ever been disappointed at a museum until I went to this one. It's a collections of photographs and propaganda about how the US aggressors are to blame for the casualties and suffering in Vietnam. There was very little information about the war. I learned more from Wiki than in the museum.
לא מראה בצורה אוביקטיבית את כל מהלכי המלחמה.לא נותן מספיק רקע והסבר לתקופה שקדמה למלחמה.מראה יותר את הצד הויאטנמי ולא את כל מהלכי המלחמה

Waste of money ($US 5.00) Derelict pieces. All the guns on display (in an open air shelter) did not relate to any of the printed information. Very little to see and very little information. Needs a lot of work to be any value to visitors. Do not recommend unless improvements made.
Không có nội dung

Was looking forward to this, since I guessed by 2016 the communist falsehoods would have been assigned to dustbin and the Museum could focus on history. So not the case, you be hard to find any actual references to the fact that this was a civil war with Vietnamese killing each other! You won't find a single reference to the wrongs that were committed against the South post the war ending in 75. Of course it covers the mistakes the US made, which it should, but lets at least try and cover the mistakes of communism in the same breath shall we? No, clearly not.
Sólo me gustó los vehículos de la calle. Dentro sólo ves fotografías y textos. Prefiero ver un libro o un documental
we about to visit Vn and with one day left, we decide to come to this museum. not the best place to go, like i said, if you don't have to visit, go somewhere else....

Sembra chissa' cosa ci sia da vedere! Posso dirvi che tanta gente che trovate li, sive' fermata per il bar e le toilette pubbliche. Naturalmente dopo che ci era andata perche' luogo da visitare! E li vicino ci sarebbe la strada piena di ristoranti con cortili all' aperto , tipici. Tre volte avanti e indietro per poi andare altrove!
Если честно, музей разочаровал. Техники очень мало, а посмотреть на фото и почитать историю войны можно и в интернете. Билет дешёвый, но более 5 минут там делать нечего.
A one sided potray of atrocities. One other hand it does show the communist council doing humanitarian relief.

No es muy divertido mi mujer se impresiono , que bueno que este lindo pueblo humilde y cordial se haya recuperado , aunque los taxistas como en todos lados son una plaga que deberia erradicarse jejejej es un broma pero siempre se aprovechan del turista. y te daban ganas de matar a todos. las fotos de las barbaridades que sucedieron ahi son espeluznantes. como vivian enterrados en el bosque haciendo trampas con fierros y palos . me gusto mas el museo de la guerra en korea menos sangre mas maquinas.

As an American from the Vietnam War era (not a veteran) I wanted to see this museum but was disjointed because it was very limited in what it had. Some posters, a little info on some of the people in relation to war but nothing that really gives insight to how terrible this was was. It had a small section on the conditions of a political prison..very limited and then some war tanks, plane, helicopter, mounted guns and ammunition. Very little to see and little to give anybody the scope of this war...it can be seen in 15 uneventful min. This could have offered a better showing of a terrible long war for The Vietnamese people.

Zeigt sehr klar und deutlich die übertriebene brutalität der Amerikaner, jedoch fehlt komplett der historische bezug. Im ganzen Museum wird kein Wort über die Sovjetunion/oder den Kalten Krieg verloren...

May well be true facts there as war is terrible but odd that the blame by the current government who runs the museum doesn’t direct it to the Leaders/ soldiers of the south Vietnamese nation that was independent post French colonialism instead of American bashing !! Odd also American funding / investment is now currently used to uplift Vietnamese!! What seemed to have happened is sad but it’s one sided , it doesn’t talk or show what the North Vietnamese of that time did to South Vietnamese citizens or to Americans......  Oddly heard very few American accents there though plenty of Aussies and French !!

Ik keek er naar uit om naar het museum te gaan en was dan ook erg benieuwd. Het museum heeft prachtige foto's en is echt erg interessant. Echter mis ik de interactie. Het bleef veelal bij foto's. Daarnaast (tja een beetje zeurderig) heeft het museum veel klimaatverschillen waardoor je steeds van heet naar super koud stapt. Ik had er persoonlijk last van.

There is only helicopter, tank, airplane, and few machine guns. A lot if history with poor pictures. Interesting to read, but you can do it on your phone too. With 40k dong entrance fee it's really not worth it.

Came to the museum expecting something more and a little more comfortable to be walking in. Quite humid so it was difficult to stop and take in everything. What was most disappointing was that a gentleman who had lost both his arms, from the elbow down, approached me and asked where I was from. I told him from Australia, so he told me his story and then proceed to push me into buying two books from him. I kept saying no, but he kept pushing. In the end I bought them. No sooner I purchased the books, one of the workers approached him and gave him another two books to sell. I found this to be in poor taste, and made me question if the gentleman truly lost his arms during the war, but more that he was being used to push and sell books. I do wonder how much he gets from the sale.

Das Museum ist methodisch unzweckmäßig aufgebaut, eine historische Einordnung geschieht beispielsweise erst im letzten Drittel der Ausstellung (3.OG). Auch sind die Bilder zwar mit Beschreibungen versehen, diese sind allerdings meist lediglich beschreibend, eine sinnvolle bzw. sinnstiftende Kommentierung sucht man vergebens. In den seltenen Fällen, in denen eine Kommentierung stattfindet, ist sie unsachlich und polemisch (ein amerikanisches M1A1 Gewehr wird bspw. als Waffe beschrieben, die vor allem zur Bekämpfung von Demonstration und bei Verhören eingesetzt wurde). Das führt zum eigentlichen, wenn auch nicht überraschenden Manko der Ausstellung: sie ist unfassbar einseitig und undifferenziert. Nordvietnam gut, USA böse. Eine solche Darstellung instrumentalisiert die Opfer des Krieges zu Propagandazwecken und wird ihnen nicht gerecht. Insgesamt ist das Museum eine große Propagandashow, die sehr schnell sehr ermüdend wirkt, vor allem weil die Bildunterschriften so nichtssagend sind und jedes weitere Interesse zunichte machen.  Eine Ausnahme stellt die Fotografieausstellung im 3.OG dar. Diese ist merklich von einem anderen Kurator zusammengestellt und wurde bereits international ausgestellt. Diese dokumentiert die Arbeit und Schicksale der Kriegsfotografen und bietet ergreifende Eindrücke des Kriegsgeschehens.  Der Informationsgehalt ist mittel bis gering. Wenn man sich nur ein wenig über das Thema informiert hat (seien es nur TV Dokus und Wikipedia) erfährt man im Museum kaum neues.  Sehr schade, da das Thema sehr viel Potential bietet, über die grundsätzlichen Gräuel des Krieges zu reflektieren und eventuell Schlüsse daraus zu ziehen. Diese Chance wird hier leider nicht genutzt.
Absolument pas instructif et anti-americains. Que des photos morbides. Ça ne vaut pas plus le coup que cela.
hiện vật giúp hồi tưởng quá khứ,nên có thuyết minh dạng nghe. ít tranh ,ảnh lại thêm các hoạt động để giúp gợi lại kí ức

When I entered I thought the museum had potential with all the American aircraft outside, but after the tour I was truly dissapointed. Yes, I know the Americans did horrible things and there is no excuse for all they have done, but when you leave the museum, you don't have a feeling that it was a civil war, but an American occupation of Vietnam. There is no mention of the thousands of the Vietnamese soliders that fought alongside Americans. What I found strange is also that there is little mention of the north Vietnamese army and the fight they fought. In most of such conflicts, the museums depict winners as gods themselves and have a lot of exibits that show their proud liberators and their battles. Not here. The whole museum is about Americans and their war which they fought with the Vietnamese. Mostly there are photos and letters and againg horrible photos about the attrocities the Americans did, a whole floor on agent orange and simmilar horrible horrible things. There is no excuse about what they did, but the history is not always so black and white.
It was okay. Lots of old photos and captions. Not enough actual exhibits. Many disturbing images, not suitable for kids.

I can't say that this was an enjoyable experience. It was a stark reminder of how futile the Vietnam war actually was and what a terrible waste of life it was. It is interesting to read about the war from the persective of the Vietnamese rather than the Western persective. On est loin de ces nouveau musées 'wow' comme le musée Solidarnosc (à Gdansk, en pologne). L'information est présentée ici de manière très linéaire, et toujours sous sa forme la plus scolaire (salle de photos, salle des armes, salle des posters ...) Le musée n'est pas interactif du tout et ne vous surprend a peu près jamais.  Les textes accompagnatifs sont parfois douteux. Je veux dire, c'est bien que le gouvernement vietnamien donne son avis sur ce conflit et le comportement américain (on a tellement vu cette guerre sous le prisme US), mais on est parfois gèné par le manque de neutralité grossier dans les commentaires. Plusieurs exemples me viennent à l’esprit, mais celui des mines antipersonnel est assez représentatif. On vous explique que les américains ont miné le territoire, et qu'aujourd'hui encore des gens peuvent marcher dessus _ avec moultes photos émouvantes et commentaires. Sauf que la quasi totalité des mines encore disséminées au VN ont été posées par ... les viet minh, qui ne les ont pas répertoriées ! Mais pas un mot là dessus, ces mines là sont magiques et ne font pas de victimes même si bien plus nombreuses.  C'est un endroit visitable si vous avez quatre jours entiers ou plus à HCMC. Si moins de temps, je vous conseille de zapper. Au delà des points soulevés et même si la guerre du vietnam vous intéresse, les ressources présentées n'ont rien d'immanquable. J'aime bien me faire un bon musée, mais celui-là n'en faisait définitivement pas partie.

I can't say I've ever been disappointed at a museum until I went to this one. It's a collections of photographs and propaganda about how the US aggressors are to blame for the casualties and suffering in Vietnam. There was very little information about the war. I learned more from Wiki than in the museum.

I felt uncomfortable here, not because of the 'horrors of war' but moreso at how in the age of information a communist government still thinks it can so blatantly spread lies and defame real democracy.

Still, that's not why it gets one star—after all it's to be expected in a red country. I did laugh out loud a few times when the Southern Vietnamese government was referred to as the 'puppet government' or the 'regime', so much so that I could have enjoyed the walk through.

What really detracted from it however, was that it was really just a mediocre photo gallery with a few weapons displays, mostly American small arms with a few vehicles on display. You'll get higher resolution imagery and less biased information by reading the Wikipedia article on the Vietnam war, and that won't set you back 40k dong a head.

What would've made this a five star experience? A timeline, an explanation of the rationale behind the driving force of communism in Vietnam, an explanation of how the NVA and the VC both fought and even some overlays and narratives of actual battles. More VC and NVA equipment, and the role the Chinese and Soviets played in the war would also be a nice touch. Until then, don't waste your time (not an exaggeration).

My advice: start charging more money-- admission is $1.50 for 2 people right now, and the poor design of the museum is evident. Upgrade your facility, hire a real professor of history to create a self guided experience for visitors, and get an audio tour.

Là một người con Sài Gòn từ nhỏ đến lớn, lần đầu tiên tôi dắt con đi tham quan nơi đây. Trải nghiệm có những điểm cộng rất tốt, nhưng cũng đi kèm với những điều cực kỳ tệ hại, thiếu chuyên nghiệp và không lịch sự.

Một trong những điểm cộng là sự tiện lợi trong việc mua vé. Bảo tàng có máy bán vé tự động cho phép thanh toán bằng thẻ hoặc Apple Pay rất nhanh chóng. Bên cạnh đó, không gian bên trong tòa nhà có máy lạnh rất mát mẻ, tạo cảm giác thoải mái cho khách tham quan.

Tuy nhiên, có những điểm trừ mà ban quản lý cần cải thiện. Đầu tiên là việc tổ chức giữ xe và bán vé chưa thực sự thuận tiện. Du khách phải gửi xe xong, rồi đi vòng ra ngoài cổng Võ Văn Tần để mua vé, khá bất tiện. Bảo tàng nên xem xét đặt một máy bán vé tự động ngay tại bãi xe để tiết kiệm thời gian cho khách.

Điều khiến tôi thất vọng nhất là thái độ của một số bảo vệ. Có thể nói là cực kỳ khó chịu và thiếu văn minh. Vì tôi có hút thuốc nên chủ động đi ra khu vực cổng giữ xe để không làm ảnh hưởng đến mọi người. Nhưng khi quay trở vào, hai nhân viên bảo vệ đã chặn lại, không cho vào dù tôi đã đưa vé. Họ nói chuyện rất gay gắt, cứ như thể tôi đang cố gắng gian lận tiền vé. Trên vé không hề có dòng chữ "Vé chỉ có giá trị sử dụng 1 lần", nhưng các bảo vệ vẫn liên tục đuổi ra với thái độ nghi ngờ.

Thật sự, 40.000 đồng không phải là số tiền quá lớn để phải gian lận. Nhưng cách hành xử của họ đã làm cho trải nghiệm của tôi trở nên không thoải mái. Tôi không muốn miệt thị nghề nghiệp của ai, nhưng với thái độ như vậy thì thật sự không đủ tư cách để làm bảo vệ cho một bảo tàng lớn tại TP.HCM. Nếu là một du khách nước ngoài, liệu họ có dám đuổi đi một cách vô cớ như thế không, hay chỉ vì thấy là người Việt nên mặc định nghĩ ai cũng lươn lẹo như họ?

Thiết nghĩ, ban quản lý bảo tàng nên giáo dục lại cách ứng xử và đối thoại của nhân viên bảo vệ đối với khách tham quan. Cần xây dựng một không gian văn minh, lịch sự để khách hàng có những trải nghiệm thật sự thoải mái khi đến thăm một di tích lịch sử quan trọng của thành phố.

I found the war museum to be deeply disappointing. Rather than offering an immersive or educational experience, it felt more like wandering through a photo gallery. The presentation relied heavily on endless photographs with brief captions, supported by outdated autocue-style machines with weak audio that were difficult to hear. Any attempt to read or listen was made frustrating by the constant flow of people pushing past or blocking the displays.  There are far more effective and engaging ways to present such important material, yet here it was delivered in the most uninspired manner. In my view, it was neither a museum in the true sense of the word nor a place that evoked reflection—it was simply a gallery of pictures with little depth. Compared to the War Museum in Hanoi, which is both powerful and profoundly moving, this one felt like a complete waste of time and money.
disappointing. I went with another to Group outside Trip.com and it was fascinating in innovating in the detail in the history was exceptional. Avoid.

The whole 3 story building was very hot and humid. The AC was not switched on or may it is always this way. Many old people above 70 were dehydrated and just could not walk. And they were sitting more than they were watching the museum.  This is extremely bad on the part of museum authorities. It is not that allow everyone for free. They take a entry ticket of 1.5 USD per head. If you are above 60 and if you cannot tolerate hot and humid conditions for 1 to 2 hours, do not visit this place

This whole museum need to be modernized with technologies and more interesting displays.

DON'T TAKE ANY CHILDREN TO SEE THIS!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!! Fortunately I am not a citizen of "that country" whose brutalities are evoked in the Museum. It documents, with hundreds of photographs, the ATROCITIES committed against innocent civilians - old persons, children, women. Both by soldiers and by chemical products such as napalm and "agent orange". Many photos can't simply be looked at without tears in the eyes.

I have to be honest I was a good bit disappointed with my time at this war museum. I left it in a bad mood, due to a few factors.  I know the Americans did absolutely horrific things in Vietnam, from dropping napalm, general torture, and dividing the country into two......but it doesn't necessarily mean I want an in depth account of each and every bad thing the yanks did........I don't really have to be confronted with gruesome photo, picture and display to get it....... They even had a long list of all the tortures....and a rolling movie to accompany..... really, whats the point of this? And I'd ask who was doing a lot of this torture? (South) Vietnamese onto other Vietnamese, something lost in all of the details.  War is war and bad things happen, but in this museum it seems its only the Americans (and the French) who were the naughty boys. History isn't like that, its not always so black and white. I need more nuance than that. Why was America there, what was the South Vietnamese state, what happened after the war, was there any real victors, etc etc?  Plus more of a human side, how the war affected people on a more personal level, from soldier (both sides), to villager, to townie. Yes there were photos with information (in English which was good, for me at least), but it still was all a bit dry really and hard to get past the pain and conflict and understand on a human level....  The real thing for me is that one should learn from History, the good and the bad. Can we really say that Vietnam, or the USA for that matter, has? It seems to me that American style capitalism is quite popular in this country. How did that happen? (cough!). Is Vietnam a bastion of freedom and liberty? For sure its a place to lead a comfortable life, but what about personal liberty of freedom and thought? Yeah all that and more.........the present and the past.  Or how about another deformed kid to look at?

DO NOT PURCHASE FROM THIS TOUR OPERATOR!!!!  I purchased a tour for 2 destinations (War Remnants & Cu Chi Tunnels). Twelve hours before the start of the tour (which was less than 24 hours notice which cancelled out my option to get a refund) I was notified that the tour I paid for would not be provided. They stated only half of the tour would be given. They provided an address to meet for the other part of the tour, I went to this address and it was only a partial address missing the building letter (example A B C D E F) so I was not able to locate this tour operator. I believe this is a scam operation. I have contacted Viator multiple times and was told they would provide a refund but a later message said they would not be refunding my money and it was “a final decision”. They advertised a service they did not provide. DO NOT PURCHASE FROM THIS TOUR OPERATOR

Entre la salle dédiée aux "vérités historiques" ou la salle appelée maladroitement "children education chamber" on n'apprend qu'une seule chose dans ce pseudo musée: c'est toujours le vainqueur qui écrit l'histoire. Je recommande aux visiteurs de s'informer sur le véritable déroulement des événements avant de visiter ce lieu. Ainsi les salles d'exposition montreront les unes après les autres comment un régime totalitaire comme celui de vietnam n'hésite pas a déformer l'histoire afin d'endoctriner son peuple. C'est le seul intérêt de ce "musee" à mon sens. Un exemple choquant de propagande d'Etat.

I found the booking form doesn't work very well. I thought that I had booked a two-way pick up - I put the details of my arrival and my departure on the form. (By the way the form wouldn't save initially because I'd not completed the boxes about boats and train times which didn't apply. Nor was it clear what to put in the boxes under these circumstances). I've just had on the phone the guy from War Remnants Museum, which is the organisation running the airport pick-up, telling me I don't have a pick-up tomorrow ('what can you expect? You only paid $25' was his remark to me - great customer relations). He wouldn't take a booking for tomorrow ('ask your hotel to make a booking for you'). When I arrived at the airport, I couldn't see the representative. I had to ask another company representative to help me phone to locate the pick-up which we eventually did. 'I was told to stand at X - didn't you get the message?'. No. He also wasn't holding up the sign to identified him! This was the first time I've used Tripadvisor to make such a booking. I doubt if I'll be doing so again in a hurry.

I want to give this museum 1 star because of their staffs (specifically some coffee shop's staffs) lacking morality, having disgusting jokes and comments towards customers. One of the employees, who happens to be the museum director's niece, even filming foreigners, a baby without them knowing and their consent. She yet posted the videos on her social media with a thirsty caption and made fun of an Indian man. This violates privacy rights, but she is not capable of understanding and acknowledging it. Her older co-worker also joined her, and her manager said nothing about that. It just seems like the situation is not serious and important enough for them to care. This is absolutely a toxic environment for visitors since the manager could not control the behavior of her employees. Fortunately, I intend not to return. I express my anger here to alert everyone about this privacy violation in such a solemn place. I hope they educate themselves and none of you, also your loved ones would be her victims in the future.

As inaccurate a portrayal as could be! First of all, the pictures related to "agent orange" could not possibly be related to the chemical, as it does not cause the problems portrayed. Secondly, the Vietnam war raged for years with the independent south requesting help from multiple nations, yet the museum refers to the war as "the war of American aggression". We traveled there with some veterans who were appalled at the way the Vietnamese turned the tables, making their vicious and cruel soldiers look like the benevolent ones. There was no mention whatsoever of the thousands of Vietnamese which the US government gave asylum to while over hundreds of thousands of the Vietnamese people left behind were tortured and slaughtered literally in front of their own families when the US pulled out. Do not waste your time on thins propaganda!

Very one sided, anti-American... I have no problem with a “museum” telling all sides of a story, but this place was very one sided... One of the exhibits was was titled “America’s war of aggression” and the theme of the entire place was similar. While the exhibits were powerful and there was some educational value, I did not care for the propaganda aspects.

Hier wird einfach nur versucht, mit der Geschichte Geld zu verdienen. Vorher dachte ich, ich werde das Museum verlassen und betrübt sein von der Geschichte. Ich war allerdings wegen zwei anderen Dingen betrübt.  Einmal von den Touristen, die sich vor den Panzern, Waffen und anderen Darstellungen posieren und ablichten lassen. Die überhaupt kein Gefühl und Feingefühl für das besitzen, was damals passiert ist und was hier versucht wird darzustellen. Es ist ein Ort, der Qualen, Elend und Tod veranschaulicht und manche haben nichts besseres zu tun, als fröhliche Selfies zu machen und über Fotos toter, verwundener Menschen zu lachen. Das allein versaut einem alles und macht einen sprachlos, wie wenig Respekt manche Menschen besitzen. Den Wärtern ist das hier völlig egal - es sollte ein absolutes Fotografieverbot hier geben.  Punkt zwei war einmal der Raum, der nach einer Fotoausstellung Behinderter Menschen folgte. Hier wurden zunächst schaurige Bilder der Folgen der Agent Orange Toxic gezeigt. Danach ging es in einen Raum, eher ein Souvenirshop, in welcher Bilder behinderter Kinder aushängen. Hier sitzt ein schwerstbehinderter Mann in einem Rollstuhl und hat keine Funktion, außer (so schlimm es sich anhört, ich kann aber keine andere Erklärung finden) die Touristen anregen, Souvenirs zu kaufen.  Es ist schlimm, wie die Behinderung der Menschen für die Geldkasse ausgenutzt wird!!!! Absolut ekelhaft.  Auch der ausgestattete Souvenirshop mit Propaganda Posters und mehr ist absolut fehl am Platz. Genauso vergleichbar mit einem Souvenirshop bei einem KZ mit Gasmasken. Absolut unvorstellbar.  Alles in allem bin ich wahnsinnig enttäuscht, kann mich mit dem Internet mehr fortbilden (hier gab es nur viel Text und Bilder, keine Audio Guides).  Hier findet nur eins statt: mit der Geschichte Geld machen und nicht aus der Geschichte lernen.

I have to give one star to balance all the nonsense reviews. Yes pictures here are horrifying but this place does not give you any info about the actual war and gives only one sided propaganda. The best part about this museum is the outside. Not a single mention of viet cong and the exposition is not even in chronological order.
